We interpret the cocoon of fainter emission embedding the radio galaxy as the result of the back-flow from both jets.

gmw_00012250.name: SPOG157
gmw_00012250.overview: SPOG157 is a post-starburst galaxy that appears to be an edge-on dusty disk galaxy [arXiv:2207.00607]. It hosts an active galactic nucleus characterized by high obscuration and high luminosity, with a very hard X-ray spectrum [arXiv:2207.00607].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SPOG157 as an exceptional hard X-ray post-starburst galaxy requiring high obscuration, high luminosity, and perhaps Compton-thick absorption rather than thermal emission.

quotes.quote: Two SPOGs, SPOG157 and to a lesser degree SPOG186, show a different tendency. SPOG157 requires n_H 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 , particularly to explain its very hard spectrum. While the model with the highest fraction of mock spectra in agreement with the observed constraints has the parameter pair of (L_X, n_H) = (3 10^ 43 erg s^ 1 , 6 10^ 23 cm^ 2 ) , the six models in Figure ResultsPL for SPOG157 with the darkest blue color have a similar fraction of mock spectra in agreement with the observations. They extend from (L_X, n_H) = (3 10^ 42 erg s^ 1 , 1 10^ 23 cm^ 2 ) to (L_X, n_H) = (6 10^ 43 erg s^ 1 , 1 10^ 24 cm^ 2 ) , indicating that SPOG157 is more luminous and more obscured than most of the sample.

Interestingly, both SPOG157 and SPOG186 appear to be edge-on dusty disk galaxies, suggesting that at least some part of their obscuration may be on the galactic scale rather than purely at the nuclear scale.

gmw_00012251.name: SPOG186
gmw_00012251.overview: SPOG186 is a post-starburst galaxy [arXiv:2207.00607] that appears as an edge-on dusty disk galaxy [arXiv:2207.00607]. It hosts a marginally detected active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2207.00607] that is highly obscured, with the potential for Compton-thick obscuration [arXiv:2207.00607].

sources.summary: The paper treats SPOG186 as a marginally detected but possibly highly obscured post-starburst AGN, with weaker constraints than SPOG157 and possible Compton-thick absorption.

quotes.quote: Two SPOGs, SPOG157 and to a lesser degree SPOG186, show a different tendency. SPOG157 requires n_H 10^ 23 cm ^ 2 , particularly to explain its very hard spectrum. While the model with the highest fraction of mock spectra in agreement with the observed constraints has the parameter pair of (L_X, n_H) = (3 10^ 43 erg s^ 1 , 6 10^ 23 cm^ 2 ) , the six models in Figure ResultsPL for SPOG157 with the darkest blue color have a similar fraction of mock spectra in agreement with the observations. They extend from (L_X, n_H) = (3 10^ 42 erg s^ 1 , 1 10^ 23 cm^ 2 ) to (L_X, n_H) = (6 10^ 43 erg s^ 1 , 1 10^ 24 cm^ 2 ) , indicating that SPOG157 is more luminous and more obscured than most of the sample. SPOG186 is only marginally detected, so its constraints are weaker. It too has a best fit model with a higher obscuration, albeit not as high of a luminosity, of (L_X, n_H) = (4 10^ 41 erg s^ 1 , 3 10^ 23 cm^ 2 ) . Its set of six models with similarly high fractions of mock spectra in agreement with observed constraints has a parameter pair range extending from (L_X, n_H) = (3 10^ 41 erg s^ 1 , 2 10^ 23 cm^ 2 ) to (L_X, n_H) = (4 10^ 42 erg s^ 1 , 1 10^ 24 cm^ 2 ) . Given that the range of similarly-well fit models for both of these extends up to the n_H = 1 10^ 24 cm^ 2 limit of the model parameters, it is possible that one or both could have Compton-thick obscuration.

Interestingly, both SPOG157 and SPOG186 appear to be edge-on dusty disk galaxies, suggesting that at least some part of their obscuration may be on the galactic scale rather than purely at the nuclear scale.

gmw_00012252.name: S2B 1
gmw_00012252.overview: S2B 1 is a double-barred SB0/a galaxy located at a spectroscopic redshift of z=0.148 [arXiv:astro-ph/0509610]. The galaxy's structure features a large primary bar, a secondary bar, and a nuclear spiral [arXiv:astro-ph/0509610].

sources.summary: The paper identifies S2B 1, GOODS J033230.93-273923.7, as a z=0.148 double-barred SB0/a galaxy with a huge primary bar, prominent secondary bar, and nuclear spiral.

quotes.quote: Two objects were clearly identified as double-barred: GOODS J033230.93-273923.7, hereafter S2B 1, and GOODS J033233.46-274312.8, hereafter S2B 2; both lie in the CDFS part of the GOODS survey. Here we present a structural analysis of S2B 1 and S2B 2.

Apart from the huge primary bar, a very prominent secondary bar is present along with a nuclear spiral. Its spectroscopic redshift, as measured by the VIMOS VLT Deep Survey vvds , is z=0.148 . S2B 1 is therefore the most distant S2B galaxy known, at a luminosity distance of 703 Mpc Distances and image scales were derived with Ned Wright's Cosmology Calculator, http://www.astro.ucla.edu/ wright/CosmoCalc.html .

We visually classify S2B 1 as SB0/a. In order to quantify the structure of S2B 1, we performed an ellipse fit using the IRAF task ELLIPSE . The result is shown in Fig. fig:images ; both the primary and secondary bar can be clearly identified as significant local peaks in the ellipticity.

gmw_00012253.name: S2B 2
gmw_00012253.overview: S2B 2 is a double-barred SB0/a galaxy located at a spectroscopic redshift of z=0.103 [arXiv:astro-ph/0509610]. Structural analysis of the galaxy reveals an inner bar and spiral structure, with the inner and outer bars oriented nearly perpendicular to each other [arXiv:astro-ph/0509610].

sources.summary: The paper identifies S2B 2, GOODS J033233.46-274312.8, as a z=0.103 double-barred SB0/a galaxy whose unsharp mask and ellipse fits reveal nearly perpendicular bars.

quotes.quote: Two objects were clearly identified as double-barred: GOODS J033230.93-273923.7, hereafter S2B 1, and GOODS J033233.46-274312.8, hereafter S2B 2; both lie in the CDFS part of the GOODS survey. Here we present a structural analysis of S2B 1 and S2B 2.
The right panels of Fig. fig:images show an i band image, unsharp mask, and the ellipse fits for S2B 2, which we classify as SB0/a.

The small unsharp mask reveals an inner bar and spiral in the center of S2B 2. This is confirmed by a local peak in ellipticity, although this peak is relatively weak due to the small apparent size of the bar. The position angle nicely tracks the inner bar and then changes by about 90 outside of it, since the inner and outer bars in this galaxy are nearly perpendicular. S2B 2's spectroscopic redshift, as measured by 2dFGRS 2df , is z=0.103 , putting it at a luminosity distance of 475 Mpc .

gmw_00012254.name: ESO 307-13
gmw_00012254.overview: ESO 307-13 is the brightest cluster galaxy in the galaxy cluster A3376 [arXiv:1206.3389]. It is located more than 900 kiloparsecs away from the peak of the cluster's X-ray emission, a separation that may have been caused by an ongoing cluster-cluster merger [arXiv:1206.3389].

sources.summary: The paper identifies ESO 307-13 as A3376's brightest cluster galaxy and notes its unusual separation of about 900 kpc from the X-ray/ICM peak, possibly from the merger.

quotes.quote: There are two prominent galaxies in A3376. One is the brightest cluster galaxy (BCG), ESO 307-13, and the other is the bent-jet radio galaxy near the peak of the X-ray emission, MRC 0600-399 (Fig. pband ).

The BCG in A3376 is unique due to its separation of >900 kpc from the peak of X-ray surface brightness. The powerful cluster-cluster merger in A3376 may have led to a setting in which the BCG is `orphaned'.
It is also noted that the brightest galaxy in A3376 is located 900 kpc away from the peak of the X-ray emission from the cluster --possibly `orphaned' by the ongoing merger in the cluster.

gmw_00012255.name: SDSS J140821.67+025733.2
gmw_00012255.overview: SDSS J140821.67+025733.2 is a system hosting an extremely massive supermassive black hole [arXiv:2007.03222]. The mass of this black hole is estimated to be approximately 1.96 × 10^{11} solar masses, with a spin lower limit of about 0.97 [arXiv:2007.03222].

sources.summary: The paper uses SDSS J140821.67+025733.2 as an extremely massive SMBH system, adopting its mass and high spin lower limit for light-boson exclusion constraints.

quotes.quote: There are some even more massive black holes. OJ 287 has the dynamically measured mass M_ bh =(1.8348 0.0008) 10^ 10 M _ and spin a_ * =0.381 0.004 . The masses of Ton 618 and SDSS J140821.67+025733.2 are estimated to be 6.6 10^ 10 M _ and 1.96 10^ 11 M _ , respectively. The allowed parameter space of the BH mass and spin within the thin disc accretion model has been examined in Ref. , giving lower limits of the spin of about 0.6 for Ton 618 and 0.97 for SDSS J140821.67+025733.2. These SMBHs will also be included in this study.

The orange (blue) bands are those for the most massive SMBHs OJ 287, Ton 618, and SDSS J140821.67+025733.2. Our results extend remarkably the constraints compared with earlier approaches . Panel (b) of Fig. scalar_vector summarizes the combined exclusion mass regions for all the SMBHs used in this work. We can see that the mass range of 10^ 22 10^ 17 eV can be effectively constrained by these sources, particularly for the vector boson scenario due to its faster superradiance rate compared with the scalar scenario (see Eq. ( eq:4 ) and Eq. ( eq:5 )).

gmw_00012256.name: XSG6
gmw_00012256.overview: XSG6 is an early-type galaxy [arXiv:2104.01051]. Its Mg I 1.18 index measurements are reliable for stellar initial mass function analysis, though some of its other calcium index measurements are affected by pixel contamination [arXiv:2104.01051].

sources.summary: The paper finds XSG6 Mg i 1.18 measurements reliable for IMF work, but excludes Ca i 1.95 for age/IMF and Ca i 2.26 for stellar-population constraints due to contamination.

quotes.quote: According to Table tab:tabB2 , for XSG2, XSG6, XSG7, and XSG10, the fraction of affected pixels for Mg i 1.18 does not exceed the maximum fraction in Table tab:tabB1 . This shows that, to perform an IMF analysis, Mg i 1.18 index measurements are reliable for these galaxies. On the other hand, this is not the case for XSG1, XSG8, and XSG9, and therefore they were not included in the upper panels of Fig. fig:fig9 . Ca i 1.95 index is reliable for analysing age and IMF slope for all galaxies in the sample but for XSG6, while it cannot be used to constrain metallicity for XSG6, XSG7, XSG9, and XSG10. Ca i 1.99a, Ca i 1.99b, and Ca i 1.99c indices fall within a clean region and none of the pixels are affected by telluric absorption or sky emission. However, for XSG8, we find that 11 of pixels in the blue bandpasses of these indices are contaminated, and thus one cannot rely on these indices to constrain metallicity of XSG8. In the case of Ca i 2.26, only for XSG6 one cannot constrain stellar population parameters, as the fraction of affected pixels within the red bandpass (5 ) is higher than the maximum fractions provided in Table tab:tabB1 (2 for age and metallicity and 3 for IMF).

gmw_00012257.name: XSG7
gmw_00012257.overview: XSG7 is a nearby, massive early-type galaxy [arXiv:2104.01051]. It is classified as a satellite galaxy rather than a brightest cluster galaxy [arXiv:2104.01051].

sources.summary: The paper treats XSG7 as a satellite ETG whose Mg i 1.18 index remains reliable for IMF analysis, while Ca i 1.95 cannot constrain metallicity.

quotes.quote: The sample consists of seven nearby massive ETGs ( _ 0 > 300 ) selected from SPIDER survey labarbera2010 and SDSS DR7 abazajian2009 . Most of these galaxies are brightest cluster galaxies, while two of them are satellites (XSG2 and XSG7). The high-quality spectra were obtained with the X-Shooter multi-wavelength medium resolution slit echelle spectrograph vernet2011 at the ESO Very Large Telescope (VLT). The NIR part of the spectra covers a wavelength range from 9800 to 25000 with a resolution power of 5500, which allows an accurate correction of sky lines. All galaxy spectra have SNR above 100 ^ 1 .

According to Table tab:tabB2 , for XSG2, XSG6, XSG7, and XSG10, the fraction of affected pixels for Mg i 1.18 does not exceed the maximum fraction in Table tab:tabB1 . This shows that, to perform an IMF analysis, Mg i 1.18 index measurements are reliable for these galaxies. On the other hand, this is not the case for XSG1, XSG8, and XSG9, and therefore they were not included in the upper panels of Fig. fig:fig9 . Ca i 1.95 index is reliable for analysing age and IMF slope for all galaxies in the sample but for XSG6, while it cannot be used to constrain metallicity for XSG6, XSG7, XSG9, and XSG10. Ca i 1.99a, Ca i 1.99b, and Ca i 1.99c indices fall within a clean region and none of the pixels are affected by telluric absorption or sky emission. However, for XSG8, we find that 11 of pixels in the blue bandpasses of these indices are contaminated, and thus one cannot rely on these indices to constrain metallicity of XSG8. In the case of Ca i 2.26, only for XSG6 one cannot constrain stellar population parameters, as the fraction of affected pixels within the red bandpass (5 ) is higher than the maximum fractions provided in Table tab:tabB1 (2 for age and metallicity and 3 for IMF).

gmw_00012258.name: XSG10
gmw_00012258.overview: XSG10 is an early-type galaxy [arXiv:2104.01051]. Its Mg I 1.18 index measurements are reliable for stellar initial mass function analysis, though its Ca I 1.95 index cannot be used to constrain its metallicity [arXiv:2104.01051].

sources.summary: The paper finds XSG10 Mg i 1.18 measurements reliable for IMF analysis, while Ca i 1.95 cannot be used to constrain its metallicity.

quotes.quote: According to Table tab:tabB2 , for XSG2, XSG6, XSG7, and XSG10, the fraction of affected pixels for Mg i 1.18 does not exceed the maximum fraction in Table tab:tabB1 . This shows that, to perform an IMF analysis, Mg i 1.18 index measurements are reliable for these galaxies. On the other hand, this is not the case for XSG1, XSG8, and XSG9, and therefore they were not included in the upper panels of Fig. fig:fig9 . Ca i 1.95 index is reliable for analysing age and IMF slope for all galaxies in the sample but for XSG6, while it cannot be used to constrain metallicity for XSG6, XSG7, XSG9, and XSG10. Ca i 1.99a, Ca i 1.99b, and Ca i 1.99c indices fall within a clean region and none of the pixels are affected by telluric absorption or sky emission. However, for XSG8, we find that 11 of pixels in the blue bandpasses of these indices are contaminated, and thus one cannot rely on these indices to constrain metallicity of XSG8. In the case of Ca i 2.26, only for XSG6 one cannot constrain stellar population parameters, as the fraction of affected pixels within the red bandpass (5 ) is higher than the maximum fractions provided in Table tab:tabB1 (2 for age and metallicity and 3 for IMF).

gmw_00012259.name: 2MASS J20570298+1412165
gmw_00012259.overview: 2MASS J20570298+1412165 is a galaxy with a typical size on the kiloparsec scale [arXiv:2112.01748]. Infrared observations in the J, H, and K bands indicate that the galaxy has a mean luminosity of approximately 10^42 erg/s [arXiv:2112.01748].

sources.summary: The paper models the AT2019dsg host background using the named 2MASS host infrared JHK data, estimating about 10^42 erg/s luminosity, kpc-scale size, and mild TeV absorption.

quotes.quote: For host galaxy background photon fields, there was no more observation of the host galaxy 2MASS J20570298+1412165, except the infrared observation skrutskie2006 , namely J ( 1.25 m ), H ( 1.65 m ), and K ( 2.16 m ). Thus we get the mean luminosity in J, H, K bands to be about 10^ 42 erg \ s^ 1 . For the spectral profile we adopt the model in (the black line for redshift z=0 in Fig. 5 therein), and normalized to the infrared luminosity. The size of the host galaxy is typically of kpc scale. we find only mild absorption beyond TeV (see Fig. GF ).

gmw_00012260.name: FRB 20190711A host
gmw_00012260.overview: The FRB 20190711A host is a star-forming galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.522 [arXiv:2211.02279]. Although marginal spectral and continuum features have been detected, observations show no CO-line or continuum emission above standard detection thresholds [arXiv:2211.02279].

sources.summary: The paper describes the FRB 20190711A host as a z=0.522 star-forming galaxy, observed with ALMA, with marginal spectral/continuum features but no significant CO-line or continuum detection.

quotes.quote: FRB 20190711A was detected on 2019 July 11 at 01:53:41.09338 UTC with ASKAP and was localized to a host galaxy at z = 0.5220 macq20 . The host galaxy is a typical star-forming galaxy at its redshift with the stellar mass of (0.81 0.29) 10^9 M_ and the SFR of 0.42 0.12 M_ yr ^ 1 hein20a .

In the FRB 20190711A host spectrum, there is a feature of emission at 200--400 km s ^ 1 . Although the peak position of the velocity-integrated intensity map coincides with the host galaxy center, the feature is marginal (S/N = 3.3 ). There is also a 3.1 signal in the continuum map of the FRB 20190711A host, although the peak position is 0 6 away from the host galaxy center. Except for the CO emission in the FRB 20180924B host, we do not find significant emission in the CO line and continuum maps.

gmw_00012261.name: IGR J18381 0924
gmw_00012261.overview: IGR J18381 0924 is an obscured, low-luminosity Seyfert 1.9 galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.031 [arXiv:1611.02289]. Optical observations reveal that the galaxy is variable, with its continuum and emission lines brightening significantly over a two-month period [arXiv:1611.02289].

sources.summary: The paper identifies IGR J18381 0924 with an extragalactic variable counterpart at z=0.031, classifying it as an obscured low-luminosity Seyfert 1.9 galaxy supported by optical and X-ray features.

quotes.quote: IGR J18381 0924 Similarly to 88, 81 \ was first reported in 2012Krivonos and studied in more details in 2016Tomsick via both \ and \ X-ray spectroscopy. The authors showed that the source had a relatively hard spectrum consistent between ACIS-I and ISGRI, with a best-fit power law =1.4 0.1 and =3.8_ 0.7 ^ +0.9 10^ 22 2. The 0 74 \ pointing accuracy allowed for the identification of two possible near-infrared counterparts to the source in the UKIDSS Galactic Plane Survey UGPS, 2008Lucas , UGPS J183818.58-092552.9 and UGPS J183818.59-092551.8, both classified as galaxies with reported K magnitudes 13.035 0.003 and 12.833 0.002 . In the optical however, as shown in imsources , only UGPS J183818.59-092551.8 is detected.

Our FORS2 spectra, obtained in April and June 2015 ( spec18381 , magenta and green, respectively) confirm its classification as extragalactic. Indeed, we only detect red-shifted emission signatures of \ and O i at 8446 , which yields a red shift z=0.031 0.002 ( speclines_18381 ). The source is also variable and the continuum is twice as bright in June compared to April. Measurements show that the two emission lines, relatively broad with FWHMs of about 2000--2600 \ for \ and 1600 \ for O i , also brightened at constant equivalent widths, pointing towards a common origin with the continuum.

gmw_00012262.name: NGC 275
gmw_00012262.overview: NGC 275 is a late-type spiral galaxy that is interacting with the early-type system NGC 274 [arXiv:astro-ph/0611617]. This prograde encounter has produced a tidal tail extending many optical radii beyond the pair, as well as clumpy, irregular star formation across the galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0611617].

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 275 as a late-type spiral in Arp 140, emphasizing its tidal tail, prograde interaction, clumpy star formation, and lack of central concentration despite an evolved merger state.

quotes.quote: We investigate the atomic and molecular interstellar medium and star formation of NGC 275, the late-type spiral galaxy in Arp 140, which is interacting with NGC 274, an early-type system. The atomic gas ( ) observations reveal a tidal tail from NGC 275 which extends many optical radii beyond the interacting pair. The morphology implies a prograde encounter between the galaxy pair approximately 1.5 10^ 8 years ago. The H emission from NGC 275 indicates clumpy irregular star-formation, clumpiness which is mirrored by the underlying mass distribution as traced by the K_ s band emission.
Despite the evolved nature of NGC 275's interaction and its barred potential, neither the molecular gas nor the star formation are centrally concentrated.

gmw_00012263.name: NGC 274
gmw_00012263.overview: NGC 274 is an early-type galaxy classified as SAB()0 pec [arXiv:astro-ph/0611617]. It is interacting with the late-type spiral galaxy NGC 275 in the pair Arp 140, sharing a small projected linear separation of 5.3 kpc [arXiv:astro-ph/0611617].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 274 as the early-type companion in Arp 140, interacting with NGC 275; both galaxies share a recessional velocity, similar projected diameter, and a 5.3 kpc projected separation.

quotes.quote: We investigate the atomic and molecular interstellar medium and star formation of NGC 275, the late-type spiral galaxy in Arp 140, which is interacting with NGC 274, an early-type system.
The other component of Arp 140 is the early-type system NGC 274 (classified as SAB()0 pec).

The recessional velocity of both NGC 275 and NGC 274 is 1750 1975ApJ...198..527S, 1991trcb.book.....D , assuming an Hubble constant of 70 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 , this corresponds to a distance of 25 Mpc, which is used is this paper. The projected major axis diameter ( D_ 25 ) for both NGC 274 and NGC 275 is 10.9 kpc (1.5 arcmin) and the angular separation of the pair is 44'' , giving a small projected linear separation of 5.3 kpc.

gmw_00012264.name: 5BZQ J0536-3401
gmw_00012264.overview: 5BZQ J0536-3401 is a bright, nearby flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:1609.08501]. This active galactic nucleus is a blazar that exhibits a steep gamma-ray spectral component [arXiv:1609.08501].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 5BZQ J0536-3401 as a bright nearby FSRQ contributing steep gamma-ray emission in a confused 3FGL field, statistically separated from the hard 2WHSP component but excluded from the HSP detections.

quotes.quote: Exploring the sky area around 3FGL J0536.4-3347 with the ASDC error circle tool (Fig. 1WHSP0536m33 ) we note that blazar 2WHSP J053628.9-334301 is just outside the ray error ellipse; Also, there is a bright FSRQ (5BZQ J0536-3401) within 15' from the 3FGL source, and it could contribute to the overall ray flux that is observed. A likelihood analysis, assuming two ray sources instead of one (with position corresponding to 5BZQ J0536-3401 and 2WHSP J053628.9-334301), results in a model adjustment with a very large statistical significance for both, as reported in Table solved1 . Each of the resolved sources is associated with a distinct ray spectral component (one steep and one hard) in agreement with expectations: hard for the 2WHSP source and steep for the BZQ object, as shown in Fig. confSED .

We then consider 2WHSP J053628.9-334301 as part of the 2WHSP-FGL sample (with updated ray parameters), but 5BZQ J0536-3401 does not count as part of the 150 new detections associated with 2WHSP blazars (since this source is not an HSP).

gmw_00012265.name: NVSS J093514-173658
gmw_00012265.overview: NVSS J093514-173658 is a radio source identified as a blazar candidate [arXiv:1609.08501]. It exhibits Swift UV and X-ray counterparts, with a multi-frequency spectral energy distribution that likely accounts for a high-significance gamma-ray peak [arXiv:1609.08501].

sources.summary: NVSS J093514-173658 is identified as a radio source with Swift UV/X-ray counterparts and a multi-frequency SED characteristic of a blazar candidate, likely accounting for the high-significance gamma-ray TS peak.

quotes.quote: However, when inspecting this region searching information from other wavelengths, we find the radio-source NVSS J093514-173658 (Fig. BZcandXUV , in red). Recent measurements with Swift satellite XRT/UVOT show both UV and an X-ray signatures matching the radio source within their error ellipses (Fig. BZcandXUV ). Together with IR, optical, UV and X-ray counterparts we study the multi-frequency SED for NVSS J093514-173658 which turns to be a blazar candidate The term blazar candidate refers to a source with multi-frequency SED characteristic of blazars, but missing optical identification (optical spectrum is not available). with synchrotron-peak parameters _ peak 10 ^ 15.0 Hz and f_ =10 ^ 12.0 ergs/cm ^2 /s, marked as BZ-cand in Fig. unassos1 , and probable counterpart for the high-significance TS peak.

gmw_00012266.name: 2WHSP J042218.3+195054
gmw_00012266.overview: 2WHSP J042218.3+195054 is an active galactic nucleus classified as a blazar and a BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1609.08501]. The source is associated with a high-energy gamma-ray signature that peaks at its position [arXiv:1609.08501].

sources.summary: High-energy TS maps relocate the gamma-ray peak for the unassociated 3FGL J0421.6+1950 field onto 2WHSP J042218.3+195054, so the paper includes it in the 2WHSP-FGL subsample using 3FGL parameters.

quotes.quote: Since the unassociated 3FGL J0421.6+1950 is 10' away from a 2WHSP source (see Fig. unassos ), we tried to better evaluate the ray signature localization by studying the high-energy TS map (E > 3 GeV), which benefits from smaller PSF with respect to lower-energy photons. For the TS map in Fig. Unass0422 , we removed 3FGL J0421.6+1950 from the model-input so that the TS distribution has no bias from previous ray catalogs. The TS map peaks at the position of 2WHSP J042218.3+195054 (thick green circle centered on +) well within the 68 confinement region for the ray signature, which is 9.7' away from the position reported in the 3FGL catalog (thick circle centered on ). This source is taken as part of the 2WHSP-FGL subsample, using 3FGL parameters to describe it (since there is no ray confusion in this particular case).

gmw_00012267.name: 2WHSP J183806.7-600032
gmw_00012267.overview: 2WHSP J183806.7-600032 is a blazar associated with the radio source SUMSS J183806-600033 [arXiv:1609.08501]. It is also associated with the gamma-ray source 3FGL J1838.5-6006, which has had its localization improved using high-energy photons [arXiv:1609.08501].

sources.summary: The paper describes 2WHSP J183806.7-600032/SUMSS J183806-600033 as the blazar associated with 3FGL J1838.5-6006, using high-energy TS maps to improve the gamma-ray localization by 6.9 arcminutes.

quotes.quote: Here we study 3FGL J1838.5-6006, which is associated with the radio-source SUMSS J183806-600033 ( 2WHSP J183806.7-600032). ht In this case, the 2WHSP blazar is just at the border of the 3FGL error-circle (Fig. better-posit , left) and therefore we try improving the ray source localization by working only with high-energy photons only. As shown in Fig. better-posit , the high-energy TS peak is few arcminutes away from the 3FGL position, and matches our 2WHSP source (which is within the 68 confinement radius for the ray signature). Although the association is correct, this is another example where we could improve the ray signature localization ( 6.9' drift) just by working with E > 3 GeV photons. We also study this region at a lower-energy band (850-950 MeV) and there is no evidence of another close-by source that could be the cause of the offset position.

gmw_00012268.overview: 1604+159 is a low-spectral peaked quasar at a redshift of z = 0.4965 [arXiv:2401.17132]. Very Long Baseline Array observations reveal a core-jet structure, with a radio jet extending 25 milliarcseconds from the core [arXiv:2401.17132].

sources.summary: The paper studies quasar 1604+159 with VLBA polarimetry, describing its pc-scale core-jet structure, magnetic-field evolution, LSP quasar classification, redshift, gamma-ray detections, and radio properties.

quotes.quote: We have analyzed the total intensity, spectral index, linear polarization, and RM distributions at pc scale for the quasar 1604+159. The source was observed at 5.0, 8.4, and 15.4 GHz in 2002, and 4.6, 5.1, 6.0, 7.8, 12.2, 15.2, and 43.9 GHz in 2020 with the American Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA). Combining the polarization results of Monitoring Of Jets in Active galactic nuclei with VLBA Experiments (MOJAVE) at 15 GHz from 2009 to 2013, we studied the evolution of magnetic field at pc scale for the source. We detected a core-jet structure. The jet extends to the distance of 25 mas from the core at a direction of 66^ north by east.

Source 1604+159 (J1607+1551) has been classified as Low-spectral peaked (LSP) Quasar with redshift z = 0.4965 2008ApJS..175..297A . It has a Luminosity Distance of D_ L = 2799 Mpc and a linear scale of 6.06 pc/mas. It was detected by both ray detector EGRET 1995ApJS..101..259T and Fermi 2010ApJS..188..405A,2012ApJS..199...31N .

gmw_00012269.name: NGC 5514
gmw_00012269.overview: NGC 5514 is a major-merger galaxy pair featuring a large tidal tail [arXiv:2005.14598]. The system hosts an active galactic nucleus that ionizes extended gaseous clouds located more than 60 kiloparsecs away [arXiv:2112.07084, arXiv:2005.14598], while its central region exhibits shock-dominated emission and expanding bubbles [arXiv:2112.07084].

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 5514 has a very distant AGN-ionized cloud, shock-dominated central emission with expanding shells, and an energy shortfall implying its AGN has faded over about 250,000 years.

quotes.quote: The emission-line cloud found in NGC 5514 is especially distant from the nucleus - 135 (75 kpc) in projection. The cloud location and structure are shown from three different data sets in Fig. fig-ngc5514 . In this case, the MaNGaL [ O III ] data suggest additional emission closer to the AGN in the same direction as the distant cloud, projected at a radius of 48 (27 kpc).

NGC 5514 have used integral-field spectroscopy to analyze the kinematics and ionization of the gas in the central regions of NGC 5514 in great detail, finding multiple expanding (super)bubbles, and attributing the bulk of the ionization to the accompanying shocks. Our long-slit spectrum (Fig. fig-ngc5514grayscale ) shows complex emission structure, with some very distant components where the slit crosses tidal tails, and double line profiles in a pattern suggesting an expanding shell on the western side.

Comparison of the total AGN luminosity, an upper bound since not all the FIR emission is from grains heated by ionizing radiation (with a substantial contribution by young stars possible), to the minimum requirements to power the extended clouds, shows that NGC 235 is consistent with a constant luminosity over the extra light-travel time from nucleus to cloud and then to the observer, while NGC 5514 has a shortfall of at least 2.5 times. Especially for NGC 5514, the shortfall may be significant despite the approximate nature of the luminosity analysis, since analysis of EELR images at higher resolution Keel2017b shows that the highest-surface-brightness EELR clouds typically absorb only a fraction 1/3 of the incident ionizing radiation.

sources.summary: The paper presents NGC 5514 as a major-merger pair with a large tidal tail, newly mapped extended ionized clouds, and an active nucleus illuminating gas beyond 60 kpc.

quotes.quote: NGC 5514. A major-merger galaxy pair with a large tidal tail in the continuum image. The map reveals for the first time the extended gaseous clouds ionized by the active nucleus at a distance of more than 60 kpc. This galaxy has been selected for observations as external ionized clouds candidate in the TELPERION survey .

gmw_00012270.name: EMU ES J0626--5341
gmw_00012270.overview: EMU ES J0626--5341 is a radio galaxy hosted by an elliptical galaxy at the center of the Abell 3391 cluster [arXiv:2012.08775]. The galaxy has a projected radio size of 290 kpc, featuring twisted lobes that may be influenced by density variations in the intracluster medium [arXiv:2012.08775].

sources.summary: EMU ES J0626--5341 is a central Abell 3391 radio galaxy with an elliptical host, 4.5 arcmin extent, 290 kpc projected size, and twisted lobes likely affected by ICM density variations.

quotes.quote: The bright radio galaxy EMU ES J0626--5341 (EMU ES: Evolutionary Map of the Universe Early Science Source) is located in the centre of the Abell 3391 cluster and extends over 4.5 arcmin (see Fig. fig:mainsource ). It is also located at the centre of the X-ray emission (see Fig. fig:xrayover ). Its host is the elliptical galaxy 2MASX J06262045--5341358 ( PA = 54 ), which is the eastern component of ESO 161-G008, with a redshift of z = 0.055. We estimate a linear projected radio size of 290 kpc. The radio lobes show numerous twists possibly due to density variations in the ICM.

gmw_00012271.name: EMU ES J0622--5334
gmw_00012271.overview: EMU ES J0622--5334 is a large, asymmetric Fanaroff-Riley class I radio galaxy located within the Abell S0584 cluster [arXiv:2012.08775]. The galaxy is hosted by WISEA J062255.56--533434.5, and its radio lobes extend over a physical distance of 400 kpc [arXiv:2012.08775].

sources.summary: EMU ES J0622--5334 is described as a large asymmetric FR I radio galaxy in Abell S0584, hosted by WISEA J062255.56--533434.5, with 400 kpc lobes and catalogued radio fluxes.

quotes.quote: EMU ES J0622--5334 (PMN 0622--5334) is a large, rather asymmetric radio galaxy with Fanaroff-Riley class I morphology in Abell S0584 (see Fig. fig:mainsource ). Its host galaxy is WISEA J062255.56--533434.5 at z = 0.0567. The radio lobes extend over 6 arcmin, corresponding to 400 kpc. The SUMSS 843 MHz flux of PMN 0622--5334 is catalogued as 75 6 mJy, likely not including the low-surface brightness emission. The 4.85 GHz flux is catalogued as 47 8 mJy 1996ApJS..103..145W .

gmw_00012272.name: EMU ES J0632--5404
gmw_00012272.overview: EMU ES J0632--5404 is a giant radio galaxy with a Fanaroff-Riley II morphology [arXiv:2012.08775]. It is hosted by a quasi-stellar object at a preferred redshift of 0.193, giving the radio source a linear size of 1.12 Mpc [arXiv:2012.08775].

sources.summary: EMU ES J0632--5404 is a giant FR II radio galaxy with a QSO host, preferred redshift 0.193, 1.12 Mpc linear size, GLEAM counterpart, and radio spectrum consistent across low and GHz frequencies.

quotes.quote: EMU ES J0632--5404 is a GRG with an FR II morphology extending 5.8 arcmin (see Fig. fig:mainsource ) along a position angle of PA 60 . Its host galaxy is the quasi-stellar object (QSO) WISEA J063201.16--540457.4, 6dFGS gJ063201.2--540458, SWIFT J0632.1--5404 for which measured a redshift of z=0.193 , while measured z=0.2036 . Comparing both spectra we believe the former redshift (0.193) to me more reliable, and therefore find an LLS of 1.12 Mpc.

measure a 843 MHz flux of 694 mJy. This source is also GLEAM J063153-540527 with a low-frequency spectral index of -0.88, and so ASKAP may show more of the extended emission as the spectrum seems to flatten above 800 MHz. The 4.85 GHz flux from PMN (source J0631-5405) is 0.155 0.011 Jy, resulting in a spectral index of ^ 1013 _ 4850 =-0.85 , in agreement with low frequencies.

gmw_00012273.name: EMU ES J0621--5217
gmw_00012273.overview: EMU ES J0621--5217 is an asymmetric Fanaroff-Riley class I radio galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.042816 [arXiv:2012.08775]. The galaxy features inner twin jets, precessing outer lobes, and a projected linear extent of 760 kpc [arXiv:2012.08775].

sources.summary: EMU ES J0621--5217 is an asymmetric foreground FR I radio galaxy with inner twin jets, precessing outer lobes, WISEA host at z=0.042816, and a 760 kpc projected extent.

quotes.quote: EMU ES J0621--5217 is an FR I-type radio galaxy with LAS 15 arcmin with a radio core of 18.2 mJy/beam peak brightness, inner twin jets, and evidence for precession given the shape of its outermost lobes (see Fig. fig:radiogal , left panel). It is highly asymmetric, likely due to projection effects, with the western lobe much brighter and closer to the core than the eastern lobe. We identify the host galaxy as WISEA J062112.81--521700.6 ( z = 0.042816; 6dF ). The projected linear extent of J0621--5217 is 760 kpc. This galaxy is located in the foreground of A3395-A3391.

gmw_00012274.name: EMU ES J0644--5152
gmw_00012274.overview: EMU ES J0644--5152 is a giant radio galaxy with Fanaroff-Riley II morphology and radio lobes extending nearly 5 arcmin [arXiv:2012.08775]. Its host galaxy is SMSS J064437.48--515205.5, which is possibly a quasar with a photometric redshift of approximately 0.75 and a largest linear size of 2.15 Mpc [arXiv:2012.08775].

sources.summary: EMU ES J0644--5152 has the largest listed GRG size in the field, with nearly 5 arcmin FR II lobes, a possible QSO host, photometric redshift near 0.75, and LLS 2.15 Mpc.

quotes.quote: EMU ES J0644--5152 has the largest LLS of all GRGs catalogued in this field with radio lobes extending nearly 5 arcmin and FR II morphology. Its host galaxy is SMSS J064437.48--515205.5, possibly a QSO, with a photometric redshift of z 0.75 as estimated from the WISE colours (see Fig. 2 in 2018ApJS..235...10K ). We estimate its largest linear size (LLS) as 2.15 Mpc.

gmw_00012275.name: EMU ES J0639--5455
gmw_00012275.overview: EMU ES J0639--5455 is a giant radio galaxy with a wide-angle tail-like morphology [arXiv:2012.08775]. Its host galaxy is located at a photometric redshift of 0.142 and has a projected linear size of 1.07 megaparsecs [arXiv:2012.08775].

sources.summary: EMU ES J0639--5455 is a giant radio galaxy with unusual WAT-like morphology, a radio-bright host region at photometric redshift 0.142, and a rare 1.07 Mpc projected size for that morphology.

quotes.quote: EMU ES J0639--5455 is a GRG with a rather unusual, WAT-like morphology (see Fig. fig:radiogal , right panel). Its host galaxy (2MASX J06391009--5455156, WISEA J063910.02--545515.2) is located at the radio-brightest spot in the far WSW and has a photometric redshift of 0.142, thus well behind A3391-A3395 2016ApJS..225....5B . We measure an angular extent of 7.15 arcmin, corresponding to a projected linear extent of 1.07 Mpc, a size very rarely seen for sources of this radio morphology.

gmw_00012276.name: EMU ES J0621--5647
gmw_00012276.overview: EMU ES J0621--5647 is a possible Fanaroff-Riley class I giant radio galaxy hosted by 2MASX J06215057--5647566 [arXiv:2012.08775]. If its faint, unconfirmed outer radio lobes are verified, the galaxy's linear size could reach 2 Mpc [arXiv:2012.08775].

sources.summary: EMU ES J0621--5647 is a possible FR I-type giant radio galaxy at the A3391/3395 redshift, with faint lobes needing confirmation and an outer-lobe size that could reach 2 Mpc.

quotes.quote: EMU ES J0621--5647 is a FR I-type GRG, possibly extending at least 15 arcmin and possibly 32 arcmin (see Fig. fig:radiogal ). Higher radio sensitivity is required to confirm the presence and extent of its faint radio lobes due ESE and WNW. We identify 2MASX J06215057--5647566 as the host galaxy at a redshift of z=0.05391 (6dF). EMU ES J0621--5647 lies at the southern edge of the ASKAP field, but at the same redshift as A3391/3395. If the outer lobes are confirmed, the LLS of J0621--5647 is 2 Mpc.

gmw_00012277.name: SDSS J130257.20+162537.1
gmw_00012277.overview: SDSS J130257.20+162537.1 is a nearby satellite galaxy that forms a gravitationally bound pair with Mrk 783 [arXiv:2311.12189]. While the satellite's nucleus exhibits starburst-like emission, its outer disk is ionized by the active galactic nucleus of Mrk 783 [arXiv:2311.12189].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J130257.20+162537.1 as a nearby satellite and bound companion of Mrk 783 whose starburst-like nucleus and outer disk are affected by Mrk 783 ionization.

quotes.quote: Also a significant emission was detected in the galaxy SDSS J130257.20+162537.1 located at 73'' (99 kpc) projected distances to the north of Mrk 783 (hereafter SDSS J1302+1625 or `the satellite').

The SCORPIO-2 spectrum along PA=34^ reveals that the brightest and nearest candidate to a possible companion --- the galaxy SDSS J1302+1625 (`the satellite') has a systemic velocity V_ sys =20038 15 that deviates only on 72 from the velocity of Mrk 783 nucleus according our estimations along PA=34^ ( 20100 20 , it is a mean value in the , and lines) or on 165 if we accepted the velocity of Mrk 783 nucleus according SDSS data in NED. These small velocity difference implies that Mrk 783 and SDSS J130257.20+162537.1 is a gravitationally bound pair.

As we already mentioned in Sec. sec:gas its nuclear spectrum corresponds to a starburst galaxy. The emission line ratios correspond to the border between AGN and HII regions in BPT diagrams (open black star in Fig. fig:BPT , bottom panels). However the excitation of emission increase dramatically to the galaxy outskirts --- the ratio / reaches 2 that is similar with the Mrk 783 EELR (Fig. fig:lines , bottom). The corresponded points on the BPT-diagramms moves upward in the AGN area, in the region occupied by Mrk 785 EELR (the filled black star in Fig. fig:BPT , bottom). This fact implies that the outer part of the satellite disk is also ionized by the Mrk 783 AGN.

gmw_00012278.name: SDSS J123359.12+084211.5
gmw_00012278.overview: SDSS J123359.12+084211.5 is a changing-look narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:2401.10600]. It exhibits spectral changes in its Balmer lines, a broad high-state hydrogen-beta emission profile, and continuum variability of a factor of two [arXiv:2401.10600].

sources.summary: The paper lists SDSS J123359.12+084211.5 as a changing-look NLS1 with Balmer-line changes, a broad high-state H profile, and factor-of-two continuum variability.

quotes.quote: SDSS J123359.12+084211.5 was identified as CL NLS1 MacLeod2019 with changes in Balmer lines and particularly in emission. In high-state, its FWHM (H ) was 2430 Liu2019 . The continuum itself varied by a factor 2.

gmw_00012279.name: ZTF18aajupnt
gmw_00012279.overview: ZTF18aajupnt is a changing-look active galactic nucleus that exhibits a narrow-line Seyfert 1 spectrum in its high state and a LINER spectrum in its low state [arXiv:2401.10600]. Its high-state spectrum displays strong coronal lines, and the source has undergone a luminous soft X-ray and mid-infrared flare [arXiv:2401.10600].

sources.summary: The paper describes ZTF18aajupnt as an NLS1/LINER changing-look source with strong high-state coronal lines and a luminous soft X-ray and mid-infrared flare.

quotes.quote: ZTF18aajupnt was classified as NLS1 in high-state, with a LINER spectrum in its low-state Frederick2019 . Its high-state spectrum shows strong coronal lines. A luminous soft X-ray and mid-infrared flare was detected.

gmw_00012280.name: GAMA J115927.23-010918.4
gmw_00012280.overview: GAMA J115927.23-010918.4 is a disk galaxy that exhibits large-scale galactic winds and a gas velocity gradient misaligned with its disk [arXiv:1601.02022]. Enhanced extraplanar line ratios suggest these winds contain embedded shocks, which may be influenced by a nearby companion galaxy [arXiv:1601.02022].

sources.summary: GAMA J115927.23-010918.4 is presented as a strong wind case: extraplanar line ratios, shocks, a misaligned gas velocity field, and a nearby companion suggest large-scale outflows.

quotes.quote: Figure fig:31452 shows the emission line flux, line ratio, and kinematic maps of GAMA J115927.23-010918.4 (CATAID CATAID is the galaxy identifier of GAMA : 31452) and demonstrates the immediate products coming out of our \ pipeline. These maps are useful for investigating the properties of the extraplanar gas, as we will elaborate on in later sections. In this particular galaxy, several features are immediately obvious and imply the presence of large-scale galactic winds. The /H , /H and /H line ratio maps show that the line ratios increase as a function of height off the disc plane, and suggest that the physical conditions of the ionized gas change dramatically off the disc plane. The enhanced line ratios are likely due to shocks embedded in galactic winds.

The velocity map of the gas, v_ gas , shows that the overall velocity gradient is mis-aligned with the galaxy disc, which is caused by the complex velocity field of the bipolar winds superimposed on the disc rotation (see Section 4). Although there are no strong indications of on-going interactions in GAMA J115927.23-010918.4 ( z=0.0202 ), the presence of nearby galaxy GAMA J115923.94-010915.4 (about 50 arcsec or 23 kpc away) at a similar redshift ( z=0.0204 ) implies that the environment might play a role in triggering the winds e.g. Rich:2010yq,Vogt:2013uq . Higher spatial resolution and multi-wavelength observations are required to determine the precise geometry and cause of the galactic winds in GAMA J115927.23-010918.4; nevertheless, the pixel-to-pixel maps reconstructed from our IFS data are sufficient to reveal the existence of strong disc-halo interactions through galactic winds.

gmw_00012281.name: SBS 0848+526
gmw_00012281.overview: SBS 0848+526 is an emission-line galaxy with a bright nucleus that is part of an interacting pair [arXiv:astro-ph/0612276]. This system features a neighboring galaxy to the northeast and a connecting plume of extended emission, suggesting that the interaction has triggered nuclear activity [arXiv:astro-ph/0612276].

sources.summary: The paper describes SBS 0848+526 as a reddened starburst nucleus in an interacting pair, with a plume and multiwavelength detections suggesting interaction-triggered nuclear activity.

quotes.quote: SBS 0848+526 This emission line object is an X-ray (ROSAT), radio (FIRST) and infrared (2MASS) source. The host galaxy is aligned roughly in the NS direction, and it has a brighter neighbor (around 0 5) separated by 14 \ to the NE (see description below). The SDSS image shows that the two objects have bright nuclei, and a plume extending between both galaxies, approximately aligned in the EW direction, which coincides with the slit alignment during the observations. Thus, it was possible to detect extended emission of , \ and \ along this plume. Hence, the pair of objects seems to be another case of nuclear activity (either star formation and/or AGN) triggered by galaxy interaction.

gmw_00012282.name: SBS 0944+540
gmw_00012282.overview: SBS 0944+540 is a well-known, radio-weak quasar [arXiv:astro-ph/0612276]. Its spectrum exhibits strong emission blends, weak helium features, and an asymmetric broad-line profile with no observed narrow components [arXiv:astro-ph/0612276].

sources.summary: SBS 0944+540 is presented as a radio-weak QSO with strong blends, weak helium features, no observed narrow components, and an asymmetric broad-line profile.

quotes.quote: SBS 0944+540 This is a well known QSO e.g. veron,hewitt and a radio weak source bischof . It shows strong emission of \ blends. After subtracting the \ emission, there is evidence for a weak \ 4686 emission. Although the spectrum at the red end is noisy, it shows a weak increase near 8750 , which corresponds to the \ 5876 emission line.

This line is clearly detected on the SDSS spectrum. Neither forbidden lines nor narrow components of permitted lines are observed. The \ profile is very asymmetric and shows a strong blue bump. This line can be fitted using either two broad Lorentzian or three broad Gaussian profiles.

gmw_00012283.name: SBS 1136+595
gmw_00012283.overview: SBS 1136+595 is a 2MASS source classified as a narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0612276]. Its narrow-line region shows evidence of stratification, and the galaxy exhibits moderate reddening for an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0612276].

sources.summary: The paper describes SBS 1136+595 as a 2MASS NLS1 whose line fitting resembles SBS 1118+541, with stratified narrow-line emission and moderate reddening.

quotes.quote: SBS 1136+595 This object is a 2MASS source. It was classified as a Sy 1 galaxy by , but appears as a NLS1 galaxy in the SBS. The \ is strong and, after subtraction, it is possible to detect a very noisy \ 4686 (Fig. fig:hbfit ). The FWHM of this line is poorly estimated, yielding a value of 10240 \ (8700 \ for the Lorentzian fit).

The remarks about the classification of SBS 1118+541 given above are also applicable to SBS 1136+595. Also, as in the case of SBS 1118+541, the NLR shows evidence of stratification. Thus, the \ and \ narrow components have widths much larger than the forbidden lines, and the differences among the FWHM of the forbidden lines, in the range 350-540 , are also large but not as much as in SBS 1118+541, probably due to the absence of the \ and \ lines. The narrow components / \ ratio (4.24) and the reddening ( A_V=0.88 ) are larger than in SBS 1118+541, but still moderate for an AGN (Table tab:redden ).

gmw_00012284.name: SBS 1204+505B
gmw_00012284.overview: SBS 1204+505B is an active galactic nucleus and a ROSAT and 2MASS source [arXiv:astro-ph/0612276]. It is classified as a Seyfert 1.9 galaxy, exhibits intrinsic reddening, and probably belongs to a compact group of three galaxies [arXiv:astro-ph/0612276].

sources.summary: SBS 1204+505B is discussed as a ROSAT/2MASS AGN in a probable compact group, with reddening, a large black-hole mass, and a revised Sy 1.9 classification.

quotes.quote: SBS 1204+505B and companion SBS 1204+505B is a ROSAT and 2MASS source. In the SBS catalogue, it is classified as a Sy 1.8 galaxy. The object probably belongs to a compact group of three galaxies.

The narrow components / \ ratio has a value of 5.02, indicating an important amount of intrinsic reddening ( A_V = 1.35 ). The BH mass estimated from the \ FWHM is 2 10^8 \ M _ . The loci of all the emission line ratios in the diagnostic diagrams (Fig. fig:diagnos ) are in the AGN domain. The spectrum presented in this paper is consistent with that of a Sy 1.9, in contrast with both the SBS catalogue and the SDSS spectrum, which favor a Sy 1.8 classification.

gmw_00012285.name: SBS 1340+569
gmw_00012285.overview: SBS 1340+569 is classified as a Seyfert 1.8 galaxy that likely contains a starburst-nucleus component [arXiv:astro-ph/0612276]. It has a nearby emission-line neighbor galaxy at the same redshift that is likely a starburst galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0612276].

sources.summary: SBS 1340+569 is presented as a ROSAT/2MASS Sy 1.8 with a starburst-nucleus component, a same-redshift neighbor, mixed diagnostic ratios, varied line widths, and low reddening.

quotes.quote: SBS 1340+569 This object is a ROSAT and 2MASS source, and it is classified as a Sy 1.8 galaxy in the SBS catalogue. It has an emission line neighbor galaxy located 4 4 to the East and 14 to the South. The SDSS image for this neighbor does not show a star-like nucleus, and the spectra show that both objects share the same redshift (0.04) and that the neighbor is probably a SB galaxy.

SBS 1340+569 is probably a Sy 1.8 galaxy with a SBN component. The diagnostic plots for the available line ratios cover both the AGN and \ region domains. Besides, there is a variety of line widths for different ions and between the broad and narrow components of \ and , being the widths of the narrow lines in the \ region rather small for an AGN. The amount of reddening is small, as well as the size estimated for the BLR ( 6 light days).

gmw_00012286.name: companion galaxy of SBS 1204+505B
gmw_00012286.overview: The companion galaxy of SBS 1204+505B is located 33 arcseconds away from SBS 1204+505B [arXiv:astro-ph/0612276]. It is classified as a starburst nucleus galaxy, although it exhibits some broad, weak-line spectral behavior [arXiv:astro-ph/0612276].

sources.summary: The companion is separated from SBS 1204+505B by 33 arcseconds, has a modest velocity offset, strong reddening, and is classified as a starburst nucleus despite broad weak-line behavior.

quotes.quote: About the companion, it is separated 33 \ from the SBS object. The systemic velocity difference between both galaxies is 420 30 .

The visual aspect (bright nucleus) and a glance to the narrow line spectrum suggest that the companion is a SBN. However, the line ratios are not well determined and two of three upper limits are compatible with both \ regions and low activity AGN (see Fig. fig:diagnos ). Only the / \ upper limit is incompatible with an AGN.

The FWHM of \ (450 ) is too broad for a SBN or a \ region, although the line is weak (it is barely detected at the 3 level) and all the other lines have FWHM less than 300 . Following oster89 , the 6717 / 6731 line ratio (1.3) yields an electron density 100 \ cm ^ 3 (for a range of temperatures between 5000 and 2 10^4 K ) which is typical for the spread-out giant \ regions. Thus, the companion galaxy can be classified as a SBN.

gmw_00012287.name: brightest cluster galaxy (BCG)
gmw_00012287.overview: This brightest cluster galaxy is located at the center of its cluster [arXiv:2209.00043]. It exhibits central plumes spanning 25 to 100 kiloparsecs, which likely result from an interaction with a nearby massive galaxy [arXiv:2209.00043].

sources.summary: The paper treats the SMACS0723 BCG as the central unmasked galaxy, measuring streams and plumes relative to it and interpreting central plumes as interaction with a massive galaxy.

quotes.quote: The brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) is left unmasked in both the ``hot'' and ``cold'' masks.

The most notable features in the image are highlighted: two East Streams (1 and 2), the Giant West Loop and plumes East and West of the BCG. The East Stream 1 is located at 150 kpc from the BCG, while the East Stream 2 is at 243 kpc. The Giant West Loop covers a diameter of 100 kpc, from 150 kpc to 250 kpc from the center of the BCG.
The plumes seen in the center of the cluster span between 25 to 100 kpc from the BCG. They seem to be the result of the interaction of the BCG with a massive galaxy Lauer1988 .

gmw_00012288.name: galaxy D
gmw_00012288.overview: Galaxy D is the brightest galaxy located near the X-ray maximum of its hosting subcluster [arXiv:astro-ph/0406478]. It is offset from the maximum of the residuals by approximately 40 kpc and has a redshift of 0.3704 [arXiv:astro-ph/0406478].

sources.summary: The paper identifies galaxy D as the brightest galaxy near the subcluster X-ray maximum, offset from the gas by about 40 kpc, and uses its redshift to constrain the subcluster velocity.

quotes.quote: Galaxy D coincides well with the X-ray maximum of the subcluster. This galaxy is by far the brightest in this region. There is an offset of roughly 5'' between galaxy D and the maximum of the residuals. The projected offset corresponds to 40 kpc.

xSince we only have the redshift for one galaxy in the subcluster, galaxy D ( z=0.3704 ) we assume that this redshift is representative for the entire subcluster. Calculating the mean redshift of the other three galaxies (A, B, and C), assuming that they all belong to the main cluster and neglecting peculiar motions, we find z=0.3612 . The difference in redshift between this mean redshift and the redshift of galaxy D corresponds to a velocity of 2750 km/s. In order to estimate the minimal v_ we compare the redshift of galaxy D with the redshift of galaxy C ( z=0.3645 ), which has of the three galaxies A, B and C the redshift closest to galaxy D.

gmw_00012289.name: G419067
gmw_00012289.overview: G419067 is a galaxy evaluated as a D-grade gravitational lens model [arXiv:2301.05320]. This model exhibited a negative likelihood due to high image residuals located in the very center of its lens light profile [arXiv:2301.05320].

sources.summary: The paper treats G419067 as a D-grade lens model with negative likelihood caused by central lens-light residuals; blind visual inspection kept it, but residual maps would likely remove it.

quotes.quote: One D-grade model (G419067) with a negative likelihood was a result of high image residuals in the very center of the lens light profile. Note the asterisk in the ln (evidence) column of Table table:graded_models . This model scored well enough for inclusion (spectrum score 4, total score 22) by the blind visual inspection. However, the visual inspection may have removed this candidate with the inclusion of a residual or ^2 map in addition to the model images.

gmw_00012290.name: G250289
gmw_00012290.overview: G250289 is a group-member galaxy that lies within a relatively small dark matter halo [arXiv:2301.05320]. It is identified as a strong gravitational lens candidate with a high stellar mass [arXiv:2301.05320].

sources.summary: The paper identifies G250289 with HSC J083726+015639 and models it as a B-grade group-member lens with high stellar mass, corrected bandpass luminosity, and a typical 1.5 arcsec Einstein radius.

quotes.quote: To the authors' knowledge, none of these lens candidates have been previously confirmed with high-resolution (HST-quality) imaging or spectroscopy. G250289 was identified in HSC as J083726+015639 by . Spectrum scores of 6 or better can be considered to be probable spectroscopic evidence for the lens candidate, and the highest spectrum scores for the two A grades can be considered spectroscopic confirmations.

Special attention should be given to G250289, which was instead modeled from its -band image. In attempting to give the models the best chance to succeed, we were inconsistent in the choice of bandpass for modeling (see Section sect:bandpass ). Luminosity and mass-to-light ratios for this model are corrected to the g-band after -band restframe k-correction. This is done by multiplying (or dividing) by an additional factor 10^ 0.4(g-) , where (g-) 0.285 is the color difference calculated by integrating the product of each bandpass response function and a template elliptical galaxy spectrum from over the bandpass range.

The only group-member galaxy, G250289 (marked with a red cross), lies in one of the smaller dark matter halos and has the highest stellar mass. G323152 (marked with a black circle, not listed in GAMA GroupFinding catalog) has a similar dark mass and about one-fifth of the stellar mass compared to G250289. Conversely, the two isolated galaxies have the highest dark masses and the lowest stellar masses. The higher stellar mass in G250289 could have more to do with effects from the difference in -band and g-band S/N than a physical interpretation.

gmw_00012291.name: G62734
gmw_00012291.overview: G62734 is a rank two group member galaxy [arXiv:2301.05320]. Its central dark matter content is poorly constrained, which may be a result of the galaxy's distance from the center of the group mass [arXiv:2301.05320].

sources.summary: The paper excludes B-grade group member G62734 from the clean final model set because its central dark matter content is poorly constrained, possibly due to distance from the group mass center.

quotes.quote: One B-grade model, G62734, is not included because its central dark matter content is poorly constrained. This and the other 14 lower-grade models are considered to be worth revisiting but were not successful enough to present alongside the cleaner examples we present here.

The rank 2 group member is G62734, which was removed from the final selection because its dark matter content was poorly constrained. This could be a result of this galaxy's distance from the center of the group mass.

gmw_00012292.name: G323152
gmw_00012292.overview: G323152 is an A-grade strong gravitational lensing candidate [arXiv:2301.05320]. It is not represented in the GAMA group catalogs [arXiv:2301.05320], and its spectrum features strong emission lines originating from its background source [arXiv:2301.05320].

sources.summary: The paper presents G323152 as an A-grade strong-lens candidate not in GAMA group catalogs, with background-source emission dominating Autoz and dark mass similar to G250289 but much lower stellar mass.

quotes.quote: G323152 (marked with a black circle, not listed in GAMA GroupFinding catalog) has a similar dark mass and about one-fifth of the stellar mass compared to G250289.
(G323152) is not represented in GAMA group catalogs. We note that most strong lensing galaxies should be the most massive galaxies in their halo, either in a group or in isolation.

One of these is one of the two highest-scoring candidates (A-grade, candidate G323152, PG+ELG). G323152 represents the case described in the section sec:autoz_cases where very strong emission lines from the background source are interpreted as the primary redshift match instead of the lower redshift passive continuum.

gmw_00012293.name: G138582
gmw_00012293.overview: G138582 is identified as an A-grade emission line galaxy lens candidate [arXiv:2301.05320]. The emission lines from both the foreground lens and the background source are clearly determined, resulting in a highly successful gravitational lens model [arXiv:2301.05320].

sources.summary: The paper highlights G138582 as an A-grade ELG+ELG lens candidate whose lens and source emission lines are clearly determined and whose model is among the study's most successful.

quotes.quote: Interestingly, one of the highest scoring (A-grade candidate G138582) candidates was one of the ELG+ELG matches. As shown in Appendix Figure fig:2828 , the emission lines from lens and source are clearly determined, and the resulting model was one of the most successful of this study. This example highlights the potential value of including (though with critical evaluation) the ELG+ foreground lens template configurations in the selection.

gmw_00012294.name: G544226
gmw_00012294.overview: G544226 is a foreground lens galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.227 [arXiv:2301.05320]. It has a stellar mass of log M_* = 11.29 and acts as a strong gravitational lens for a background emission line galaxy at z=0.650 [arXiv:2301.05320].

sources.summary: The paper discusses duplicate Autoz entries for G544226, accepting the foreground-lens PG match at z=0.227 with ELG source z=0.650 and noting its overlap between GAMA and LinKS selections.

quotes.quote: For G544226, both entries show a PG template match at redshift z=0.227 with an ELG at redshift z=0.650 . The accepted entry shows higher _1 , _2 , and R, and it attributes the primary redshift match to the foreground lens galaxy. The other entry is an example where _1 can refer to the background source galaxy and _2 to the foreground lens galaxy, effectively reversing which shows "better" match while still identifying the redshifts and type correctly.

G544226 then would have been the one overlap between the GAMA spectroscopic and LinKS machine learning catalogs in if it had not been removed. G544226 made the selection for high-quality candidates in . With a redshift of z=0.227 and M_* =11.29 , it existed squarely in the overlap of parameters space between the GAMA spectroscopy and LinKS machine learning candidates.

gmw_00012295.name: Oii blob 10
gmw_00012295.overview: Oii blob 10 is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of z=1.18 that exhibits spatially extended [Oii] emission [arXiv:1406.7052]. Spectroscopic analysis reveals blueshifted magnesium and iron absorption lines, indicating a gas outflow with a velocity of 80 to 260 kilometers per second [arXiv:1406.7052].

sources.summary: The paper studies Oii blob 10 as a z=1.18 high-redshift galaxy with extended [Oii] emission, blueshifted absorption, outflow near escape velocity, and possible composite star formation/type-2 AGN power.

quotes.quote: We present the Keck/MOSFIRE and Magellan/LDSS3 spectroscopy and the archival imaging data of thirteen bands for the object with the spatially extended Oii 3726,3729 emission at z=1.18 , Oii blob 10 ( ). Following the study of 2013ApJ...779...53Y , our data provide new insight into the physical properties of . This is the first detailed spectroscopic study of oxygen-line blobs which includes the analyses of the escape velocity, the mass loading factor, and the presence of an AGN , and is a significant step for understanding the nature of oxygen-line blobs and the relationship between gas outflow and star formation quenching at high redshift.

The velocity offsets from the systemic velocity are v =-260 40 \ and v =-80 50 \ km \ s^ 1 for the Mg ii and Fe ii absorption lines, respectively. These blueshifted absorption lines indicate that \ has an outflow whose velocity is 80-260 \ . The escape velocity of \ is estimated to be v_ esc =250 140 \ .

gmw_00012296.name: PKS B0800 09
gmw_00012296.overview: PKS B0800 09 is a polarized double radio galaxy characterized by its radio lobes [arXiv:2005.09266]. Archival VLA observations reveal a probable radio core and a one-sided jet, suggesting its host galaxy is AllWISE J080236.28 095739.9 [arXiv:2005.09266].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PKS B0800 09 as a polarised double radio galaxy, discusses its radio lobes, and argues from archival VLA core/jet evidence for a different host galaxy.

quotes.quote: These sources are (i) POGSII-EG-250 and POGSII-EG-251 (GLEAM J080225 095823 and GLEAM J080253 095822), which together form the radio lobes of PKS B0800 09 e.g. Bolton1968 , catalogued by White2020b as `G4Jy 680', and (ii) POGSII-EG-265 and POGSII-EG-266 (GLEAM J094633 132703 and GLEAM J094739 134806), which comprise the hotspots associated with the giant radio galaxy `J0947 1338' e.g. Kumicz2018 .

We note some disagreement in the literature regarding the host galaxy of PKS B0800 09. The host currently adopted by the the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database (NED), marked by the green `x' in Figure fig:exgal_doubles , is AllWISE J080239.90 095809.8 at z=0.0892 SDSS DR12; Alam2015 . However, archival B/C configuration VLA data at 4.89 GHz (project AJ 141, observed 1986 Oct 01) sourced from the NVAS reveal a probable radio core and one-sided jet associated with AllWISE J080236.28 095739.9 z=0.0699 ; Jones2009 , and so we suggest that this is in fact the correct host for PKS B0800 09 (marked by a white `+'). We note that, as discussed by , this was the host identification favoured by Schilizzi1975 .

gmw_00012297.name: NGC 4049
gmw_00012297.overview: NGC 4049 is a small irregular galaxy with a disturbed, bar-like appearance [arXiv:astro-ph/0511081]. It is a potential nearby interaction partner for the larger galaxy NGC 4064, although a collision scenario remains uncertain [arXiv:astro-ph/0511081].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4049 as a possible nearby interaction partner for NGC 4064, describing it as a disturbed small irregular galaxy but finding a collision scenario uncertain.

quotes.quote: The closest candidate for such an interaction is NGC 4049, a small irregular galaxy, located at a distance of 30' ( 130 kpc) from NGC 4064. Its velocity is 829 , and its magnitude is B=14.25 which implies a mass of 1/6 the mass of NGC 4064. The DSS image shows that NGC 4049 is a disturbed galaxy with a bar-like appearance.
The nearby galaxy NGC 4049 is disturbed, although has more gas than NGC 4064, inconsistent with a collision scenario since it is a smaller galaxy than NGC 4064.
A tidal interaction or collision with a companion (such as NGC 4049) cannot however be ruled out.

gmw_00012298.name: JO171
gmw_00012298.overview: JO171 is a Hoag-like ring galaxy located in the dense cluster Abell 3667 [arXiv:1802.07294]. It features a young stellar ring that counter-rotates relative to its central spheroid, as well as extended tails of ionized gas stripped by ram pressure [arXiv:1802.07294].

sources.summary: The paper presents JO171 as a Hoag-like ring galaxy in Abell 3667, with a young stellar ring, stripping tails, counter-rotation, and ram-pressure quenching after earlier gas accretion.

quotes.quote: Through an ongoing MUSE program dedicated to study gas removal processes in galaxies (GAs Stripping Phenomena in galaxies with MUSE, GASP), we have obtained deep and wide integral field spectroscopy of the galaxy JO171. This galaxy resembles the Hoag's galaxy, one of the most spectacular examples of ring galaxies, characterized by a completely detached ring of young stars surrounding a central old spheroid. At odds with the isolated Hoag's galaxy, JO171 is part of a dense environment, the cluster Abell 3667, which is causing gas stripping along tentacles. Moreover, its ring counter-rotates with respect to the central spheroid. The joint analysis of the stellar populations and the gas/stellar kinematics shows that the origin of the ring was not due to an internal mechanism, but was related to a gas accretion event that happened in the distant past, prior to accretion onto Abell 3667, most probably within a filament.

Figure fig:rgb also shows clear signatures of extended tails of ionized gas towards the north, in the direction opposite to the cluster center, confirming that JO171 is a jellyfish galaxy, whose disturbed morphology is linked with the environmental conditions.

At odds with the Hoag's galaxy, JO171 shows extended tails towards the north characterized by ionized gas and ongoing star formation: while the Hoag's galaxy is isolated, JO171 belongs to a very active and rich galaxy cluster whose intracluster medium exerts a ram pressure force that creates the tails of stripped gas to the north. As a consequence, its ring is clearly asymmetric in terms of the stellar populations/gas properties: the stripping of gas by ram pressure has caused the south-western part of the galaxy to be devoid of gas and, as we will see in 3.3, of ongoing star formation.

gmw_00012299.name: SDSS J0012-1022
gmw_00012299.overview: SDSS J0012-1022 is an active galactic nucleus with an asymmetric broad-line profile [arXiv:0912.0541]. The object is a candidate binary black hole system [arXiv:0912.0541] that exhibits broad-line variability, including a velocity shift detected over 8.3 years and line narrowing [arXiv:1312.6694].

sources.summary: The paper presents SDSS J0012-1022 as an asymmetric broad-line AGN whose profile can be fit by two components, making it a promising binary candidate despite not being obviously double-peaked.

quotes.quote: An example, SDSS J0012-1022 Strictly speaking, this is not a double-peaked profile, but an asymmetric profile with an extended red wing. Nevertheless it was included in the double-peaked Balmer line AGN sample in Strateva_etal_2003 . , is shown in Fig. \ fig:dp_examp , whose broad line profile can be well fitted by two components.

Note that the velocity splitting of this object is smaller than the FWHM of the redshifted component, and both broad components are reasonably well-fitted by a single Gaussian. If it is of a binary origin, its separation must be large enough such that the two BLRs are mostly distinct. Even though its profile is not obviously double-peaked, it still stands out as a promising binary candidate.

sources.summary: The paper attributes this object to broad-line variability, describing an 8.3 yr velocity shift, line narrowing, matching accelerations from prior work, and earlier reported velocity changes.

quotes.quote: J001224.02 102226.5 . We detect a velocity shift over 8.3 yr in broad . The broad \ line, and in particular the broad base of the line profile, got narrower, similar to the profile change reported in broad \ by Decarli2013 . The radial acceleration measured from broad \ is [ 29 , 16 ] km s ^ 1 yr ^ 1 (2.5 ), consistent with the acceleration ( 32^ +17 _ 26 km s ^ 1 yr ^ 1 ) reported in broad \ by Decarli2013 based on a spectrum taken 8.1 yr after the original SDSS observation. This object was also noted by shen10 , zamfir10 , and eracleous11 . eracleous11 reported a velocity shift of +125^ +35 _ 30 km s ^ 1 in the \ region based on a spectrum taken 6.8 yr after the original SDSS observation; no second-epoch spectrum was available for the \ region.

gmw_00012300.name: Abell 780
gmw_00012300.overview: Abell 780 is a brightest cluster galaxy that hosts a very young stellar population of less than 200 million years in its inner region [arXiv:2011.05350]. In contrast, the galaxy's outer region exhibits an intermediate to old stellar population, and its spectrum displays emission lines [arXiv:2011.05350].

sources.summary: The paper reports that the BCG in Abell 780 has a very young central stellar population, an intermediate/old outer age, and emission lines.

quotes.quote: Two of these BCGs, Abell 780 and Abell 2055, have intermediate/old SSP-equivalent ages in their outer bins (and thus relatively large SSP-equivalent age gradients), whereas Abell 1795 has a fairly young ( 2 Gyr) SSP-equivalent age in its outer aperture.

Unlike the BCGs in Abell 780, 1795 and 2055, which show young components as well as emission lines in their spectra, the BCG in Abell 646 shows some emission lines in its spectrum, but a stellar component in the inner aperture of age 3.7 Gyr.

We summarise our main conclusions as follows: Of the fourteen MENeaCS BCGs, we find three BCGs (Abell 780, Abell 1795 and Abell 2055) with very young stellar populations ( < 200 Myr) in their inner (0 -- 5 kpc) apertures.

gmw_00012301.name: BCG in Abell 2055
gmw_00012301.overview: The brightest cluster galaxy in Abell 2055 hosts a dominant BL Lac point source [arXiv:2011.05350]. It features optical emission lines, a blue core, and a stellar population that is very young in its inner region but intermediate to old in its outer bins [arXiv:2011.05350].

sources.summary: The paper describes the BCG in Abell 2055 as having a young central population, intermediate/old outer age, emission lines, a blue core, and a dominant BL Lac point source.

quotes.quote: Two of these BCGs, Abell 780 and Abell 2055, have intermediate/old SSP-equivalent ages in their outer bins (and thus relatively large SSP-equivalent age gradients), whereas Abell 1795 has a fairly young ( 2 Gyr) SSP-equivalent age in its outer aperture.

From Figure fig:ML it follows, similar to our conclusions in Loubser2016 , that the BCGs with young stellar populations are located in host clusters with short cooling times, with the exception of the BCG in Abell 2055. The BCG in Abell 2055 (top left corner in Figure fig:ML ) shows optical emission lines in its spectrum, a blue core Bildfell2008 , and a young stellar component, however the host cluster has the longest cooling time of all the clusters considered here. This BCG hosts a BL Lac point source that is the dominant contributor to the observed emission Green2017 .

We summarise our main conclusions as follows: Of the fourteen MENeaCS BCGs, we find three BCGs (Abell 780, Abell 1795 and Abell 2055) with very young stellar populations ( < 200 Myr) in their inner (0 -- 5 kpc) apertures.

gmw_00012302.name: BCG in Abell 646
gmw_00012302.overview: The brightest cluster galaxy in Abell 646 is an intermediate-component galaxy that features a blue core [arXiv:2011.05350]. Its spectrum exhibits some emission lines, and its inner aperture hosts an intermediate-age stellar population estimated at 3.7 billion years old [arXiv:2011.05350].

sources.summary: The paper distinguishes the BCG in Abell 646 from younger-component BCGs, noting emission lines, a blue core, and an intermediate-age inner stellar population fit with an SSP model.

quotes.quote: Unlike the BCGs in Abell 780, 1795 and 2055, which show young components as well as emission lines in their spectra, the BCG in Abell 646 shows some emission lines in its spectrum, but a stellar component in the inner aperture of age 3.7 Gyr. It also has a blue core as determined by Bildfell2008 . There is no significant difference between the ^ 2 values for the stellar population fits in the inner aperture for an SSP and a two component composite stellar population fit, so we default to an SSP fit (as described in Loubser2016 ). This is thus an intermediate component galaxy, but with a stellar population component (in the inner bin) on the limit of where a small fraction of young stars cannot be confidently detected (as described in detail in Loubser2016 ).

gmw_00012303.name: Abell 1942
gmw_00012303.overview: The brightest cluster galaxy in Abell 1942 is a bright early-type galaxy with a high K-band luminosity [arXiv:2011.05350]. It has a below-average central velocity dispersion and experienced relatively recent star formation, resulting in a single stellar population equivalent age of 4 Gyr [arXiv:2011.05350].

sources.summary: The paper finds the BCG in Abell 1942 has low dynamical mass-to-light ratio, recent star formation, high K-band brightness, below-average velocity dispersion, and peculiar properties.

quotes.quote: Discussion on constraining the IMF From Figure ML_ML , we can see that, for a Salpeter IMF, the majority (ten) of the BCGs fall above the 1-to-1 line, but there is a subset of four BCGs below the 1-to-1 line (Abell 754, 1689, 1763 and 1942). The BCGs above the line (with _ DYN > _ POP ) are better described by the bottom-heavy Salpeter, or an even `heavier', IMF due to a higher fraction of low mass stars. For relatively old stellar populations (as expected for passively-evolving, massive early-type galaxies), only stars with masses below 1M _ are present (see MartinNavarro2015b, Lyubenova2016 ). This limits stellar populations based IMF analysis of massive early-type galaxies to its low-mass end. As seen from Figure ML_ML , a Kroupa IMF (which has fewer stars with masses below 0.5M _ than a Salpeter IMF) describes the four BCGs below the 1-to-1 line better, and is closer to reconciling the stellar mass-to-light ratios measured from stellar population and kinematic properties.

Abell 1942 ( _ DYN = 1.23 ) has an SSP-equivalent age of 4 Gyr, and therefore had more recent star formation (but not enough or recent enough to constrain the young stellar component, see Loubser2016 ). Abell 1942 is also bright (M _ K = --27.40 mag, compared to the average in our sample M _ K = -26.52 mag), but with a below-average central velocity dispersion (296 km s ^ 1 ) compared to the other BCGs. For the BCG in Abell 1763 ( _ DYN = 1.89 ), we also find that it has one of the lowest contributions of dark matter mass to total mass in the centre (3.4 per cent) on account of its brightness (M _ K = --27.33 mag) and high stellar mass. Therefore, the BCGs of Abell 1942 and 1763 are peculiar in that they are very bright (i.e. high stellar mass, and low _ DYN ) compared to other BCGs of similar velocity dispersion.

gmw_00012304.name: source 127
gmw_00012304.overview: Source 127 is a spiral galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.032 [arXiv:astro-ph/0510674]. Its absorbed X-ray spectrum and optical counterpart suggest that the galaxy hosts a low-luminosity Seyfert-2 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0510674].

sources.summary: The paper links source 127 to the spiral galaxy ESO 217-G29 and argues from its absorbed X-ray spectrum, redshift, luminosity, and optical counterpart that it is a new low-luminosity Seyfert-2 galaxy.

quotes.quote: After the astrometric correction, the resulting X--ray position is _ J2000 =12 ^h 10 ^m 28.87 ^s , _ J2000 =-52 ^ 21' 45.7''. Searching the NED ( Nasa/Ipac Extragalactic Database ) we found the spiral galaxy ESO 217-G29, located at 1.28" from the X--ray source position. The magnitudes of ESO 217--G29 are B_ J =16.74 and F=14.93 and its redshift is z=0.032 Visvanathan1992 . These parameters, together with the X--ray spectrum and the estimated X--ray--to--optical flux ratio, suggest that source 127 could be an AGN.

The X--ray luminosity of the source in the 2--10 keV energy band, corrected by the absorption and with the redshift at 0.032, is 2.59 _ 1.07 ^ +1.84 10^ 42 erg s ^ 1 , corresponding to a low luminosity Seyfert galaxy. Thus, the X--ray spectrum, together with the best--fit value of N _ H2 and the nature of the optical candidate counterpart led us to propose that source 127 could be a new, low--luminosity Seyfert--2 galaxy discovered serendipitously in our field.

gmw_00012305.name: System 1
