Given the fact that the AGN contribution to the overall flux in these objects is lower than 10 , this is a clear lower limit and the AGN itself is radio-loud in both objects.

gmw_00012548.name: BLAST 59
gmw_00012548.overview: BLAST 59 is a high-redshift infrared galaxy [arXiv:0910.3642]. It has an estimated photometric redshift of 2.29, though a spectroscopic redshift of 1.097 is also possible, and identifying its correct radio counterpart remains extremely difficult due to multiple candidates [arXiv:0910.3642].

sources.summary: BLAST 59 has a difficult identification among multiple radio counterparts; the preferred GOODS-MUSIC source has z phot 2.29, with likely blended far-infrared flux.

quotes.quote: BLAST 59: z_ est = 2.29 (or z_ spec = 1.097 ) Stepping down by a factor of two in 250 m brightness, the third brightest BLAST source has a flux density of S_ 250 75 mJy, more typical of what is expected in a field of this size given the source counts (Devlin et al. 2009). However, for this object, selection of the correct identification is extremely difficult. The BLAST centroid lies roughly equidistant between 3 alternative radio counterparts. The statistically preferred radio ID for this source lies to the east of the 250 m position, but gains additional support from the 500 m position. Statistically, the association seems reasonably secure.

gmw_00012549.name: BLAST 104
gmw_00012549.overview: BLAST 104 is a red galaxy located at a spectroscopic redshift of z = 0.547 [arXiv:0910.3642]. It has nearby companion galaxies, including an object to the east-northeast situated within 28 kiloparsecs of the main source [arXiv:0910.3642].

sources.summary: BLAST 104 is reinforced by 350 and 500 m detections and identified with a red z=0.547 galaxy, with nearby companions also noted.

quotes.quote: BLAST 104: z_ spec = 0.547 This 250 m source has detections at 350 m and 500 m which reinforce the BLAST position. The VLA identification is centred on a red galaxy with a spectroscopic redshift of z = 0.547 . The closest companion seen in Fig. 7 lies at z_ est = 1.44 , but the object ENE of the BLAST position has z_ est = 0.54 and lies within 28 kpc of the BLAST source at this redshift (although it has no radio detection, so presumably is contributing less to the far-infrared flux density). The next nearest radio counterpart lies 15.7 arcsec north-east of the BLAST position, and thus just outside our adopted search radius. This object has a spectroscopic redshift z = 2.578 .

gmw_00012550.name: BLAST 109
gmw_00012550.overview: BLAST 109 is a low-redshift, edge-on disc galaxy [arXiv:0910.3642]. It is identified as a 250-micrometer source that lies at a moderate redshift of z = 0.124 [arXiv:0910.3642].

sources.summary: BLAST 109 is a moderate-redshift 250 m source; the closer lower-P radio counterpart ties it to a low-redshift edge-on disc galaxy.

quotes.quote: BLAST 109: z_ spec = 0.124 This 250 m source is not found in the BLAST catalogues at longer wavelengths, suggesting it lies at only moderate redshift. There are two formally-significant radio counterparts, but one lies close to our search boundary at d = 15 arcsec. We adopt the closer radio counterpart, with the lower value of P as the most likely identification. This ties the BLAST source to a low-redshift, edge-on disc galaxy.

gmw_00012551.name: BLAST 158
gmw_00012551.overview: BLAST 158 is a high-redshift galaxy with an estimated redshift of 1.85 [arXiv:0910.3642]. At optical wavelengths, the galaxy exhibits a complex, faint morphology, but it is bright in the infrared [arXiv:0910.3642].

sources.summary: BLAST 158 has secure longer-wavelength BLAST counterparts, a faint unique radio ID, z_est 1.85 photometry, LABOCA support, and complex faint optical morphology.

quotes.quote: BLAST 158: z_ est = 1.85 Secure 350 m and 500 m counterparts to the 250 m source confirm its position as just off the southern edge of the ISAAC K band mosaic. The radio ID is faint, but unique and reasonably secure. As with source 66, the available ACS optical and Spitzer IRAC photometry are sufficient to provide a reasonably solid estimated redshift for the associated galaxy, at z_ est = 1.85 , and the high-redshift nature of this source receives support from its detection by LABOCA at 870 m . In Fig. 7 the galaxy looks like an extremely complex faint system at optical wavelengths, but it is bright at IRAC wavelengths.

gmw_00012552.name: BLAST 257
gmw_00012552.overview: BLAST 257 is a moderate-redshift infrared galaxy detected at 250 micrometers [arXiv:0910.3642]. It is associated with two radio identifications at similar redshifts, which likely both contribute to its observed flux density [arXiv:0910.3642].

sources.summary: BLAST 257 is a moderate-redshift source with two radio identifications at similar redshift; VLA 585 is adopted as the statistically most likely association.

quotes.quote: BLAST 257: z_ est = 0.689 This 250 m source is not found in the BLAST catalogues at longer wavelengths, suggesting it lies at only moderate redshift. This is the second source in the sample which has two alternative radio identifications associated with galaxies at the same redshift. The preferred VLA counterpart (585) has a COMBO17 redshift z_ phot = 0.689 , while the alternative radio identification (VLA source 852, 19 arcsec distant (so outside our formal search radius) lies in a galaxy with z_ spec = 0.664 . It seems likely that both galaxies contribute to the 250 m flux density, but the precise choice of ID does not affect the final redshift distribution. We adopt the galaxy associated with VLA source 585 as the statistically most likely association.

gmw_00012553.name: BLAST 503
gmw_00012553.overview: BLAST 503 is a high-redshift infrared galaxy with a photometrically constrained redshift near z ~ 2 [arXiv:0910.3642]. Optical and infrared imaging reveals that the galaxy is a complex, multiple interacting system [arXiv:0910.3642].

sources.summary: BLAST 503 has ambiguous redshift alternatives, but the statistically preferred VLA 132 counterpart appears as a complex interacting system photometrically constrained near z 2.

quotes.quote: BLAST 503: z_ est = 1.96 (or z_ spec = 0.241 ) This source is not found in the BLAST catalogues at longer wavelengths, which would suggests it lies at only moderate redshift. However, while there is a possible radio counterpart with a spectroscopic redshift z= 0.241 (VLA 315), the statistically preferred option is VLA 132. The optical to infrared images shown in Fig. 7 appear to reveal a complex multiple interacting system, which again is well constrained by the photometry to lie at z 2 (Fig.7). The 250 m flux density may receive contributions from both sources, but in this case our choice of ID obviously does (strongly) influence the adopted redshift. There is no LABOCA detection to help support a high redshift, but neither does the non-detection strongly exclude it (see Section 6.1).

gmw_00012554.name: BLAST 637
gmw_00012554.overview: BLAST 637 is an interacting galaxy pair located at a spectroscopic redshift of z = 0.279 [arXiv:0910.3642]. The system's far-infrared emission is associated with the more luminous galaxy of the pair [arXiv:0910.3642].

sources.summary: BLAST 637 is a moderate-redshift interacting pair; both radio counterparts have z=0.279, but the far-infrared position favors the more luminous galaxy.

quotes.quote: BLAST 637: z_ spec = 0.279 This 250 m source is not found in the BLAST catalogues at longer wavelengths, suggesting it lies at only moderate redshift. There are two alternative formally-significant radio counterparts, and we select the closer and marginally more significant VLA 110 in favour of VLA 74. Both radio sources are associated with galaxies with a spectroscopic redshift z = 0.279 , and Fig. 7 indicates that this is another interacting galaxy pair. However, unlike the situation for BLAST 6, the far-infrared position does not lie between the two objects, and favours association with the more luminous galaxy. Clearly the choice of galaxy counterpart does not influence the adopted redshift.

gmw_00012555.name: BLAST 654
gmw_00012555.overview: BLAST 654 is a high-redshift candidate galaxy [arXiv:0910.3642]. It lacks a radio detection and has a potential counterpart identified as GOODS MUSIC 2977 at an estimated redshift of 2.62 [arXiv:0910.3642].

sources.summary: BLAST 654 is a high-redshift candidate lacking radio detection; modeling favors GOODS MUSIC 2977 at z_est 2.62, though later evidence makes it uncertain.

quotes.quote: BLAST 654: z_ est = 2.62 This source has no catalogued 350 m source, but is detected at 500 m only 4 arcsec distant from the 250 m position. This 500 m detection, combined with the lack of any radio detection (no counterparts within 30 arcsec), suggest the source lies at high redshift. We therefore fitted galaxy models to the optical-infrared photometry of all objects in the ISAAC K band image within 15 arcsec of the 250 m position. This search produced seven potential counterparts. Only one of these lies at z > 1.5 , and it is the second closest to the 250 m position (4.1 arcsec).

gmw_00012556.name: BLAST 983
gmw_00012556.overview: BLAST 983 is a dusty galaxy located at a redshift of z = 0.366 [arXiv:0910.3642]. It has a nearby, less massive red companion galaxy situated only 3 arcseconds away at a similar redshift of z = 0.368 [arXiv:0910.3642].

sources.summary: BLAST 983 has a formally secure but offset radio identification with a dusty z=0.366 galaxy and a nearby less massive red companion.

quotes.quote: BLAST 983: z_ spec = 0.366 This 250 m source is not found in the BLAST catalogues at longer wavelengths, suggesting it lies at only moderate redshift. The radio identication is formally secure with P < 0.1 . It lies rather far from the 250 m position, almost at our limiting search radius, but this is not unreasonable given that it is the second least significant source in our sample. The resulting galaxy identification at z = 0.366 can clearly be seen to be dusty in Fig. 7. Only 3 arcsec distant is another less massive red galaxy at essentially the same redshift ( z = 0.368 ).

gmw_00012557.name: rD
gmw_00012557.overview: The object rD is a faint high-redshift galaxy candidate detected in both IRAC channels [arXiv:1308.6280]. It is classified as a dropout candidate that is undetected in filters bluer than f625w, yielding an estimated photometric redshift of approximately 5 [arXiv:1308.6280].

sources.summary: The paper identifies rD as a faint IRAC-detected dropout candidate, undetected blueward of f625w, with z_phot around 5 inferred from f775w and redder detections.

quotes.quote: We analyse these images and find that three of our candidates (ID2, ID3 and rD) are detected in both IRAC channels, even if they are very faint.

The candidate rD is a dropout in the band: it is not detected in the filter bluer than f625w, in which is detected at the 3 limit, and it has detection >5 in the f775w and redder filters. For this candidate we get z _ phot 5 , in agreement with the >5 detection in f775w and with the fainter detection in the f625w filter.

gmw_00012558.name: J0147+4630
gmw_00012558.overview: J0147+4630 is a quadruple lensed quasar system discovered in Pan-STARRS1 survey data [arXiv:2209.14320]. The quasar is located at a spectroscopic redshift of 2.341, while its deflector galaxy lies at a redshift of 0.678 [arXiv:2209.14320].

sources.summary: The paper treats J0147+4630 as a quadruple lensed quasar system and provides an independent deflector velocity-dispersion measurement consistent with the previous value.

quotes.quote: J0147+4630 This quadruple lensed quasar system was coincidentally discovered J0147_discovery_2017 in the Panoramic Survey Telescope and Rapid Response System Pan-STARRS1; Pan-STARRS1_survey survey data. The spectroscopic redshift of the quasar is 2.341 0.001 J0147_quasar_z_Lee_2017 . The redshift of the deflector is also measured, \ = 0.678 0.001 , along with the velocity dispersion, 313 14 , by J0147_lens_z_veldis_Goicoechea . In this paper, we provide an independent measurement of the velocity dispersion of the deflector using a larger wavelength range than the one used in the previous measurement.

For the deflector in J0147+4630, the previous measurement is 313 14 \ using a 0.5 width slit under subarcsecond seeing conditions J0147_lens_z_veldis_Goicoechea . This result is within the uncertainty of our measurement of 283 30 12 \ (see Table table:veldis_measurements ) .

gmw_00012559.name: B0445+123
gmw_00012559.overview: B0445+123 is a radio-loud active galactic nucleus that is gravitationally lensed into a double-imaged system [arXiv:2209.14320]. The deflector galaxy has a redshift of 0.5583 [arXiv:2209.14320], and its stellar velocity dispersion has been measured [arXiv:2209.14320].

sources.summary: The paper describes B0445+123 as a radio-loud double-imaged AGN lens with known deflector redshift and adds a first stellar velocity-dispersion measurement.

quotes.quote: B0445+123 This radio-loud double-imaged AGN was discovered by B0445_discovery_paper in the Cosmic Lens All-Sky Survey CLASS; CLASS_survey,CLASS_survey_2 . Radio observations have measured the separation between the lensed images to be 1.32 . Although the AGN redshift is yet to be measured, spectroscopic observations have found that the deflector redshift is \ = 0.5583 0.0003 McKean_keck_spec_2004 .

Made the first measurements of the stellar velocity dispersions for six of the eight lensing galaxies in our sample. The new velocity dispersion measurements were made for the deflectors in B0445+123, B0631+519, J0659+1629, J0818-2613, J0924+0219 and J1817+2729 systems.

gmw_00012560.name: B0631+519
gmw_00012560.overview: B0631+519 is a radio-loud double-imaged gravitational lens system [arXiv:2209.14320]. It features two compact lensed images separated by approximately 1.16 arcseconds, with two galaxies located along the line of sight of the background quasar [arXiv:2209.14320].

sources.summary: The paper describes B0631+519 as a radio-loud double-imaged lens with two line-of-sight galaxies and adds a first velocity-dispersion measurement for the main deflector.

quotes.quote: B0631+519 This is another radio-loud double-imaged lens system discovered by CLASS B0631_discover_paper . Observations at different radio wavelengths have revealed two compact lensed images separated by around 1.16 . HST imaging of the system has shown that there are two galaxies along the line of sight of the quasar. The main lensing galaxy is at redshift, \ = 0.6196 0.0004 and the other one is at redshift 0.0896 0.0001 McKean_keck_spec_2004 .

Made the first measurements of the stellar velocity dispersions for six of the eight lensing galaxies in our sample. The new velocity dispersion measurements were made for the deflectors in B0445+123, B0631+519, J0659+1629, J0818-2613, J0924+0219 and J1817+2729 systems.

gmw_00012561.name: J0659+1629
gmw_00012561.overview: J0659+1629 is a quadruply lensed quasar system with a source redshift of 3.083 [arXiv:2209.14320]. The system's deflector galaxy is located at a redshift of 0.766, and its stellar velocity dispersion has been directly measured [arXiv:2209.14320].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0659+1629 as a quadruply lensed quasar with known source and deflector redshifts, adding a new deflector velocity dispersion.

quotes.quote: J0659+1629 This quadruply-lensed quasar was first identified as a lens candidate by Delchambre_2019 using Gaia data release 2 Gaia_DR2 . This detection was confirmed by Stern_2021 with measured source redshift, \ =3.083 and deflector redshift, \ =0.766 . The system was also independently discovered by Lemon_2022 . Lens modeling of the system is conducted by Schmidt_2022 using HST imaging.

Made the first measurements of the stellar velocity dispersions for six of the eight lensing galaxies in our sample. The new velocity dispersion measurements were made for the deflectors in B0445+123, B0631+519, J0659+1629, J0818-2613, J0924+0219 and J1817+2729 systems.

gmw_00012562.name: J1433+6007
gmw_00012562.overview: J1433+6007 is a quadruply lensed system detected in the SDSS photometric catalog [arXiv:2209.14320]. The system features a background source at a redshift of 2.737 and a foreground deflector galaxy at a redshift of 0.407 with a measured velocity dispersion of 261 km/s [arXiv:2209.14320].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1433+6007 as an SDSS quad and presents a higher-S/N, larger-wavelength deflector velocity-dispersion measurement consistent with the prior value.

quotes.quote: J1433+6007 This quad was detected in the SDSS data release 12 photometric catalog SDSS_DR_11&12 . The source and deflector redshift, and deflector velocity dispersion were first measured by Agnello_2018_J1433 . The reported redshifts of the source and the deflector are, \ = 2.737 0.003 and \ = 0.407 0.002 , respectively. The measured velocity dispersion is 216 55 \ using a 1 \ aperture Agnello_2018_J1433 . However, this measurement only used the Ca K line and the spectrum was significantly contaminated by quasar light.
Our measured velocity dispersion for this deflector is 261 6 7 \ (see Table table:veldis_measurements ) , which is higher but still within the uncertainty of the previous measurement.

gmw_00012563.name: J1817+2729
gmw_00012563.overview: J1817+2729 is a confirmed quadruple gravitational lens system [arXiv:2209.14320]. The system consists of a background lensed source at a redshift of 3.07 and a foreground deflector galaxy [arXiv:2209.14320].

sources.summary: The paper describes J1817+2729 as a confirmed quad lens and reports the deflector redshift and velocity dispersion, including a previously unknown redshift.

quotes.quote: J1817+2729 This quad was first identified as a lens candidate by Delchambre_2019 using Gaia data release 2 and then independently confirmed with measured source redshift, \ =3.07 , by Lemon_2019, Stern_2021 . Detailed mass modeling has been conducted by Rusu_2018_J1817_modeling using SUBARU/FOCAS imaging and by Schmidt_2022 using HST imaging . In this paper, we present the deflector's redshift and velocity dispersion.

In this paper we have : Developed a forward modeling technique and used it to extract 1D spectra of eight lensing galaxies, even in the presence of much brighter lensed quasar images. Used the extracted spectra to measure the deflector redshifts in all of our targets, including two that have never been measured before, in the J0818-2613 and J1817+2729 systems. For the remainder of the targets, our measured redshifts agree with those in the literature to within 1 . Made the first measurements of the stellar velocity dispersions for six of the eight lensing galaxies in our sample.

gmw_00012564.name: VGS 38a
gmw_00012564.overview: VGS 38a is a void dwarf galaxy that is clearly interacting with a larger companion located approximately 40 kiloparsecs away [arXiv:1410.5821]. Along with VGS 38b, it is part of a linear configuration with a third, larger void galaxy [arXiv:1410.5821].

sources.summary: The paper notes VGS 38a falls furthest from a closed-box model, is clearly interacting with a larger companion, and appears in a linear void-galaxy configuration without distinct metallicity behavior.

quotes.quote: We also note that two of these void dwarf galaxies are not isolated, and it is in fact one of these, VGS 38a, that falls furthest from the closed box model. This galaxy is very clearly interacting with a larger nearby (D 40 kpc) companion (Figure fig:ha ).

This does not rule out the possibility of filaments within our sample Beygu2013 , and in fact VGS 38a and VGS 38b both appear in a linear configuration with a third, larger void galaxy (VGS 38). These two dwarf galaxies, however, also appear indistinguishable from the other void dwarf galaxies and the isolated dwarf galaxy control sample.

gmw_00012565.name: VGS 39a
gmw_00012565.overview: VGS 39a is a dwarf galaxy with an estimated stellar mass of 40 million solar masses [arXiv:1410.5821]. It exhibits low metallicity for its estimated luminosity and displays a relatively large equivalent width in its oxygen emission lines [arXiv:1410.5821].

sources.summary: The paper flags VGS 39a as low-metallicity for its estimated luminosity, but strong [O III] line contamination changes the photometric estimate and makes it less of an outlier.

quotes.quote: We note that one of the VGS dwarfs, VGS 39a, exhibits a low metallicity for its estimated M _B , particularly in the M91 and KK04 diagnostics, and even with the temperature-sensitive method. However, as this galaxy also exhibits a relatively large equivalent width in the [ O  ] 5007   line (EW _ 5007 220 ), we estimate that this line emission contributes 0.2 magnitudes to the measured g-band filter (which extends to 5500 ) and invalidates our assumption of no strong emission lines in the conversion to B-band. Using the SDSS photometry directly (see below) we estimate a stellar mass of 4 10 ^7 M _ , more consistent with M _B 14 mag, making VGS 39a less of an outlier in these plots.

gmw_00012566.name: ESO 079-G003
gmw_00012566.overview: ESO 079-G003 is a galaxy featuring centrally peaked radio emission that spans nearly its entire optical disk [arXiv:2403.05709]. This emission is thicker on one side, displaying several clumps, plumes, and extraplanar emission that suggests the presence of a halo component [arXiv:2403.05709].

sources.summary: The paper finds ESO 079-G003 has centrally peaked radio emission over most of the disk, clumps and plumes, and extraplanar emission needing a second component, though the halo detection is preliminary.

quotes.quote: ESO 079-G003: The contour map of this galaxy shows that the radio emission is centrally peaked. It covers almost all the optical disk, thicker on one side and with several clumps and plumes along its extension. We determined that a Gaussian function offered a good fit for the disk and found the presence of extraplanar emission that required the addition of a second component to achieve an accurate fit. However, the disk and halo scale heights resulted in smaller values than the angular resolution, making this a preliminary detection and indicating the need for even higher-resolution data.

gmw_00012567.name: ESO 163-G011
gmw_00012567.overview: ESO 163-G011 is a galaxy paired with its companion, ESO 163-G010 [arXiv:2403.05709]. It exhibits an asymmetric emission profile that is stronger on the side of the disk facing its companion, which is interpreted as a preliminary radio halo [arXiv:2403.05709].

sources.summary: The paper describes ESO 163-G011 as paired with ESO 163-G010 and asymmetric toward its companion; a second profile component is interpreted as a preliminary radio halo.

quotes.quote: ESO 163-G011: This galaxy is paired with ESO 163-G010. The contour map and profile exhibit an evident asymmetry; the left side of the profile displays greater emission, which corresponds to the side of the disk closer to its companion galaxy. For this reason, it was necessary to fit the profile with a two-component function: an exponential for modeling the disk and a second component to account for the asymmetric emission, which we interpret as a radio halo. However, the angular value of the average halo scale height was smaller than the angular resolution, making it another preliminary detection.

gmw_00012568.name: IC 3908
gmw_00012568.overview: The galaxy IC 3908 exhibits radio emission that extends far beyond its galactic disk on both sides, creating a somewhat rectangular shape [arXiv:2403.05709]. To model this structure, researchers utilized a Gaussian disk combined with a second component to account for the extraplanar emission [arXiv:2403.05709].

sources.summary: The paper adjusts IC 3908's position angle, finds radio contours extending beyond both sides of the disk, and fits a Gaussian disk plus extraplanar component as a preliminary halo.

quotes.quote: IC 3908: For this galaxy, we had to adjust the position angle provided by HYPERLEDA by a small margin. We observed that the major axis was not horizontal when rotating, indicating the need for a slightly different angle. Upon examining the radio contours, we observed that the galaxy exhibits emission extending far beyond the galactic disk on both sides, forming a somewhat rectangular shape. To obtain the best fit for the z profile, we utilized a Gaussian disk as well as a second component to account for the extraplanar emission. However, the halo scale height on the right side of the profile resulted in the same order as the angular resolution, making it a preliminary detection.

gmw_00012569.name: NGC 2221
gmw_00012569.overview: NGC 2221 is a dwarf irregular galaxy that forms an interacting pair with NGC 2222 [arXiv:2403.05709]. Despite its small angular size and low inclination angle, the galaxy exhibits extraplanar emission that requires a two-component profile fit [arXiv:2403.05709].

sources.summary: The paper classifies NGC 2221 as a dwarf irregular interacting with NGC 2222 and finds prominent extraplanar emission requiring a two-component fit, needing higher-resolution confirmation.

quotes.quote: NGC 2221: This is a dwarf irregular galaxy, part of an interacting pair together with NGC 2222. Some small sources in the vicinity had to be removed before the analysis. Despite being one of the sample galaxies with a smaller angular size and a low inclination angle, this galaxy exhibits prominent extraplanar emission, which requires fitting its profile using a two-component function. An exponential disk provided the best fit, but the disk and halo scale heights were found to be smaller than the angular resolution. Thus, higher-resolution data are needed to confirm these results.

gmw_00012570.name: NGC 1406
gmw_00012570.overview: NGC 1406 features a strong nuclear source and exhibits extraplanar radio emission that spans almost its entire optical disk [arXiv:2403.05709]. This radio emission is more intense near the central region on one side of the galactic disk, where a radio halo is detected with a revised scale height of 1.06 kpc [arXiv:2403.05709].

sources.summary: The paper removes NGC 1406's strong nuclear source, finds extraplanar radio emission over most of the optical disk, and revises the previously detected halo scale height.

quotes.quote: NGC 1406: The strong nuclear source present in this galaxy had to be removed before the analysis. The radio contour map shows evidence of extraplanar emission, more intense at the central part on one side of the galactic disk. The emission covers almost the entire optical disk in the radial direction. The best fit resulted from using a Gaussian disk and a component to account for the halo. As the left halo scale height resulted in the same order as the disk scale height, we conclude that the halo is detected only on the right side.
The radio halo of this galaxy was detected for the first time by , who found an average halo scale height of 1.66 kpc using 43'' resolution VLA observations. We correct this value to 1.06 0.08 kpc .

gmw_00012571.name: NGC 1421
gmw_00012571.overview: NGC 1421 is a spiral galaxy with a relatively low inclination that allows its spiral arms and disk structure to be clearly seen [arXiv:2403.05709]. It exhibits asymmetric extraplanar emission, suggesting the presence of a radio halo that is masked on one side of the galactic plane but reliably detected on the other [arXiv:2403.05709].

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 1421's low inclination reveals spiral structure, with asymmetric extraplanar emission requiring a two-component fit and a halo masked on one side but reliable on the other.

quotes.quote: NGC 1421: This galaxy has a relatively low inclination, allowing for clear visibility of its spiral arms and disk structure around the central source. We observed evidence of extraplanar emission in both the z profile and contour map after removing nearby sources. To accurately describe the profile, a two-component function with a Gaussian disk was necessary, but due to the high asymmetry of the profile, obtaining a good fit was challenging. The resulting disk scale height was found to be large, and the left halo scale height was near the size of the telescope beam, resulting in a negligible halo contribution to the flux density on that side.

This suggests the possible presence of a radio halo on this side of the galactic plane, but it is masked by the disk emission. On the other side of the disk, the halo emission is more reliably detected, resulting in a highly asymmetric halo.

gmw_00012572.name: ESO 428-G028
gmw_00012572.overview: ESO 428-G028 is a galaxy featuring centrally peaked radio emission that extends across its entire disk [arXiv:2403.05709]. Although the emission appears thick and displays some plumes extending from the galactic plane, its vertical profile is adequately described by a single-component exponential function [arXiv:2403.05709].

sources.summary: The paper uses ESO 428-G028 as a no-halo comparison: centrally peaked radio emission extends across the disk, but a one-component exponential adequately fits its apparent thickness and plumes.

quotes.quote: As an example, we show in Fig. figure:galaxies-example the radio contour map over the optical Digital Sky Survey (DSS) image, the z profile and the fit for two galaxies: ESO 209-G009, one of the galaxies for which we detected a radio halo for the first time, and ESO 428-G028, for which we did not detect a radio halo.

ESO 428-G028: The radio emission of this galaxy is centrally peaked and extends over the entire disk. Despite its apparent thickness in the z direction, with some plumes extending from the galactic plane, a one-component exponential function adequately described the z profile.

gmw_00012573.name: IC 4595
gmw_00012573.overview: The radio emission of the galaxy IC 4595 extends to the outermost parts of its optical disk [arXiv:2403.05709]. Its structure is best fit by an exponential disk model [arXiv:2403.05709].

sources.summary: The paper reports IC 4595's radio emission reaches the outer optical disk, required removal of a bright nearby source, and was best fit with an exponential disk.

quotes.quote: IC 4595: The radio emission of this galaxy extends to the outermost parts of the optical disk. Before analysis, a very bright, nearby source had to be removed to ensure accurate results. The most optimal fit for this particular galaxy was achieved using an exponential disk.

gmw_00012574.name: NGC 134
gmw_00012574.overview: NGC 134 is a galaxy with a relatively low inclination angle that allows its disk structure to be clearly visible [arXiv:2403.05709]. Its radio emission closely follows the optical disk, and a Gaussian disk fit yields an unusually large scale height [arXiv:2403.05709].

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 134's low-inclination disk is clearly visible; radio emission follows the optical disk without extraplanar signs, and a Gaussian disk fit yields an unusually large scale height.

quotes.quote: NGC 134: This galaxy has a relatively low inclination angle that allows the structure of its disk to be clearly visible. The radio contour map analysis indicates that the emission closely follows the optical disk without a central peak and no detectable signs of extraplanar emission. It is possible that, if present, any extraplanar emission is masked by the strong disk emission due to the galaxy's low inclination angle. After removing nearby sources, we were able to obtain the most optimal fit for this galaxy using a Gaussian disk without requiring a second component to describe the z profile. Interestingly, the resulting disk scale height was significantly larger than that of the rest of the sample, which may be a consequence of the low inclination angle of the galaxy.

gmw_00012575.name: NGC 2613
gmw_00012575.overview: NGC 2613 is a galaxy with a relatively low inclination disk that exhibits a ring-shaped radio emission following its optical disk [arXiv:2403.05709]. Recent studies suggest the galaxy may host an obscured active galactic nucleus or starburst activity, which could be associated with extraplanar emission [arXiv:2403.05709].

sources.summary: The paper notes possible AGN/starburst-related extraplanar features previously reported for NGC 2613, but finds its ringlike radio emission follows the optical disk and is best fit without a halo.

quotes.quote: NGC 2613: recently found evidence for a possible AGN in this galaxy. studied this galaxy in X-rays and suggested the presence of an obscured AGN and a bubble that may correspond to extraplanar emission. They suggest that it could be produced by a starburst or an AGN in case of being an outflow.

The disk of this galaxy has a relatively low inclination angle, allowing its structure to be clearly visible. Its ring shape shows two regions brighter than the rest and even brighter than the emission from the galaxy center. It can be seen that the radio emission follows the optical disk, with no evidence of extraplanar emission. The high asymmetry and the low inclination of the disk made this galaxy's profile very difficult to fit. After the removal of surrounding sources and once obtained the profile, we found the best fit using a single Gaussian function.

gmw_00012576.name: NGC 2706
gmw_00012576.overview: NGC 2706 is a galaxy with a small angular size and a relatively low inclination angle [arXiv:2403.05709]. This low inclination causes its optical disk to appear thick, while its radio emission extends through the galactic disk and is broad in the z direction [arXiv:2403.05709].

sources.summary: The paper says NGC 2706 is small and low-inclination with a thick optical disk; radio emission extends through the disk but a single component fits the z profile.

quotes.quote: NGC 2706: This galaxy is one of the sample galaxies with a smaller angular size and has a relatively low inclination angle, which causes the optical disk to be thick. The radio emission extends through the galactic disk and also appears broad in z direction. However, we determined that a single-component function was enough to accurately describe the shape of the z profile.

gmw_00012577.name: NGC 3263
gmw_00012577.overview: NGC 3263 is an interacting galaxy located within the NGC 3256 group [arXiv:2403.05709]. It is interacting with the radio-quiet galaxy NGC 3262 and features a tidal tail at one end of its disk [arXiv:2403.05709].

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 3263 as interacting with radio-quiet NGC 3262 in the NGC 3256 group, with a tidal tail but no required second profile component.

quotes.quote: NGC 3263: This galaxy is interacting with the radio-quiet galaxy NGC 3262, and is a member of the NGC 3256 group. It shows a tidal tail located at one end of the disk. When constructing the z profile, it was challenging to identify an area that did not include any of the emission from the tail. However, we were able to select an appropriate region where the contribution from the tail was negligible, and the z profile analysis could be performed. Despite the presence of the tidal tail and the galaxy's low inclination, we determined that a single-component function was enough to accurately fit the profile.

gmw_00012578.name: NGC 3717
gmw_00012578.overview: NGC 3717 is a galaxy paired with IC 2913 [arXiv:2403.05709]. Its radio emission is concentrated in the central region and follows the optical disk, with no signs of a radio halo [arXiv:2403.05709].

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 3717, paired with IC 2913, has centrally concentrated radio emission following the optical disk; after source removal, a single exponential fit confirms no halo.

quotes.quote: NGC 3717: The radio emission in this galaxy, paired with IC 2913, is primarily concentrated in the central region, with no discernible signs of extraplanar emission as it follows the optical disk. After removing the strong nuclear source and nearby sources, we fit the z profile and determined that a single exponential function provided the best fit. Our results agree with , who also studied this galaxy and did not find a radio halo.

gmw_00012579.name: NGC 4835
gmw_00012579.overview: NGC 4835 is a galaxy with a low inclination [arXiv:2403.05709]. It shows signs of apparent extraplanar emission in both its vertical profile and its radio contour map [arXiv:2403.05709].

sources.summary: The paper compares NGC 4835 to NGC 4666, noting possible extraplanar signs but concluding low inclination prevents component separation and a single exponential adequately fits the profile.

quotes.quote: NGC 4835: This galaxy is similar to NGC 4666, in that its inclination is low, but it still shows signs of apparent extraplanar emission from both the z profile and the radio contour map. However, a single-component exponential function was enough to accurately describe the profile. A possible explanation is that the low inclination of the galaxy does not allow one to differentiate the two components from each other if a radio halo is indeed present.

gmw_00012580.name: UGCA 150
gmw_00012580.overview: The radio emission of the galaxy UGCA 150 closely follows its optical disk [arXiv:2403.05709]. Although a small bilateral excess is observed when fitting its profile, a single Gaussian function provides the best fit for the emission [arXiv:2403.05709].

sources.summary: The paper finds UGCA 150's radio emission follows the optical disk, with small bilateral excesses too weak for a second component, so a single Gaussian gives the best fit.

quotes.quote: UGCA 150: The radio emission of this galaxy follows the optical disk, as can be seen from the radio contour map. However, we observed a small excess on both sides when attempting to fit a single-component function to the profile. Nevertheless, the observed excess is not significant enough to include a second component. Consequently, the best fit was achieved using a single Gaussian function.

gmw_00012581.name: UGCA 394
gmw_00012581.overview: The nuclear emission of the galaxy UGCA 394 is strong [arXiv:2403.05709]. Its radio emission generally follows the optical disk, though a small excess is present near the center and on the right side of the profile [arXiv:2403.05709].

sources.summary: The paper removes UGCA 394's strong nuclear emission and finds radio emission mostly follows the disk; a small central/right-side excess does not require a second component.

quotes.quote: UGCA 394: The nuclear emission of this galaxy is strong and had to be removed before the analysis. The radio emission follows the optical disk, except near the galaxy center, where is a small excess. This emission, however, is not enough to add a second component and a single exponential function was found to adequately fit the profile. The profile clearly displays a deviation from the exponential fit on the right side, indicating the presence of the excess emission mentioned earlier.

gmw_00012582.name: BCG
gmw_00012582.overview: The BCG is the dominant galaxy of the Abell 1995 cluster, residing 0.8 magnitudes brighter than the second brightest member [arXiv:1210.5927]. It is located in the GV1 group on the border with GV2, centering an optical substructure and peaking in ROSAT-HRI X-ray emission [arXiv:1210.5927].

sources.summary: The paper identifies the Abell 1995 BCG as the dominant ID 61 galaxy, links it to ROSAT X-ray emission, and centers one optical substructure on it.

quotes.quote: A1995 hosts a dominant galaxy (ID. 61, R=17.78 , hereafter BCG) 0.8 mag brighter than the second brightest member galaxy (ID. 76). The X-ray ROSAT-HRI emission peaks on the BCG (e.g., Patel et al. ).
The BCG resides in the GV1 group, but it lies on the border with GV2.
We found a NE-SW elongated structure with two significant peaks, one of them (the NE one) centered on the BCG (Fig. figk2z ).

gmw_00012583.name: J123318+103542
gmw_00012583.overview: J123318+103542 is a galaxy featuring a galactic disk with a modeled velocity scale height of 10 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1611.04579]. Sightline absorption velocities associated with the galaxy fall short of the disk's projected circular velocity, which can be modeled by radial inflow in the disk plane [arXiv:1611.04579].

sources.summary: For J123318+103542, the sightline absorption falls short of disk rotation; the paper models it with radial inflow but treats the required large inflow speed skeptically.

quotes.quote: Toward the sightline associated with 123318+103542, the absorption velocities do not reach the projected circular velocity of the galactic disk. In the center panels of Figure fig:model1 , we successfully fit these two systems by introducing radial infall in the disk plane. The observed line-of-sight velocities are the projection of the total velocity vector produced by the addition of rotation and inflow in the disk plane. Whether the infall boosts or cancels the projected circular velocity depends on geometry.

This solution has a reasonable disk of thickness H_ eff = 5 kpc and H_ eff = 2 kpc, with the velocity scale height h_v = 10 kpc in the models for 084725+254104 and 123318+103542 respectively. The corresponding inflow speeds are v_R = -140 \ and v_R = -1120 \ in the two models.

One sightline, quasar 123317+103538 associated with galaxy 123318+103542, required a much larger inflow velocity to cancel the tangential velocity. We remain skeptical of inflow velocities as large as 1000 ; however, some high velocity clouds have velocities this large relative to the Local Standard of Rest Wakker1997 .

gmw_00012584.overview: The galaxy 160951+353838 is an interacting merger that exhibits an irregular rotation curve [arXiv:1611.04579]. It features a tail-like structure extending toward the northeast and displays strong Mg II absorption associated with the merger [arXiv:1611.04579].

sources.summary: The paper describes 160951+353838 as an interacting merger with a northeastern tail-like structure, irregular rotation, and the sample's strongest Mg II absorption tied to the merger.

quotes.quote: One galaxy, 160951+353838, shows a tail-like structure toward the northeast side. The irregularity of rotation curve in this region confirms the identification of a merger. The quasar sightline shows the strongest Mg 2 absorption in the sample, and we suggest that this is related directly to the merger.

gmw_00012585.name: NGC 2936
gmw_00012585.overview: NGC 2936 is a peculiar spiral galaxy interacting with the elliptical companion NGC 2937 in the pair Arp 142 [arXiv:1907.02193]. The galaxy features a highly distorted disk with widely distributed CO emission [arXiv:2106.15041], an AGN-ionized central region [arXiv:1907.02193], and a starbursting eastern tidal plume [arXiv:1907.02193, arXiv:2106.15041].

sources.summary: The paper studies NGC 2936 as the peculiar spiral member of Arp 142, finding enhanced star formation, solar-like H II region abundances, AGN-like central ionization, and a starbursting tidal plume.

quotes.quote: In this manuscript, we report the characterization of selected H ii regions along the peculiar galaxy NGC 2936, member of the galaxy pair Arp 142, an E+S interaction, known as ``The Penguin". Using Gemini South spectroscopy we have derived a high enhancement of the global star formation rate SFR = 35.9 M _ yr ^ 1 probably stimulated by the interaction. Star-forming regions on this galaxy display oxygen abundances that are consistent with solar metallicities. The current data set does not allow us to conclude any clear scenario for NGC 2936. Diagnostic diagrams suggest that the central region of NGC 2936 is ionized by AGN activity and the eastern tidal plume in NGC 2936 is experiencing a burst of star formation, which may be triggered by the gas compression due to the interaction event with its elliptical companion galaxy: NGC 2937.

sources.summary: The paper identifies NGC 2936 as Arp 142's spiral galaxy and reports CO spread across its distorted disk, no nuclear concentration, a ring-like channel-map structure, and strongly varying SFE.

quotes.quote: The CO emission in Arp 142 is widely distributed over a highly distorted disk of the spiral galaxy (NGC 2936) without any nuclear concentration, and an off-centric ring-like structure is discovered in channel maps. There is a significant variation of the SFE within Arp 142. The starburst region (Region 1) near the eastern tip of the distorted disk has an SFE more than 0.7 dex higher than that of the CO-peak region (Region 3) and shows a moderate SFE enhancement ( 0.3 dex) compared to the mean of the AMIGA control sample of isolated galaxies Lisenfeld2019 . Only 10 of the molecular gas in Arp 142 is found in the starburst region, whereas the majority of the remaining gas has relatively low SFE as suggested by the result for the main disk (Region 4) which has an SFE 0.43 dex lower than the mean of control sample.

gmw_00012586.name: NGC 2937
gmw_00012586.overview: NGC 2937 is a spheroidal elliptical galaxy that is currently interacting with the spiral galaxy NGC 2936 in the system Arp 142 [arXiv:1907.02193]. It exhibits tidal distortions, including non-concentric inner isophotes and a faint outer surface brightness profile excess [arXiv:1907.02193].

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 2937 as the spheroidal/elliptical companion in Arp 142, showing tidal distortions through non-concentric isophotes, profile excesses, and a plume from NGC 2936 toward it.

quotes.quote: The isophotal analysis shows tidal features on NGC 2937: at inner radii non-concentric (or off-centering) isophotes, and at large radii, a faint excess of the surface brightness profile with respect to de Vaucouleurs law.
The system is composed by the spheroidal NGC 2937 and the spiral galaxy NGC 2936 that displays a complex morphology.

Visual inspection of Hubble Space Telescope (HST) WFC3-UVIS2 (Hubble proposal ID:12812, PI: Levay) imaging shows that NGC 2936 and NGC 2937 are distinguishable and not fully mixed yet (see Figure Figure1 ) , hinting an early stage of the merger (or a going on close encounter). Furthermore, the high spatial resolution of the HST image shows clearly that arc-like or ring-like forms described by earlier authors is actually due to the North-West tidal plume stems from NGC 2936 and bending towards the elliptical member of Arp 142 (NGC 2937).

gmw_00012587.name: UGC 05138
gmw_00012587.overview: UGC 05138 is a small galaxy located at a projected distance of 1.3 arcminutes north of the center of NGC 2936 [arXiv:1907.02193]. Its spectrum displays several emission lines, yielding a radial velocity of 4971 km/s that rules it out as the perturber of the Arp 142 system [arXiv:1907.02193].

sources.summary: The paper checks UGC 05138 as a possible northern perturber of Arp 142, measures a discrepant radial velocity, and rules it out as the disturbing agent.

quotes.quote: However, there is also another possibility, which is a small galaxy north of the system, UGC 05138 NOTES01, at a projected distance of 1.3 arcmin north of the center of NGC 2936. We have placed one of the slits on this galaxy and its spectrum is then shown in Figure. Figure3 , bottom panel. From the several emission-lines, we could obtain a radial velocity for this galaxy of 4971 18 km s ^ 1 , which is 2000 km s ^ 1 different from the Arp 142 system. Therefore we conclude that this galaxy cannot be the disturbing agent that caused the morphological and kinematic changes of NGC 2936.

gmw_00012588.name: Mrk 506
gmw_00012588.overview: Mrk 506 is a Seyfert galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0606476]. Its optical surface-brightness profile is modeled using a nucleus, a bulge, a disk, and a Gaussian ring component [arXiv:astro-ph/0606476].

sources.summary: The paper decomposes Mrk 506 optical surface-brightness profiles with nucleus, bulge, disk, and ring components, deriving structural parameters and total magnitudes.

quotes.quote: We present the results of the UBVR_ C I_ C surface brightness profiles decomposition of the Seyfert galaxies III Zw 2, Mrk 506 and Mrk 509. The profiles were modelled as a sum of a Gaussian, a S ersic law and an exponent. A Ferrers bar and a Gaussian ring were added to the model profiles of III Zw 2 and Mrk 506, respectively. The parameters and the total magnitudes of the structural components were derived.

gmw_00012589.name: COOL J0335 1927
gmw_00012589.overview: COOL J0335 1927 is a lensed quasar at a redshift of z = 3.12 that is split into three images by a foreground galaxy cluster [arXiv:2305.14317]. Multi-epoch observations demonstrate variability in the quasar, and its spectra show several strong intervening absorbers [arXiv:2305.14317].

sources.summary: The paper reports COOL J0335 1927 as the highest-redshift wide-separation lensed quasar known, with three images, intervening absorbers, predicted time delays, and variable light curves.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of , a quasar at z = \ lensed into three images with a maximum separation of 23 3 by a galaxy cluster at z = . To date this is the highest redshift wide-separation lensed quasar known. In addition, shows several strong intervening absorbers visible in the spectra of all three quasar images with varying equivalent width.

We construct a parametric strong gravitational lens model using ground-based imaging, constrained by the redshift and positions of the quasar images as well as the positions of three other multiply-imaged background galaxies. Using our best-fit lens model, we calculate the predicted time delays between the three quasar images to be t _ AB = \ (stat) and t _ AC = \ (stat) days. Folding in systematic uncertainties, the model-predicted time delays are within the ranges 240 < t _ AB < 700 and 300 < t _ AC <-30 . We also present g band photometry from archival DECaLS and Pan-STARRS imaging, and new multi-epoch observations obtained between September 18, 2022 UT and February 22, 2023 UT, which demonstrate significant variability in the quasar and which will eventually enable a measurement of the time delay between the three quasar images. The currently available light curves are consistent with the model-predicted time delays.

gmw_00012590.name: 3-27A
gmw_00012590.overview: The emission lines in the spectra of the galaxy 3-27A exhibit a double-peaked profile [arXiv:1111.6728]. This profile reveals two distinct inner velocity components separated by more than 200 km/s, which correspond to a fast rotating ring that is decoupled from the disk motion [arXiv:1111.6728].

sources.summary: The paper reports double-peaked emission lines in 3-27A and an inner rotation-curve split over 200 km/s, interpreted as a fast rotating ring decoupled from the disk.

quotes.quote: The emission lines of the spectra of 3-27A and 3-78C2 galaxies showed a double peak. The peaks' separation is 5.4 and 3 respectively, clearly indicating two kinematical components, as discussed in Sect. 4.1.
3-27A : two inner velocity components separated by more than 200 km/s arise in the RC of this galaxy. They correspond to the opposite sides of a fast rotating ring decoupled from the disk motion.

gmw_00012591.name: 3-44A1
gmw_00012591.overview: The galaxy 3-44A1 is classified as a Seyfert 1 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1111.6728]. Its spectrum exhibits a broad component in the hydrogen emission line [arXiv:1111.6728].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3-44A1 as a new Seyfert 1 galaxy, with a broad H emission component, fitted broad-line measurements, spectra in the H region, and AGN diagnostic classification.

quotes.quote: The spectra of the galaxies 3-44A1, 3-70A, and 3-96A exhibit a broad component in the H emission line typical of Seyfert I galaxies, which was fitted by adding a second Gaussian function to the fit. The FWHM and the flux of their broad line components are reported in Table broad_line . Figure AGN_spec shows their spectra and our fits in the H region.

Three galaxies, 3-44A1, 3-70A, and 3-96A, show the H with a broad line component (Fig. AGN_spec ), and we classified them as Sy 1. In Fig. diagno they are residing in the AGN region and will be discussed further below.
Three new Sy 1 galaxies were identified, 3-44A1, 3-70A, and 3-96A.

gmw_00012592.name: 3-70A
gmw_00012592.overview: The galaxy 3-70A is classified as an active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1111.6728]. Specifically, it is identified as a Seyfert 1 galaxy based on a broad component in its H-alpha emission line [arXiv:1111.6728].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3-70A as a new Seyfert 1 galaxy, with a broad H emission component, fitted broad-line measurements, spectra in the H region, and AGN diagnostic classification.

quotes.quote: The spectra of the galaxies 3-44A1, 3-70A, and 3-96A exhibit a broad component in the H emission line typical of Seyfert I galaxies, which was fitted by adding a second Gaussian function to the fit. The FWHM and the flux of their broad line components are reported in Table broad_line . Figure AGN_spec shows their spectra and our fits in the H region.

Three galaxies, 3-44A1, 3-70A, and 3-96A, show the H with a broad line component (Fig. AGN_spec ), and we classified them as Sy 1. In Fig. diagno they are residing in the AGN region and will be discussed further below.
Three new Sy 1 galaxies were identified, 3-44A1, 3-70A, and 3-96A.

gmw_00012593.name: 3-96A
gmw_00012593.overview: The galaxy 3-96A is classified as a Seyfert 1 active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1111.6728] that exhibits a broad H-alpha emission line [arXiv:1111.6728, arXiv:astro-ph/0609569]. It is a distant, warm infrared source with a high star formation rate [arXiv:astro-ph/0609569].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3-96A as a new Seyfert 1 galaxy, with a broad H emission component, fitted broad-line measurements, spectra in the H region, and AGN diagnostic classification.

quotes.quote: The spectra of the galaxies 3-44A1, 3-70A, and 3-96A exhibit a broad component in the H emission line typical of Seyfert I galaxies, which was fitted by adding a second Gaussian function to the fit. The FWHM and the flux of their broad line components are reported in Table broad_line . Figure AGN_spec shows their spectra and our fits in the H region.

Three galaxies, 3-44A1, 3-70A, and 3-96A, show the H with a broad line component (Fig. AGN_spec ), and we classified them as Sy 1. In Fig. diagno they are residing in the AGN region and will be discussed further below.
Three new Sy 1 galaxies were identified, 3-44A1, 3-70A, and 3-96A.

sources.summary: The paper describes 3-96A as a broad H emission-line galaxy with AGN optical properties, a distant warm high-SFR 60 m source, and an AGN source removed from the 15 m LF analysis.

quotes.quote: One ULIRG, 3-53A, and two broad H emission line galaxies with AGN optical properties (i.e., 3-70A and 3-96A, see Bettoni et al. (2006) for more details), are also included in the complete subsample.
The more luminous 60 m sources, i.e., those with higher SFRs, correspond to the more distant systems, 3-96 and 3-53, two warm sources (Fig. eps4 ).

To perform these analyses we remove from our sample the sources 3-70 and 3-96 with AGN properties (see farir ) and used the parametric solution of the 60 m LF derived by Takeuchi et al. (2003, 2004), which agrees well with our results (see lffir , eq. eq:saunders ).

gmw_00012594.name: 3-26B
gmw_00012594.overview: The galaxy 3-26B is an edge-on spiral galaxy [arXiv:1111.6728]. It interacts with its companion galaxy, 3-26A, as evidenced by a GALEX-detected bridge and a double rotation-curve component in its northern region [arXiv:1111.6728].

sources.summary: The paper describes 3-26B as an edge-on spiral with a double rotation-curve component toward 3-26A and a GALEX bridge, indicating interaction with its companion.

quotes.quote: In some cases more than one component was detected, either because of the presence of two decoupled kinematic components as in 3-26B and 3-27A or because of interacting regions of two galaxies (3-78C2).

3-26B : this is a edge-on spiral galaxy. Its RC shows a double component in the northern region, towards the companion galaxy 3-26A. In the GALEX archival images, a bridge connecting the two galaxies appears in the same region where the RC curve shows the double component; the plateau of its RC is hinted at because the slit of the spectrograph, placed along the major axis, is smaller than the apparent dimension of such a galaxy.

gmw_00012595.name: 3-78C2
gmw_00012595.overview: The galaxy 3-78C2 is an emission-line galaxy that forms a pair with its companion, 3-78C1 [arXiv:1111.6728]. It exhibits double-peaked emission lines and a double rotation-curve component, which may be caused by an ongoing interaction [arXiv:1111.6728].

sources.summary: The paper reports double-peaked emission lines and a double rotation-curve component in 3-78C2, attributing the kinematics to interaction while noting its companion 3-78C1 lacks tidal signatures.

quotes.quote: The emission lines of the spectra of 3-27A and 3-78C2 galaxies showed a double peak. The peaks' separation is 5.4 and 3 respectively, clearly indicating two kinematical components, as discussed in Sect. 4.1.

In some cases more than one component was detected, either because of the presence of two decoupled kinematic components as in 3-26B and 3-27A or because of interacting regions of two galaxies (3-78C2).
3-78C2 : there is a double component in the RC of this galaxy that may be due to interaction; however, its companion galaxy, 3-78C1, shows no signs of tidal interaction.

gmw_00012596.name: 4C 67.26
gmw_00012596.overview: The radio galaxy 4C 67.26 is a member of the RX J1716+6708 cluster [arXiv:2403.03242]. It is an active galactic nucleus host that exhibits a strong, undisturbed Fanaroff-Riley II-like morphology dominated by diffuse radio lobes [arXiv:2403.03242].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 4C 67.26 as a member galaxy of RX J1716+6708, dominated by AGN radio lobes with a strong, undisturbed FR II-like morphology outside the cluster feedback cycle.

quotes.quote: We included the radio source 4C 67.26 in this figure as it was identified to be a member galaxy of RX J1716+6708 gioia99 and it dominates the region at radio wavelengths. Our images reveal clear sets of diffuse radio structures opposite and equidistant from their host galaxies in EGSXG J1420.5+5308 and 4C 67.26, which we therefore identify as radio lobes associated with the AGN.

The radio source 4C 67.26 shows a strong and undisturbed FR II-like morphology, but as this member galaxy is located far outside of the cluster center and therefore not part of the feedback cycle, it will be disregarded from further analysis.

gmw_00012597.name: SDSS J141531.93+112850.7
gmw_00012597.overview: SDSS J141531.93+112850.7 is a quasar located at a redshift of approximately 0.360 [arXiv:2008.12778]. It is classified as an extreme X-ray variable source, showing an 11-fold increase in full-band count flux between 2000 and 2005 as it transitioned toward an X-ray-normal state [arXiv:2008.12778].

sources.summary: The paper reports SDSS J141531.93+112850.7 as a newly found extreme X-ray variable quasar that brightened by a factor of 11, then appeared to soften toward an X-ray-normal state.

quotes.quote: SDSS J141531.93+112850.7 There were only two observations of SDSS J1415+1128 ( z = 0.360 ; log _ 10 (L_ 2500 ) = 29.11 erg s ^ 1 ) in the archive that satisfied our requirements, with the first taken in April 2000 ( 18 ks exposure) and the second in March 2005 ( 84 ks exposure). A third observation was found from April 2013; however, the quasar coincided with an ACIS chip-gap, and thus the observation was automatically removed from our analysis. Between 2000 and 2005 ( t 1.15 10^ 8 s; 1330 days), this object increased in full-band count flux by a factor of 11 . The measurement from the observation in 2013 suggests that the flux then decreased by a factor of 2.7 ; however, a more detailed analysis is required since in 2013 the quasar lies on a chip gap and thus the measurement is likely highly uncertain.

The measured values from the 2 keV flux density are _ ox = -1.87 and _ ox = -1.46 for the first and second epoch, respectively. Comparing the measured values of _ ox with the expected value indicates that the X-ray emission from the quasar was significantly weaker than expected in the first epoch ( _ ox = -0.50 ) but consistent with being X-ray normal in the second epoch ( _ ox = -0.09 ) considering the intrinsic dispersion of _ ox values in Table 5 of Steffen2006 . Using the hard-to-soft band ratios and the Portable, Interactive Multi-Mission Simulator (PIMMS) https://cxc.harvard.edu/toolkit/pimms.jsp software, we measured the effective power-law photon index, , for the faint and bright epochs (assuming only Galactic absorption).

gmw_00012598.name: SDSS J141751.13+522311.0
gmw_00012598.overview: SDSS J141751.13+522311.0 is a low-luminosity quasar located at a redshift of z = 0.281 [arXiv:2008.12778]. Between 2002 and 2014, its X-ray flux increased by a factor of 9.9 while maintaining a consistently hard, possibly absorbed spectrum [arXiv:2008.12778].

sources.summary: The paper describes SDSS J141751.13+522311.0 as a low-luminosity quasar whose X-ray flux rose by factors of 9.9 to 29 while retaining a hard, possibly absorbed spectrum.

quotes.quote: SDSS J141751.13+522311.0 There are five serendipitous observations of SDSS J1417+5223 ( z = 0.281 ; log _ 10 (L_ 2500 ) = 28.76 erg s ^ 1 ) with three in August/September 2002 ( 122 ks of stacked exposure time) and two more in September/October 2014 ( 34 ks of stacked exposure time) as depicted in Figure fig:1417_LC . The X-ray flux of this quasar has increased between these two epochs ( t 3.0 10^ 8 s; 3472 days) by a factor of 9.9 . From the single-epoch UV luminosity and the relationship in Just2007 , the expected X-ray to-optical spectral slope is _ ox = -1.32 for this quasar. In 2002, this quasar was X-ray weaker than expected, with a minimum _ ox = -1.80 ( _ ox = -0.48 ); however, in 2014 the X-ray flux significantly increased, corresponding to a value of _ ox = -1.43 ( _ ox = -0.09 ) which indicates this quasar became X-ray normal.

We measured the effective photon indices using the stacked observations in 2002 and 2014, respectively, since there is only a small temporal separation between the measurements in these two epochs. We find a similar effective photon index between 2002 ( = 1.23^ +0.17 _ 0.20 ) and 2014 ( = 1.13 ^ +0.09 _ 0.09 ). The consistently hard spectrum again suggests that the X-ray s are being absorbed in both epochs, though it is puzzling that the source has retained a hard X-ray spectrum when its flux returns to a nominal level for a typical quasar. As before, if a BAL wind is the primary reason for the absorption, this quasar must be removed from the analysis in Section sec:binomial , which we do when performing the statistical analysis for Figure fig:Binom_rmabs .

gmw_00012599.name: SDSS J142037.84+525452.8
gmw_00012599.overview: SDSS J142037.84+525452.8 is an AEGIS-X quasar located at a redshift of z = 1.269 [arXiv:2008.12778]. It exhibits extreme X-ray variability, changing in flux by a factor of 21 over a 439-day rest-frame timescale and by a factor of 12 over 2.73 days [arXiv:2008.12778].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J142037.84+525452.8 as an AEGIS-X quasar with extreme X-ray variability on both 439-day and 2.73-day rest-frame timescales, with possible absorption or intrinsic coronal changes.

quotes.quote: SDSS J142037.84+525452.8 SDSS J1420+5254 ( z = 1.269 ; log _ 10 (L_ 2500 ) = 30.05 erg s ^ 1 ) was observed 22 times with as part of the AEGIS-X survey (e.g. \ Laird2009 ), which was a deep observation of the Extended Groth Strip between 2005 and 2008. The full-band X-ray light-curve for this object is depicted in Figure fig:1420_LC , where the full-band count flux values of the X-ray detections (upper limits) are depicted by the green circular points (black downward arrows). We found that the largest factor by which the full-band count flux of this object varied was 21 on a timescale of t 3.8 10^ 7 s (439 days) in the rest frame. This object also exhibited short-timescale extreme variability by a factor of 12 on a timescale of t 2.0 10^ 5 s (2.73 days) in the rest frame.

In the bright state (December 2007), we found that _ ox = -1.34 ( _ ox = 0.16 ) and we found a steep effective photon index ( = 2.5^ +0.4 _ 0.5 ) consistent with that of a typical quasar. As before, the transition from the flat, X-ray weak state to the steep, X-ray brighter state may suggest that this quasar is absorbed. Since this quasar was observed over many more epochs that the previous objects, we further investigated the X-ray variability of this quasar by limiting our consideration to the epochs in which the quasar was detected in all three bands (seven epochs). After limiting to only these epochs, the quasar still exhibits extreme X-ray variability, dropping from the aforementioned high state by factor of 14 on a timescale of t 7.1 10^ 6 s (82 days) in the rest frame.

gmw_00012600.name: LOCK850.15
gmw_00012600.overview: LOCK850.15 is a high-redshift submillimeter galaxy that contains multiple robust counterparts [arXiv:1403.2994]. The galaxy is classified as an active galactic nucleus host that exhibits near-infrared power-law emission [arXiv:1403.2994].

sources.summary: The paper includes LOCK850.15 as a multiple-counterpart SMG, classifies it among AGN with near-infrared power-law emission, and uses power-law-subtracted photometry that improves the SED fit.

quotes.quote: Four sources (LESS10, LOCK850.03, LOCK850.04 and LOCK850.15) have multiple robust counterparts for which only one counterpart has a spectroscopic redshift. Following M12 we include these galaxies in our sample, as the 24 m and radio flux densities of the spectroscopic counterpart agree with the infrared luminosity computed from the FIR-submillimetre flux densities. This supports the assumption that the submillimetre emission originates from one counterpart.

We find 6/34 galaxies are classed as AGN in this way (AzLOCK.01, AzLOCK.10, AzTECJ100019+023206, LOCK850.04, LOCK850.15 and GN20 Although the observed S_ 8.0 /S_ 4.5 colour traces the rest-frame 1.6 m stellar bump at z 4 , we retain GN20 in our AGN sample as Riechers13 found that GN20 has an obscured AGN from power-law emission in the rest-frame MIR spectrum. ). Following the method of Hainline11 , we subtract a power-law with f_ ^ , where =2 or 3, from all photometry shortwards of 8 m (observed), incrementally adjusting the power-law contribution at 8 m to achieve the best-fit. In the following results we use the best-fit power-law subtracted values for the four AGN with weak power law components (AzLOCK.1, AzLOCK.10, LOCK850.15 and GN20).
The subtraction of a power-law from the photometry of the AGN hosts in all cases results in a better SED fit indicated by a lower ^2 .

gmw_00012601.name: LESS67
gmw_00012601.overview: LESS67 is a submillimeter galaxy that is comprised of multiple submillimeter counterparts [arXiv:1403.2994]. A single ALMA component, which is coincident with a robust radio and MIPS counterpart, supplies most of the source's deboosted LABOCA flux [arXiv:1403.2994].

sources.summary: The paper describes LESS67 as a multi-component SMG whose ALMA counterpart supplies most of the deboosted LABOCA flux; any blended-photometry dust-mass overestimate remains within the SED-fitting uncertainty.

quotes.quote: The other two sources (LESS67 and LESS79) are comprised of multiple submillimetre counterparts, with only one component coincident with the position of the robust radio/MIPS counterpart identified in Biggs11 . Since the FIR photometry for the multi-counterpart SMGs are blended, it is possible that the dust masses are overestimated. The flux for the ALMA component coincident with the LABOCA robust counterpart position amounts to 0.73 and 0.25 of the total ALMA flux in these two blended cases. Given that the flux of the ALMA counterparts are 0.87 and 0.43 of the deboosted LABOCA flux for LESS67 and LESS79, respectively, we may expect that the dust masses would be overestimated by similar factors. The change in dust mass for LESS67 is within the 1 uncertainty on the dust masses from the SED fitting, and the change in dust mass for LESS79 is within the 3 uncertainty on the dust mass.

gmw_00012602.name: LESS79
gmw_00012602.overview: LESS79 is a high-redshift submillimeter galaxy that consists of multiple submillimeter counterparts [arXiv:1403.2994]. Only one of these components is coincident with the position of its robust radio and MIPS counterpart [arXiv:1403.2994].

sources.summary: The paper describes LESS79 as a multi-component SMG with one ALMA component matching the robust radio/MIPS counterpart; its blended-photometry dust-mass change remains within the three-sigma SED-fitting uncertainty.

quotes.quote: The other two sources (LESS67 and LESS79) are comprised of multiple submillimetre counterparts, with only one component coincident with the position of the robust radio/MIPS counterpart identified in Biggs11 . Since the FIR photometry for the multi-counterpart SMGs are blended, it is possible that the dust masses are overestimated. The flux for the ALMA component coincident with the LABOCA robust counterpart position amounts to 0.73 and 0.25 of the total ALMA flux in these two blended cases. Given that the flux of the ALMA counterparts are 0.87 and 0.43 of the deboosted LABOCA flux for LESS67 and LESS79, respectively, we may expect that the dust masses would be overestimated by similar factors. The change in dust mass for LESS67 is within the 1 uncertainty on the dust masses from the SED fitting, and the change in dust mass for LESS79 is within the 3 uncertainty on the dust mass.

gmw_00012603.name: 4C +39.23B
gmw_00012603.overview: 4C +39.23B is a compact steep spectrum radio galaxy [arXiv:2202.04852]. During a low-activity state, it dominates the local gamma-ray emission, which originates from its core region [arXiv:2202.04852].

sources.summary: The paper argues that 4C +39.23B dominates the gamma-ray low state, derives its flux and photon index, and models its core and extended emission with a two-zone leptonic SED.

quotes.quote: During MJD 57500--58500, 4FGL J0824.9+3915 is in a low state with a steady ray flux. Analyzed the Fermi /LAT observation data in this time interval, it is found that the TS values of the ray emission from 4C +39.23A and 4C +39.23B are 5 and 31 , respectively, indicating that the ray emission in this time interval is dominated by the CSS 4C +39.23B. The derived average flux in this time interval for 4C +39.23B is (9.40 4.10) 10^ 9 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 with _ =2.45 0.17 .

Attributing the spectral energy distribution (SED) of 4C +39.23B to the radiations from its core and extended region, we show that the SED can be represented with a two-zone leptonic model. Its ray emission is contributed by the core region. The derived magnetic field strength and Doppler boosting factor of the core are 0.13 G and 6.5.

gmw_00012604.name: 4C +39.23A
gmw_00012604.overview: 4C +39.23A is a flat-spectrum radio quasar located at a redshift of z=1.21568 [arXiv:2202.04852]. It is associated with the Fermi gamma-ray source 4FGL J0824.9+3915, acting as its dominant time-integrated gamma-ray emitter and exhibiting variability at a 6.7 sigma confidence level [arXiv:2202.04852].

sources.summary: The paper treats 4C +39.23A as a nearby flat-spectrum radio quasar, identifies it as the dominant time-integrated gamma-ray contributor, and notes catalog associations with the Fermi source.

quotes.quote: Thirteen yr observation data of 4FGL J0824.9+3915 with the Large Area Telescope on board the Fermi Gamma Ray Space Telescope ( Fermi /LAT) are analyzed for revisiting whether 4C +39.23B, a compact steep-spectrum (CSS) source closed to a flat-spectrum radio quasar (FSRQ) 4C +39.23A in the ray emitting region of 4FGL J0824.9+3915, is a ray emitter. We find that the time-integrated ray emission of 4FGL J0824.9+3915 is overwhelmingly dominated by 4C +39.23A. It shows significant variability at a 6.7 confidence level and the average ray flux in the 0.1--300 GeV energy band is (1.60 0.15) 10^ 8 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 with a power-law photon spectral index of 2.48 0.05 .

However, it is controversial whether 4C +39.23B is a ray emitter since a flat-spectrum radio quasar (FSRQ) 4C +39.23A ( z=1.21568 , P a ris et al. 2018) is also reported to be the counterpart of 3FGL J0824.9+3916 in the 3LAC (Ackermann et al. 2015). Massaro et al. (2015) also argued that 4C +39.23A may be responsible for the ray detections of 3FGL J0824.9+3916. In the updated Fourth Fermi LAT Source Catalog (4FGL, Abdollahi et al. 2020), 4FGL J0824.9+3915 is suggested to be associated with 4C +39.23A.

gmw_00012605.name: host of SN 2018avk
gmw_00012605.overview: The host of SN 2018avk is a dwarf galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.132 [arXiv:1910.02968]. It is characterized as an emission-line galaxy with strong emission lines and an extinction level close to zero [arXiv:1910.02968].

sources.summary: The paper models the SN 2018avk host as an emission-line, low-extinction SLSN host with assumed half-solar metallicity and dwarf-galaxy-like context.

quotes.quote: A spectrum taken with the Andalucia Faint Object Spectrograph and Camera (ALFOSC) on the 2.5m Nordic Optical Telescope (NOT) on 2018 May 04 gives a redshift of z=0.132 from narrow host galaxy lines, and features matching typical SN Ic, consistent with the classification reported by ngb+18 .

In particular, the host of SN 2018avk has strong emission lines, where the ratio of H to H constrains the extinction to be close to zero. Similarly, for the SN 2018bym, SN 2018avk and SN 2018bgv host galaxies, we assume a model metallicity of 0.5 Z _ , while for the host of SN 2018don we assume solar metallicity based on the relatively strong [ N 2 ] 6548,6584 host emission lines compared to H seen in the spectra. The derived host galaxy stellar masses, extinction, stellar population ages and star formation rates are listed in Table  tab:host_prop , and the SED fits are shown in Figure  fig:host_sed . The SEDs of the hosts of SN 2018avk and especially SN 2018bym are not particularly well constrained, and the latter lacks any UV data to constrain the star formation rate in the fit.

To put the host galaxies in context, we plot the galaxy stellar masses in Figure  fig:host_z_mass , compared to literature data over the same redshift range pqy+16,skl+18,bdp+18 . The hosts of SN 2018avk, SN 2018bym and SN 2018bgv are consistent with the bulk of SLSN host galaxies at this redshift, which are dominated by dwarf galaxies.

gmw_00012606.name: host of SN 2018bym
gmw_00012606.overview: The host of SN 2018bym is a dwarf galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.274 [arXiv:1910.02968]. Spectral analysis of the galaxy indicates a model metallicity of 0.5 solar metallicity [arXiv:1910.02968].

sources.summary: The paper gives the SN 2018bym host redshift from narrow emission lines, assumes half-solar metallicity, and places it among dwarf-dominated SLSN host galaxies.

quotes.quote: Narrow emission lines in the classification spectrum reported by bgb+18 gives the host galaxy redshift z=0.274 , which is also confirmed in our own follow-up spectroscopy.

Similarly, for the SN 2018bym, SN 2018avk and SN 2018bgv host galaxies, we assume a model metallicity of 0.5 Z _ , while for the host of SN 2018don we assume solar metallicity based on the relatively strong [ N 2 ] 6548,6584 host emission lines compared to H seen in the spectra. The derived host galaxy stellar masses, extinction, stellar population ages and star formation rates are listed in Table  tab:host_prop , and the SED fits are shown in Figure  fig:host_sed . The SEDs of the hosts of SN 2018avk and especially SN 2018bym are not particularly well constrained, and the latter lacks any UV data to constrain the star formation rate in the fit.

To put the host galaxies in context, we plot the galaxy stellar masses in Figure  fig:host_z_mass , compared to literature data over the same redshift range pqy+16,skl+18,bdp+18 . The hosts of SN 2018avk, SN 2018bym and SN 2018bgv are consistent with the bulk of SLSN host galaxies at this redshift, which are dominated by dwarf galaxies.

gmw_00012607.name: host of SN 2018bgv
gmw_00012607.overview: The host galaxy of SN 2018bgv is an emission-line galaxy located at a redshift of z=0.0795 [arXiv:1910.02968]. Photometric modeling of the host suggests a stellar population with an assumed metallicity of half-solar [arXiv:1910.02968].

sources.summary: The paper measures the SN 2018bgv host redshift, builds a host template for UV photometry, and assumes half-solar metallicity while fitting host properties.

quotes.quote: Narrow emission lines from the host galaxy seen in our subsequent spectra give a precise redshift of z=0.0795 .

For SN 2018bgv, we do see the UVOT fluxes leveling off in the later epochs suggesting host contamination. To correct for this, we use the data taken between June 22 2018 and November 10 2018 to build a host template. We then measure the flux of the transient in a 6 \ circular aperture, and quantify the host flux by applying the same aperture to the template images.

Similarly, for the SN 2018bym, SN 2018avk and SN 2018bgv host galaxies, we assume a model metallicity of 0.5 Z _ , while for the host of SN 2018don we assume solar metallicity based on the relatively strong [ N 2 ] 6548,6584 host emission lines compared to H seen in the spectra. The derived host galaxy stellar masses, extinction, stellar population ages and star formation rates are listed in Table  tab:host_prop , and the SED fits are shown in Figure  fig:host_sed .

gmw_00012608.name: host of SN 2018don
gmw_00012608.overview: The host galaxy of SN 2018don is a comparatively massive system with a stellar mass exceeding 10^9 solar masses [arXiv:1910.02968]. It is characterized by a solar metallicity, and its photometry is consistent with the possibility that the supernova is reddened [arXiv:1910.02968].

sources.summary: The paper describes the SN 2018don host as solar-metallicity, comparatively massive, well sampled in SED fitting, and consistent with significant supernova reddening.

quotes.quote: Similarly, for the SN 2018bym, SN 2018avk and SN 2018bgv host galaxies, we assume a model metallicity of 0.5 Z _ , while for the host of SN 2018don we assume solar metallicity based on the relatively strong [ N 2 ] 6548,6584 host emission lines compared to H seen in the spectra. The derived host galaxy stellar masses, extinction, stellar population ages and star formation rates are listed in Table  tab:host_prop , and the SED fits are shown in Figure  fig:host_sed .

The host of SN 2018don has the best sampled SED, and is the only galaxy out of the four with derived stellar mass >10^9  M _ . We note that while the best-fit model (with a reduced ^2 statistic of 0.6) has A_V=0 , models with A_V up to 1.8 are within the 1 uncertainty range. If we assume A_V 1.2 as suggested by the supernova spectral comparisons, the resulting host model has a similar mass but younger stellar population age (as expected from the age-extinction degeneracy), and the fit has a reduced ^2 of 1.06. The galaxy photometry is therefore consistent with the possibility that the supernova is significantly reddened.

The host of SN 2018don stands out by being one of the most massive SLSN-I host galaxies found to date at low redshift -- it is not unique, however: the host of PTF10uhf has a derived stellar mass >10^ 11   M _ pqy+16 , and the host of SN 2017egm similarly has a stellar mass of several times 10^ 10   M _ bdp+18,nbm+17 .
