Thus, SN 2018don adds to the small but growing sample of SLSN-I that occur in high-mass, solar metallicity galaxies.

gmw_00012609.name: UGC 7399
gmw_00012609.overview: UGC 7399 is a late-type dwarf galaxy [arXiv:1207.2729]. It exhibits a lopsided appearance and a strong deviation from axisymmetry, with its gas extending nearly twice as far on one side of the galaxy as on the other [arXiv:1207.2729].

sources.summary: The paper identifies UGC 7399 as the lone discrepant late-type dwarf in the baryonic scaling fits, attributing the mismatch to its lopsided gas distribution and non-axisymmetric structure.

quotes.quote: For the late-type dwarf galaxies (Figure figdwarfs ), there is a clear discrepancy between the model and the observed rotation curve for only one of the galaxies, UGC 7399. We note that this galaxy has a lopsided appearance in , with the \ extending almost twice further on one side of the galaxy than on the other. Because of this strong deviation from axisymmetry, the derived \ profile is likely not to be representative of the true radial \ distribution.

gmw_00012610.name: HSC J021930--050915
gmw_00012610.overview: HSC J021930--050915 is a high-redshift Lyman-break dropout galaxy [arXiv:1704.06004]. Spectroscopic observations confirm its redshift at z = 4.580, showing an asymmetric emission line that is likely redshifted Lyman-alpha [arXiv:1704.06004].

sources.summary: The paper confirms HSC J021930--050915 as a z = 4.580 high-redshift dropout galaxy, with FOCAS spectroscopy showing an asymmetric line likely to be redshifted Ly emission.

quotes.quote: We find the five LBG candidates, HSC J090704+002624, HSC J100332+024552, HSC J084818+004509, HSC J084021+010311, and HSC J021930--050915, are real high- z galaxies at z 5.96 , z = 5.957 , z 5.80 , z 5.61 , and z = 4.580 . The first four galaxies are included in our i dropout sample, and the last one is in our dropout sample. The first and the last three galaxies were selected for our follow-up targets because they are relatively bright among sources in our samples that could be targeted during our observing runs and had not been spectroscopically observed.

For HSC J100332+024552 and HSC J021930--050915, we detect an emission line that shows an asymmetric profile with a steeply rising edge at the shorter wavelength of the peak and a slowly decaying red tail, which are characteristic features of Ly at high redshift 2006ApJ...648....7K,2006PASJ...58..313S . We confirm that no other emission lines are detected in their spectra, which excludes the possibilities that the detected line is a strong emission line at lower z , i.e., H , or [ Oiii ] for HSC J100332+024552, and H , [ Oii ] , or [ Oiii ] for HSC J021930--050915. In other words, the single line detections in our spectra cannot completely rule out the possibilities that the detected lines are H or [ Oii ] for HSC J100332+024552 and H for HSC J021930--050915. However, their asymmetric line profiles suggest that the detected line is likely to be redshifted Ly , not H or [ Oii ] (e.g., ).

gmw_00012611.name: HSC J084021+010311
gmw_00012611.overview: HSC J084021+010311 is a high-redshift Lyman-break galaxy candidate [arXiv:1704.06004]. Spectroscopic follow-up identifies the source at a redshift of approximately 5.61 based on its Lyman-break and absorption line features [arXiv:1704.06004].

sources.summary: FOCAS follow-up identifies HSC J084021+010311 as a real z 5.61 high-redshift LBG candidate in the i-dropout sample, with redshift support from Ly-break and absorption features.

quotes.quote: We find the five LBG candidates, HSC J090704+002624, HSC J100332+024552, HSC J084818+004509, HSC J084021+010311, and HSC J021930--050915, are real high- z galaxies at z 5.96 , z = 5.957 , z 5.80 , z 5.61 , and z = 4.580 . The first four galaxies are included in our i dropout sample, and the last one is in our dropout sample. The first and the last three galaxies were selected for our follow-up targets because they are relatively bright among sources in our samples that could be targeted during our observing runs and had not been spectroscopically observed.

For the other three sources, their Ly break feature and low-S/N absorption line features in their continua are used for their redshift determinations, although their uncertainties are relatively large.

Although these five sources are likely to be high- z galaxies because of these observational results, it should be noted that it is difficult to completely rule out the possibilities that they are foreground sources such as Galactic brown dwarfs based on these low-S/N spectra. The nature of these sources will be checked by future follow-up observations.

gmw_00012612.name: HSC J084818+004509
gmw_00012612.overview: HSC J084818+004509 is a high-redshift Lyman-break galaxy candidate located at a redshift of approximately 5.80 [arXiv:1704.06004]. Spectroscopic observations of the galaxy confirm its Lyman-break feature, which is broadly consistent with a redshift of 5.78 determined by the SHELLQs survey [arXiv:1704.06004].

sources.summary: The paper identifies HSC J084818+004509 as a z 5.80 high-redshift LBG candidate, noting noisy FOCAS and LDSS3 spectra, confirmed Ly-break evidence, and consistency with a SHELLQs redshift.

quotes.quote: We find the five LBG candidates, HSC J090704+002624, HSC J100332+024552, HSC J084818+004509, HSC J084021+010311, and HSC J021930--050915, are real high- z galaxies at z 5.96 , z = 5.957 , z 5.80 , z 5.61 , and z = 4.580 . The first four galaxies are included in our i dropout sample, and the last one is in our dropout sample. The first and the last three galaxies were selected for our follow-up targets because they are relatively bright among sources in our samples that could be targeted during our observing runs and had not been spectroscopically observed.

For the other three sources, their Ly break feature and low-S/N absorption line features in their continua are used for their redshift determinations, although their uncertainties are relatively large.

Since we have taken only two exposures for HSC J084818+004509 due to a technical problem in our observations, the reduced spectrum is severely affected by cosmic rays. This source has also been observed with LDSS3. However, the number of exposures with LDSS3 is also only two and it is difficult to remove cosmic rays in its reduced spectrum, although the Ly break feature in its continuum is confirmed. Note that HSC J084818+004509 has been reported as a z=5.78 galaxy by the Subaru high- z exploration of low-luminosity quasars (SHELLQs) survey 2016ApJ...828...26M , whose redshift determination result is broadly consistent with our result.

gmw_00012613.name: HSC J090704+002624
gmw_00012613.overview: HSC J090704+002624 is a high-redshift Lyman-break galaxy candidate [arXiv:1704.06004]. Spectroscopic follow-up identifies the galaxy at a redshift of approximately 5.96 based on its continuum Lyman-break and absorption features [arXiv:1704.06004].

sources.summary: FOCAS follow-up identifies HSC J090704+002624 as a real z 5.96 high-redshift LBG candidate in the i-dropout sample, with redshift evidence from continuum Ly-break and absorption features.

quotes.quote: We find the five LBG candidates, HSC J090704+002624, HSC J100332+024552, HSC J084818+004509, HSC J084021+010311, and HSC J021930--050915, are real high- z galaxies at z 5.96 , z = 5.957 , z 5.80 , z 5.61 , and z = 4.580 . The first four galaxies are included in our i dropout sample, and the last one is in our dropout sample. The first and the last three galaxies were selected for our follow-up targets because they are relatively bright among sources in our samples that could be targeted during our observing runs and had not been spectroscopically observed.

For the other three sources, their Ly break feature and low-S/N absorption line features in their continua are used for their redshift determinations, although their uncertainties are relatively large.

Although these five sources are likely to be high- z galaxies because of these observational results, it should be noted that it is difficult to completely rule out the possibilities that they are foreground sources such as Galactic brown dwarfs based on these low-S/N spectra. The nature of these sources will be checked by future follow-up observations.

gmw_00012614.name: HSC J100332+024552
gmw_00012614.overview: HSC J100332+024552 is a high-redshift Lyman-break galaxy candidate [arXiv:1704.06004]. Spectroscopic observations confirm its redshift at z = 5.957 based on an asymmetric emission line that is likely redshifted Lyman-alpha emission [arXiv:1704.06004].

sources.summary: The paper confirms HSC J100332+024552 as a z = 5.957 high-redshift LBG candidate, using FOCAS spectroscopy and an asymmetric emission line likely to be redshifted Ly emission.

quotes.quote: We find the five LBG candidates, HSC J090704+002624, HSC J100332+024552, HSC J084818+004509, HSC J084021+010311, and HSC J021930--050915, are real high- z galaxies at z 5.96 , z = 5.957 , z 5.80 , z 5.61 , and z = 4.580 . The first four galaxies are included in our i dropout sample, and the last one is in our dropout sample. The first and the last three galaxies were selected for our follow-up targets because they are relatively bright among sources in our samples that could be targeted during our observing runs and had not been spectroscopically observed. The second galaxy was a mask filler source that was randomly chosen from our i dropout candidates within the field-of-view of FOCAS centered on a primary target, a bright LAE.

For HSC J100332+024552 and HSC J021930--050915, we detect an emission line that shows an asymmetric profile with a steeply rising edge at the shorter wavelength of the peak and a slowly decaying red tail, which are characteristic features of Ly at high redshift 2006ApJ...648....7K,2006PASJ...58..313S . We confirm that no other emission lines are detected in their spectra, which excludes the possibilities that the detected line is a strong emission line at lower z , i.e., H , or [ Oiii ] for HSC J100332+024552, and H , [ Oii ] , or [ Oiii ] for HSC J021930--050915. In other words, the single line detections in our spectra cannot completely rule out the possibilities that the detected lines are H or [ Oii ] for HSC J100332+024552 and H for HSC J021930--050915. However, their asymmetric line profiles suggest that the detected line is likely to be redshifted Ly , not H or [ Oii ] (e.g., ).

gmw_00012615.name: PGC072471
gmw_00012615.overview: PGC072471 is a radio galaxy located at a redshift of approximately 0.034130 [arXiv:1808.04057]. It hosts a compact core and faint, diffuse radio lobes that extend nearly symmetrically east and west over a distance of 109 kiloparsecs [arXiv:1808.04057].

sources.summary: The paper reports faint symmetric radio lobes around PGC072471, gives its redshift, and measures a compact core plus lobes extending 109 kpc east-west.

quotes.quote: We have detected faint diffuse lobes around a compact source in our uGMRT 402 MHz image. The compact source is associated with the galaxy PGC072471 that is located at the redshift of 0.034130 0.000067 1996A&AS..116..203S . The diffuse lobes extend nearly symmetrically on the east and west of the compact source.
The compact core has a flux density of 2.6 mJy and total flux density including the lobes is 26.6 mJy.
The east-west extent of the lobes is 193'' which is equal to 109 kpc at the redshift of the host galaxy.

gmw_00012616.name: AGC 122966
gmw_00012616.overview: AGC 122966 is a low surface brightness dwarf galaxy with a velocity dispersion of 7 km/s [arXiv:2004.14392]. It is characterized by a large optical disc scale length and high rotation velocity, making it a high-angular-momentum outlier [arXiv:2004.14392].

sources.summary: The paper models AGC 122966 from WSRT data, attributes apparent axis misalignment to beam elongation, measures 7 km/s dispersion, and identifies it as the highest-j outlier.

quotes.quote: AGC 122966: The size of the beam is 33.16" 18.70", oriented at 15 ^ . Given the orientation of the galaxy, the component of the beam along its kinematic major axis is 20.5". Considering the extension of the galaxy ( 33") we oversample by a factor 1.2, using RADSEP = 16.5" and allowing us to have two points. This galaxy gives the impression of having perpendicular morphological and kinematic major axes, but this is an apparent effect due to the elongated shape of the WSRT beam, as it can be seen in Appendix app:extra with the less elongated beam of the VLA data.
The WSRT data for AGC 122966, which is Hanning smoothed and of lower spatial resolution ( _ v 6 km s ^ 1 ), has = 7 2 km s ^ 1 .

The outlier with the highest j_ is AGC 122966, which has both the largest optical disc scale length and highest rotation velocity of our sample, resulting in a j_ about 9 times larger than expected. Note, however, that the Fall relation is not well-constrained at M_ < 10^8 M _ . A caveat to bear in mind regarding AGC 122966 is that it has the lowest surface brightness of our sample (see Table 1 in btfr ), so its scale length is relatively more uncertain than for the other UDGs.

gmw_00012617.name: J0212
gmw_00012617.overview: The Lyman-break analog J0212 is characterized by unusually influential nebular color excesses [arXiv:1911.07854]. It exhibits some of the faintest Paschen-alpha emission in its sample, for which only a single kinematic model component is favored [arXiv:1911.07854].

sources.summary: J0212 has unusually influential nebular color excesses, fainter uncertain line fits, and among the faintest Pa emission in the sample, with only a single kinematic model component favored.

quotes.quote: Inclusion of J0212 in the weighted average would bias the sample E(B-V)_ neb in an unrepresentative manner: 0.24 0.04 , 0.26 0.09 , and 0.24 0.11 for H + H , Pa + H , and Pa + H respectively.
Some lines are fit with considerable uncertainty, such as the fainter lines from J0212 and J2116a.
For the two LBAs with the faintest Pa emission, J0212 and J2116a, only a single kinematic model component is favored, but the others require two components.

gmw_00012618.name: J0213
gmw_00012618.overview: J0213 is classified as a composite active galactic nucleus and star-forming galaxy based on its emission line ratios [arXiv:1911.07854]. It appears to host a particularly large mass fraction of warm molecular gas and exhibits a broad second Paschen component [arXiv:1911.07854].

sources.summary: J0213 shows a broader second Pa component, appears to have the largest warm molecular gas fraction, and is classified as a composite AGN/star-forming galaxy by emission-line ratios.

quotes.quote: For J0150, J0213, and J1434, the second Pa component has a broader line width than the first.
J0213 appears to host the largest mass fraction of warm molecular gas, even higher than the vigorously star-forming J2104 (although the two agree to within 1 uncertainties).
Both J0213 and J2104 are composite AGN/star-forming galaxies according to their emission line ratios 2009ApJ...706..203O using the or BPT 1981PASP...93....5B classification, so this seems possible.

gmw_00012619.name: J2104
gmw_00012619.overview: J2104 is a Lyman-break analog galaxy characterized by extreme ionization properties [arXiv:1911.07854]. It is the most Paschen-alpha luminous galaxy of its class and exhibits blueshifted ionized gas consistent with an energetic outflow [arXiv:1911.07854].

sources.summary: J2104 is the most Pa-luminous LBA, with blueshifted ionized gas, anomalously high He/H ratios, extreme ionization, no compact radio continuum, and possible obscured AGN or X-ray-binary contribution.

quotes.quote: For J2104, the most Pa luminous LBA, we find evidence for blueshifted ( v 300 km s^ 1 ) ionized gas with significant Pa flux, consistent with expectations for an energetic outflow.

We repeat our comparison for He 1 and H 1 lines extracted over larger apertures (5 pixel radius, or 1.5 ), and find that J2104 has anomalously high He 1 / H 1 line ratios as before (implying T_ eff > 40,000 K), whereas J2116b becomes more consistent with T_ eff = 40,000 K.

However, no compact radio continuum emission is detected in the LBA with the most extreme ionization properties J2104; 2012MNRAS.423.1325A , leading us to surmise that its X-ray emission may be due to a combination of weak/obscured nuclear activity and high-mass X-ray binaries 2011ApJ...731...55J .

gmw_00012620.name: IRAS 05246 + 0103
gmw_00012620.overview: IRAS 05246 + 0103 is a galaxy that features a primary and a secondary nucleus [arXiv:astro-ph/0010077]. Its primary nucleus exhibits unresolved H2 1-0 S(1) line emission and possible [Si VI] emission [arXiv:astro-ph/0010077].

sources.summary: The paper treats IRAS 05246 + 0103 as the only other sample galaxy with possible [Si VI] emission, supported by a 5-sigma feature and unresolved primary-nucleus H2.

quotes.quote: The only other galaxy in the sample with possible [ Si 6 ] emission is IRAS 05246 + 0103. The spectrum of this galaxy shows emission at the expected position of [ Si 6 ] , with a signal-to-noise ratio of 5. The adjacent H _ 2 line appears broad on the short-wavelength side as well, calling into question the nature of the emission on the long-wavelength side of this line. No emission appears at this position in the secondary nucleus spectrum of Figure fig:secondary , ruling out a problem with atmospheric calibration or related issues. The H _ 2 1--0 S(1) line appears unresolved in the primary nucleus, strongly suggesting that the [ Si 6 ] emission is real.

gmw_00012621.name: ACT-S J065207-551605
gmw_00012621.overview: ACT-S J065207-551605 is a high-redshift dusty galaxy with an estimated redshift of approximately 4.1 [arXiv:2310.17535]. Spectral energy distribution modeling indicates the galaxy has a dust temperature of 56.0 K, a dust mass of $10^{9.65}$ solar masses, and a physical size of 6.0 kiloparsecs [arXiv:2310.17535].

sources.summary: The paper models ACT-S J065207-551605 as a high-redshift dusty galaxy, combining ACT coadd and ancillary SPT/APEX/ALMA/Herschel data to estimate z about 4.1, dust temperature, dust mass, and size.

quotes.quote: To illustrate the potential of this data for such analyses, we carry out SED modeling of one high-redshift dusty galaxy. We model ACT-S J065207-551605, which has also been observed by SPT, APEX, ALMA and Herschel and references therein Everett2020 . These measurements, listed in Table tab:fluxes2sed_ancillary , and our ACT coadd flux densities (labeled ACT ), allow us to display its SED (Fig. fig:sed_dusty_ACT-S-J065207-551607 ).

The parameter estimation provides z=4.1 0.7 , T_ d =56.0^ +7.7 _ 7.4 K, ( M_ d /M_ ) = 9.65^ +0.18 _ 0.17 and d=6.0 0.5 kpc for the high-redshift dusty Galaxy ACT-S J065207-551605 (dashed line in Fig. fig:sed_dusty_ACT-S-J065207-551607 ). Our dust temperature is larger than the temperature value of 40 4 K reported by , although the difference can be understood in terms of the models and assumptions considered.

gmw_00012622.overview: 154956+070056 is a well-defined disk galaxy that forms a close blended pair with a much redder companion galaxy [arXiv:1901.09123]. Observations of its circumgalactic medium show systemic absorption at the disk plane rather than compelling evidence of a galactic outflow [arXiv:1901.09123].

sources.summary: The paper treats 154956+070056 as a close blended disk pair, identifies the target from imaging, and finds systemic absorption rather than compelling outflow evidence.

quotes.quote: For illustration, the bottom panel of Figure fig:nirc2_hst shows the the NIRC2 Ks image of 154956+070056. We adopted the fainter galaxy in the Ks image as our target because its coordinates were closer to the SDSS coordinates. Later, the HST images showed that the fainter galaxy in Ks is the brighter member of the pair in F390W. Our target is a well-defined disk; the companion galaxy is much redder, and its spectroscopic redshift has not been measured.

Similarly, a 150 \ outflow from 154956+070056 \ would produce absorption at Doppler shifts from -142 \ to +46 \ relative to the target galaxy systemic velocity in the 154956+070056 spectrum. This sightline intersects the disk plane at R=48 kpc where the projected rotation speed is positive but near the systemic velocity. We detect \ absorption at the systemic velocity.

gmw_00012623.overview: The object 142815+585442 is a close galaxy pair resolved by high-resolution imaging [arXiv:1901.09123]. The brighter member of this pair is identified as the target galaxy, which exhibits a high specific star-formation rate [arXiv:1901.09123].

sources.summary: The paper resolves 142815+585442 into a two-galaxy close pair, identifies the brighter member as the target, and says its high specific star-formation rate needs spatially resolved SEDs.

quotes.quote: The NIRC2 image also resolved 142815+585442 into two galaxies. We have no high-resolution color data in this case, and we define the target galaxy as the brighter member of the pair.
One of these is the close pair 142815+585442; spatially resolved SEDs are needed to interpret the high specific SFR.

gmw_00012624.overview: The galaxy 104151.26+174558.7 is a confirmed member of a galaxy group [arXiv:1901.09123]. It is identified as the most likely host for a nearby absorption system and has the lowest stellar mass in its sample [arXiv:1901.09123].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 104151.26+174558.7 as the most likely absorber host, notes possible contamination by a brighter neighbor, and links its group status to unusually strong absorption.

quotes.quote: Our target 104151.26+174558.7 lies just 4 7 from the 104151+174603 sightline, making it the most likely absorber host. It is possible, however, that a much more luminous galaxy, 104152.39+174520.2 which is 45 5 SE of the quasar, contributes to the absorption system; its redshift z_ spec = 0.14189 is 112 \ bluer than our target's redshift.

The 104151+174558 \ absorption is about four times stronger than the second ranked system, suggesting this sightline has some unique attribute. The target galaxy is a confirmed group member, but it is not clear whether the environment explains the strong absorption. This galaxy also has the lowest stellar mass in our sample.

gmw_00012625.overview: The galaxy 091954+291345 is involved in an interaction with other members of its group [arXiv:1901.09123]. High-resolution imaging reveals a two-arm spiral structure that may represent tidal tails generated by this galaxy-galaxy interaction [arXiv:1901.09123].

sources.summary: The paper describes 091954+291345 as interacting with group members, possibly showing tidal tails, and suggests tidal streams may contribute to its absorption components.

quotes.quote: We find evidence that our target galaxy 091954+291345 is involed in an interaction with other group members. The NIRC2 image shows a prominent 2-arm spiral that may be two tidal tails. The candidate group includes two luminous galaxies with red colors in addition to our target. Spectroscopic redshifts are needed to confirm these potential group members: (1) J091954+291345 is 4 4 from our target and has a similar impact parameter, and (2) J091954+291336 is 8 7 from our target and further than our target from the quasar sightline.
The second component may be associated with a different galaxy or a group. Tidal streams generated by a galaxy -- galaxy interaction may contribute to one or both of these absorption components.

gmw_00012626.overview: The galaxy 165930+373527 exhibits a disturbed morphology, featuring a large arm that extends southward and wraps toward the east [arXiv:1901.09123]. This structure, combined with its rotation curve, indicates a negative disk inclination that positions a blueshifted outflow lobe along the quasar sightline [arXiv:1901.09123].

sources.summary: The paper finds disturbed morphology in 165930+373527, infers a negative disk inclination, and attributes its strongly blueshifted absorption mainly to an outflow rather than disk rotation.

quotes.quote: Our high resolution images draw attention to the disturbed morphology of 165930+373527; a large arm extends southward wrapping towards the east. The wrapping direction of the arms and the rotation curve require a negative disk inclination if the arms are trailing. In this orientation the quasar sightline intersects the blueshifted outflow lobe. The main absorption component is much more blueshifted than expected for a galactic disk component in this sightline. We infer an outflow speed of roughly 100 , about 20 \ higher than fit because we measure a correspondingly higher galaxy redshift.

gmw_00012627.overview: The galaxy 123249+433244 features a minor-axis sightline that intersects its disk plane at a very large radius [arXiv:1901.09123]. Absorption detected near the galaxy likely includes an outflow component, which is predicted to produce substantial absorption near the systemic velocity [arXiv:1901.09123].

sources.summary: The paper argues absorption near 123249+433244 likely includes an outflow, because the minor-axis sightline intersects the disk plane at large radius and shows excess equivalent width.

quotes.quote: The absorption near the galaxies 123249+433244 and 133541+285324 likely includes an outflow component for several reasons. The 123249+433244 and 133542+285330 sightlines are not only close to the minor axis; they also intersect the disk plane at very large radii. The \ systems are fairly weak in absolute terms, but their equivalent widths are twice the predicted value for their large impact parameter.

Both sightlines intersect outflow cones with a wide range of opening angles. Outflowing gas would be blueshifted along most of each sightline. However we predict substantial absorption from these outflows near the systemic velocity.

gmw_00012628.overview: The galaxy 133541+285324 is associated with nearby absorption that likely includes an outflow component [arXiv:1901.09123]. This outflowing gas is predicted to be blueshifted along most of the sightline [arXiv:1901.09123].

sources.summary: The paper says absorption near 133541+285324 likely includes an outflow, with a minor-axis sightline, large disk-plane radius, excess equivalent width, and blueshifted outflow gas.

quotes.quote: The absorption near the galaxies 123249+433244 and 133541+285324 likely includes an outflow component for several reasons. The 123249+433244 and 133542+285330 sightlines are not only close to the minor axis; they also intersect the disk plane at very large radii. The \ systems are fairly weak in absolute terms, but their equivalent widths are twice the predicted value for their large impact parameter.

Both sightlines intersect outflow cones with a wide range of opening angles. Outflowing gas would be blueshifted along most of each sightline. However we predict substantial absorption from these outflows near the systemic velocity.

gmw_00012629.name: MCG+14-02-011
gmw_00012629.overview: MCG+14-02-011 is a nearby late-type galaxy located at a redshift of 0.0149 [arXiv:2402.09304]. It has an estimated stellar mass of approximately 10^9.6 solar masses and exhibits emission-line star formation [arXiv:2402.09304].

sources.summary: The paper identifies MCG+14-02-011 as the robust nearby host of FRB 20221022A, with redshift 0.0149, emission-line star formation, late-type classification, and estimated stellar mass of about 10^9.6 M_ .

quotes.quote: Cross-matching the source localization ellipse with existing galaxy catalogs led to the identification of the galaxy MCG+14-02-011 as the probable host (panel (e) of Fig. fig:waterfall ). This association was confirmed after careful subtraction of contaminant light from a bright foreground star, which provided an improved estimate on the host galaxy -band brightness of 15.1(3) (AB mag) and a probability of association of 99 as determined using the Probabilistic Association of Transients to their Hosts (PATH) methodology (see Methods). Our confidence in MCG+14-02-011 as the host was independently bolstered by spectroscopic followup with the Gran Telescopio Canarias (GTC) that established the galaxy as nearby with a redshift of 0.0149(3), consistent with the low excess (extragalactic) dispersion measure, DM_ extra , of this event after subtracting an estimate of the Galactic DM contribution (see Table tab1 ).

We determine two scintillation scales in the burst spectrum indicating the presence of two scattering screens, one residing in our own Galaxy and another likely located in the host galaxy, further supporting the extragalactic nature of FRB 20221022A despite its very low DM excess (see Methods; Nimmo et al. in preparation). Spectroscopic analysis of GTC data identified several emission lines (e.g., [ O _ III ] ) that indicate the presence of star formation, supporting MCG+14-02-011 as a late type galaxy (see Methods).

To estimate the stellar mass (M _ ) of MCG+14-02-011 we follow ref. . The mass luminosity relation at redshift z 0 is given as a function of (g-) _ 0 colors, log_ 10 (M_ /L_ ) = 1.097(g-)_ 0 0.406, where L _ = 10 ^ 7.8 L _ is the -band luminosity of the host calculated using z = 0.0149 and m _ = 15.30 AB mag, and the factor 0.406 is derived by ref. using the Chabrier initial mass function . Using the Galactic extinction corrected (g-) _ 0 color = 0.35, we estimate M _ 10 ^ 9.6 M _ .

gmw_00012630.overview: The flat-spectrum radio quasar 0917+449 is an active galactic nucleus that was active during the first two years of Fermi/LAT operation [arXiv:1511.02654]. Since then, it has shown no pronounced gamma-ray activity, and its 95 GHz variations lag the 37 GHz variations by 8 days in the millimetre waveband [arXiv:1511.02654].

sources.summary: The paper describes the FSRQ 0917+449 as formerly gamma-ray active, now quieter, with significant millimetre-band lag but no gamma-ray correlation because the gamma-ray light curve is noise-dominated.

quotes.quote: 0917+449 : This FSRQ was active during the first two years of Fermi /LAT operation and since then has showed no pronounced activity in rays. The cross-correlation in the millimetre waveband was significant at a time lag 8 d with the 95 GHz lagging the 37 GHz variations. The ray detections occur mostly during the rise of the millimetre flare. However, no correlation with the rays was observed with the light curve being dominated by its noise.

gmw_00012631.overview: The object 2141+175 is a flat-spectrum radio quasar that exhibits sporadic variations in its gamma-ray emission [arXiv:1511.02654]. In the millimetre band, it experienced a flare around 2012.6, with the millimetre-wavelength variations lagging behind the gamma-ray activity [arXiv:1511.02654].

sources.summary: The paper describes the FSRQ 2141+175 as sporadically gamma-ray variable, with a 2012.6 millimetre flare and millimetre light curves lagging gamma rays and each other.

quotes.quote: 2141+175 : The FSRQ 2141+175 portrays sporadic variations in the rays with one marginal activity period around 2013.3. In the millimetre band a flare around 2012.6 was noticed. A time lag of 181 and 336 d was obtained with the 95 and 37 GHz variations lagging those at rays, respectively. In millimetre bands, the 37 GHz lagged the 95 GHz ones by 65 d.

gmw_00012632.overview: The active galactic nucleus 2201+171 is a blazar that is fairly faint across all observed bands [arXiv:1511.02654]. It exhibits year-long activity periods in millimetre data, with 37 GHz variations lagging those at 95 GHz by 12 days [arXiv:1511.02654].

sources.summary: The paper describes 2201+171 as faint across the observed bands, with year-long millimetre activity, a 12-day 37 GHz lag, and too few gamma-ray detections for correlation.

quotes.quote: 2201+171 : This fairly faint source in all the bands showed a year-long activity periods in the millimetre data. The 37 GHz variations lagged those at 95 GHz with a time delay of 12 d. In rays we had a few detections that were insufficient for producing any significant correlation.

gmw_00012633.name: PKS 2311 477
gmw_00012633.overview: PKS 2311 477 is a gigahertz-peaked spectrum (GPS) radio source with a 1.4 GHz radio luminosity of at least 1.0 × 10^27 W Hz^-1 [arXiv:2110.03046]. Optical imaging reveals at least two interacting galaxies at the position of the radio source, which may explain the detection of its neutral hydrogen gas reservoir [arXiv:2110.03046].

sources.summary: The paper presents PKS 2311 477 as a GPS radio source with ASKAP H I absorption, powerful radio luminosity, probable host association, and optical evidence for interacting galaxies.

quotes.quote: As an example, we present here a FLASH detection of H i absorption towards the GPS radio source PKS 2311 477.

The line has a peak optical depth of 0.1 and FWHM of v 150 km s ^ 1 , with an asymmetric tail that extends to about +300 km s ^ 1 from the peak. The spectral energy distribution (SED) of the background radio source is shown in figure:PKS2311-477_radio_sed . Using the least-squares method we fit the analytical GPS model of , obtaining a peak flux density of S_ peak = 1.3 Jy at 1.6 GHz and optically thick and thin spectral indices of _ tk = 1.1 and _ tn = -0.9 , respectively. Based on this best-fitting SED model, we use the redshift of the H i absorption-line to obtain a lower limit for the 1.4 GHz radio luminosity of L _ 1.4 1.0 10^ 27 W Hz ^ 1 , which is typical of powerful GPS sources.

In figure:PKS2311-477_image we show a grz band optical image of PKS 2311 477, overlaid with contours showing the 856 MHz continuum from FLASH. The optical image reveals at least two interacting galaxies at the position of the radio source; such interactions are thought to be progenitors of luminous radio galaxies and, if gas-rich, could be the reason why we detect a significant reservoir of H i gas towards this source (e.g. RamosAlmeida:2012, Chiaberge:2015 ).

gmw_00012634.name: SGRS J0515 8100
gmw_00012634.overview: SGRS J0515 8100 is a giant radio galaxy characterized by wide, steep-spectrum lobes and a variable jet direction [arXiv:astro-ph/0509275]. Its optical host galaxy shows evidence of an ongoing galaxy-galaxy interaction, which may perturb the central accretion disk [arXiv:astro-ph/0509275].

sources.summary: The paper presents SGRS J0515 8100 as a Fat-Double giant radio galaxy whose broad steep-spectrum lobes, variable jet direction, and interacting optical host point to jet perturbation by companions.

quotes.quote: The lobes of the double radio galaxy SGRS J0515 8100 have transverse widths that are 1.3 times their extent from the center, their surface brightness is the lowest among known giant radio sources and the lobes have relatively steep radio spectra. We infer that these wide lobes were created as a result of a highly variable and intermittent jet whose axis direction also varied significantly: the Fat-Double lobes in this giant radio source are a result of the ejection and deposition of synchrotron plasma over a wide range of angles over time rather than the expansion of relic lobes. Additionally, the optical host shows evidence for an ongoing galaxy-galaxy interaction. SGRS J0515 8100 supports the hypothesis that interactions with companions might perturb the inner accretion disk that produces and sustains the jets at the centers of active galactic nuclei.

gmw_00012635.name: GALEXASC J202849.46-042255.5
gmw_00012635.overview: GALEXASC J202849.46-042255.5 is a small, very faint galaxy that hosted the Type II supernova SN 2017ivv [arXiv:2008.09628]. It exhibits an extremely low luminosity, an exceptionally low oxygen abundance, and a low star-formation rate [arXiv:2008.09628].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GALEXASC J202849.46-042255.5 as SN 2017ivv's small, very faint host, using MUSE and Pan-STARRS data to infer an exceptionally low luminosity, oxygen abundance, and star-formation rate.

quotes.quote: The host galaxy of SN 2017ivv is GALEXASC J202849.46-042255.5. It is a small and very faint galaxy with no previously published redshift. The only available information comes from a detection in Pan-STARRS 3Pi at a Kron mag of g=21.27 0.06 mag Flewelling16 . We obtained MUSE observations on 2019 May 11 and October 10 as a part of the All-weather MUse Supernova Integral field Nearby Galaxies (AMUSING, Galbany16a ). The observations were taken at around 520 and 671 days from explosion and the SN was still detected.

Given the synthetic band magnitude measured from the MUSE spectrum ( =21.74 0.19 mag), the Galactic reddening and the distance modulus, we obtain a host galaxy absolute band magnitude of 10.3 mag (M _g=-10.8 mag, using the Pan-STARRS magnitude). This extremely low luminosity makes GALEXASC J202849.46 042255.5 the faintest host for a SN II to date. Using the spectrum of the H II region near SN 2017ivv, we estimate the oxygen abundance and the star-formation rate (SFR).

gmw_00012636.name: GRS J0844 + 4627
gmw_00012636.overview: GRS J0844 + 4627 is a giant radio galaxy with a linear size of 2.2 Mpc [arXiv:2209.01428]. Its central compact source resolves into two components, which may represent a compact symmetric object indicating a young, restarted period of activity [arXiv:2209.01428].

sources.summary: The paper identifies GRS J0844 + 4627 as a giant radio galaxy whose central compact source resolves into two components; a putative compact symmetric object implies very young restarted activity.

quotes.quote: It is identified with SDSS J084408.85+462744.2 galaxy at the redshift of z = 0.5696228 . For this redshift and the cosmological parameters specified at the end of this section, the radio galaxy's overall angular size of 5.5 arcmin translates to 2.2 Mpc of linear size hence the object qualifies as a giant radio galaxy.
The apparent single CSS source at the centre of GRS J0844 + 4627, as seen by GMRT, reveals two components separated by 0.4 arcsec (2.7 kpc in projection).

Based on the morphology of the SW component, it could be hypothesized that it is a compact symmetric object CSO, Wilkinson1994 . CSOs are objects with sub-kpc linear sizes and a two-sided structure expanding at rates exceeding 0.1 . Both these factors imply low kinematic age; in the case of GRS J0844 + 4627 it is of the order of 10^3 yr. On the other hand, the spectral age of the large-scale structure of GRS J0844 + 4627 may reach 20 Myr (Paper I), so if the CSO hypothesis is correct, then GRS J0844 + 4627 manifests restarted activity and the CSO is the emanation of the most recent active period.

gmw_00012637.name: TXS 0818 + 214
gmw_00012637.overview: TXS 0818 + 214 is a radio galaxy that appears as a triple source in FIRST observations [arXiv:2209.01428]. High-resolution observations revealed that its apparent core is actually a double, classifying the object as a double-double radio source [arXiv:2209.01428].

sources.summary: The paper uses TXS 0818 + 214 as an analog radio source: a FIRST triple whose apparent core was resolved as a double, making it a double-double radio source.

quotes.quote: In some sense, GRS J0844 + 4627 resembles TXS 0818 + 214. The latter radio source appears in FIRST as a triple, where the central component also has a steep spectrum. Owing to the high resolution provided by MERLIN Multi-Element Remotely-Linked Interferometer Network ( www.e-merlin.ac.uk ) and the EVN European VLBI Network ( www.evlbi.org ) observations, Marecki2009 unveiled the nature of the apparent core of TXS 0818 + 214 -- it is a double. Consequently, TXS 0818 + 214 turned out to be a double--double radio source Schoenmakers2000 .

gmw_00012638.name: J155019
gmw_00012638.overview: J155019 is a highly asymmetric quasar host galaxy [arXiv:2304.12061]. It has a close companion galaxy located five kiloparsecs to its south, which is not resolved as a separate source in SDSS imaging [arXiv:2304.12061].

sources.summary: The paper highlights J155019 as a highly asymmetric QSO host with a neighbour 5 kpc south; HST contours show close companions that SDSS does not separate.

quotes.quote: The presence of neighbour galaxies in both QSOs can be seen in the HST continuum images, shown as contours in Fig. fig:qso_bruno , with the neighbour galaxies highlighted by the green circles in the figure.

In J155019, the neighbour galaxy is also 5 kpc to the south the QSO host. In these two cases, Figure fig:qso_bruno shows that the neighbour galaxies are so close to the QSO host that the SDSS images do not show them as separate galaxies. Thus in our analysis of these images, they are not identified as separate galaxies but, instead, as galaxies with high asymmetry: A=0.16 and A_s=0.21 and A=0.21 and A_s=0.69 for J135251 and J155019, respectively.

gmw_00012639.name: FCC 303
gmw_00012639.overview: FCC 303 is a dwarf elliptical galaxy located in the central region of the Fornax Cluster [arXiv:1608.01266]. It features a central surface brightness profile similar to those of ultracompact dwarf galaxies and has a mass-to-light ratio consistent with a pure stellar population [arXiv:1608.01266].

sources.summary: The paper uses FCC 303 to parameterize a dwarf elliptical nucleus model, noting its bright Fornax-cluster central location, UCD-like surface brightness profile, and stellar-population mass-to-light ratio without dark matter.

quotes.quote: Model 4 is based on parameters fitted to the nucleus of the dwarf elliptical galaxy FCC 303 by Hilker et al. (2007, see also Turner et al. 2012). In the optical, this object is among the brightest in the central region of the Fornax Cluster and has a central surface brightness profile similar to those of ultra-compact dwarf galaxies (UCDs). Hilker et al. (2007) used high resolution spectroscopy and surface brightness modeling techniques to derive a dynamical mass, mass-to-light ratio, cut-off radius and half-light radius that are consistent with virial estimators. They concluded that the mass-to-light ratio of FCC 303 is entirely compatible with a pure stellar population, i.e. no dark matter is required in order to explain it.

gmw_00012640.name: SDSS J1157+0816
gmw_00012640.overview: SDSS J1157+0816 is a galaxy that exhibits double-peaked active galactic nucleus emission lines [arXiv:1810.11543]. It has two imaged companion galaxies, with the southwest companion sharing a similar redshift [arXiv:1810.11543].

sources.summary: The paper treats SDSS J1157+0816 as a candidate dual AGN: the double-peaked AGN galaxy has two imaged companions, the southwest companion shares a similar redshift, and the peaks have multiple possible origins.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1157+0816 (candidate dual AGNs). The galaxy with the double-peaked AGN emission lines has two companions visible in the SDSS imaging: one to the northeast, and one to the southwest. These three sources are also seen in NIRC2 K' band imaging FU12.1 . The galaxy with the double-peaked AGN emission lines and the southwest companion have similar redshifts (Table tbl-comp ), but the northeast companion's continuum was too faint in our longslit spectrum to extract a redshift. We cannot confirm whether it is associated with the other two galaxies, although in the SDSS image all three galaxies appear to be related (Figure fig:dualoffset ). classified the peaks in SDSS J1157+0816 as unresolved structure with a range of possible explanations, including dual AGNs, NLR kinematics, and small-scale outflows.

gmw_00012641.name: SDSS J1245+3723
gmw_00012641.overview: SDSS J1245+3723 is a candidate dual active galactic nucleus system [arXiv:1810.11543]. The system consists of two interacting galaxies connected by continuous emission, with one companion galaxy displaying an elongated morphology [arXiv:1810.11543].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J1245+3723 as a candidate dual AGN where longslit spectra show continuous emission between two galaxies and imaging suggests an elongated, interacting companion.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1245+3723 (candidate dual AGNs). The longslit spectra show continuous emission connecting the two galaxies, and the SDSS image also shows that the companion galaxy has an elongated morphology (Figure fig:dualoffset ). This suggests that the two galaxies may already be interacting. No other studies have published follow-up observations for this object, so it has no previous classifications.

gmw_00012642.name: SDSS J1248 0257
gmw_00012642.overview: SDSS J1248 0257 is a candidate dual active galactic nucleus system [arXiv:1810.11543]. High-resolution imaging reveals a companion galaxy, and the system features an extended narrow-line region with ambiguous kinematics [arXiv:1810.11543].

sources.summary: The paper classifies SDSS J1248 0257 as a candidate dual AGN with both galaxies inside the SDSS fiber, ambiguous kinematics, a companion seen in NIRC2 imaging, and an extended narrow-line region.

quotes.quote: Of these eight dual AGN candidates, four (SDSS J0952+2552, SDSS J1245+3723, SDSS J1248 0257, and SDSS J1301 0058) have a companion galaxy located within the 1 5 radius SDSS fiber. These systems are kinematically classified as outflow (SDSS J0952+2552) or ambiguous (SDSS J1245+3723, SDSS J1248 0257, and SDSS J1301 0058). However, because both galaxies are located within the SDSS fiber, the motion of dual AGNs could contribute to the double-peaked emission lines in these systems.
SDSS J1248 0257 (candidate dual AGNs). NIRC2 H band and K' band imaging of this system reveal the companion galaxy RO11.1,FU12.1 . classified this system as having an extended NLR.

gmw_00012643.name: SDSS J1301 0058
gmw_00012643.overview: SDSS J1301 0058 is a candidate dual active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1810.11543]. The system features a companion galaxy with an elongated tidal tail, indicating that the two galaxies are interacting [arXiv:1810.11543].

sources.summary: The paper classifies SDSS J1301 0058 as a candidate dual AGN with ambiguous fiber kinematics, a companion seen in HST imaging, an elongated tidal tail, and an unresolved narrow-line region.

quotes.quote: Of these eight dual AGN candidates, four (SDSS J0952+2552, SDSS J1245+3723, SDSS J1248 0257, and SDSS J1301 0058) have a companion galaxy located within the 1 5 radius SDSS fiber. These systems are kinematically classified as outflow (SDSS J0952+2552) or ambiguous (SDSS J1245+3723, SDSS J1248 0257, and SDSS J1301 0058). However, because both galaxies are located within the SDSS fiber, the motion of dual AGNs could contribute to the double-peaked emission lines in these systems.

SDSS J1301 0058 (candidate dual AGNs). A companion galaxy is seen in HST /ACS/F550M imaging FU12.1 , and the companion's elongated tidal tail suggests that the two galaxies are already interacting. classified this system as having an unresolved NLR.

gmw_00012644.name: SDSS J1323+0308
gmw_00012644.overview: SDSS J1323+0308 is an interacting system that is classified as a candidate dual active galactic nucleus [arXiv:1810.11543]. The system displays visible tidal debris, and a longslit spectrum reveals an ionized-gas bridge connecting the two galaxies [arXiv:1810.11543].

sources.summary: The paper identifies SDSS J1323+0308 as a candidate dual AGN system with tidal debris and a longslit-detected ionized-gas bridge between its two galaxies, with no prior follow-up classifications.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1323+0308 (candidate dual AGNs). The SDSS image shows tidal debris in the system, and the longslit spectrum shows ionized gas forming a bridge between the two galaxies (Figure fig:dualoffset ). No other studies have published follow-up observations for this object, so it has no previous classifications.

gmw_00012645.name: SDSS J1541+2036
gmw_00012645.overview: SDSS J1541+2036 is a candidate dual active galactic nucleus system [arXiv:1810.11543]. The system features a companion galaxy detected in near-infrared imaging, which represents a merger component that may have depleted its gas reservoir [arXiv:1810.11543].

sources.summary: The paper classifies SDSS J1541+2036 as a candidate dual AGN with a companion detected in NIRC2 imaging, an unresolved narrow-line region, and a possibly gas-depleted less massive merger component.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1541+2036 (candidate dual AGNs). The companion galaxy is seen in NIRC2 H band and K' band imaging RO11.1,FU12.1 . classified this system as having an unresolved NLR.

Simulations of galaxy mergers find that the less massive black hole accretes with a higher Eddington ratio until the less massive black hole's host galaxy is stripped of its gas VA12.1,ST16.1 , which implies that in the five systems with f_ Edd,1 /f_ Edd,2 >1 (SDSS J1245+3723, SDSS J1301-0058, SDSS J1323+0308, SDSS J1541+2036, SDSS J1610+1308), the less massive galaxy in the merger may have depleted its gas reservoir. Follow-up observations of the gas contents of the galaxies would confirm whether this is the case.

gmw_00012646.name: SDSS J1610+1308
gmw_00012646.overview: SDSS J1610+1308 is a candidate dual active galactic nucleus system undergoing a galaxy merger [arXiv:1810.11543]. A stream of ionized gas connects the galaxy containing double-peaked emission lines to its companion, which may have depleted its gas reservoir [arXiv:1810.11543].

sources.summary: The paper classifies SDSS J1610+1308 as a candidate dual AGN whose companion is connected by ionized gas, with double peaks attributed to outflows and a merger component that may be gas depleted.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1610+1308 (candidate dual AGNs). The galaxy with the double-peaked AGN emission lines has a companion that is found in NIRC2 H band and K' band imaging RO11.1,FU12.1 , and a stream of ionized gas connects the galaxy with the double-peaked AGN emission lines to the companion (Figure fig:dualoffset ). classified the double peaks in as being produced by outflows, and this complicated system may include both outflows and dual AGNs.

Simulations of galaxy mergers find that the less massive black hole accretes with a higher Eddington ratio until the less massive black hole's host galaxy is stripped of its gas VA12.1,ST16.1 , which implies that in the five systems with f_ Edd,1 /f_ Edd,2 >1 (SDSS J1245+3723, SDSS J1301-0058, SDSS J1323+0308, SDSS J1541+2036, SDSS J1610+1308), the less massive galaxy in the merger may have depleted its gas reservoir. Follow-up observations of the gas contents of the galaxies would confirm whether this is the case.

gmw_00012647.name: S2CLSJ104554+584754
gmw_00012647.overview: S2CLSJ104554+584754 is a bright submillimeter galaxy detected at 850 μm [arXiv:2103.09677]. It is undetected in optical, infrared, and radio wavelengths, suggesting it is a candidate high-redshift, highly star-forming galaxy that is extremely reddened [arXiv:2103.09677].

sources.summary: The paper identifies S2CLSJ104554+584754 as a bright S2CLS-only source undetected in LOFAR, optical, IR, and other radio data, likely real, reddened, and at high redshift with z > 5.4.

quotes.quote: Two S2CLS detections -- S2CLSJ104554+584754 and S2CLSJ104501+590403, sources 8 and 16, respectively, in our sample -- are below the 5 detection threshold in the LOFAR map. Inspecting these sources in all other available wavelengths, we also find them to be below the 5 detection thresholds across the optical and IR as well as at all other radio frequencies (see Fig. fig:cutout8 ). While eight sources appear in the S2CLS catalogue that do not have counterparts in the LOFAR catalogue, these two sources are of particular interest due to their high S/Ns. With S/Ns of 8.14 and 7.14, respectively, they are very unlikely to be false detections, the false detection rate in S2CLS being less than 0.001 per cent for a 7 source. We focused on these two bright S2CLS sources as they are most likely to be real sources, and we did not attempt a further analysis of the fainter sources without significant multi-wavelength counterparts due to the probability of them being false detections.

However, if we assume that these are indeed single sources, then these objects are either extremely reddened or they are at very high redshift ( z > 4 ). Most likely, we are observing a combination of both redshift and reddening effects 1998Natur.394..241H, 2012Natur.486..233W , and thus these lone SCUBA-2 detections are candidate high redshift sources.
This results in limiting redshifts of z = 5.4 for source 8 and z = 4.7 for source 16, both of which are plausible for highly star-forming galaxies selected at 850 m.

gmw_00012648.name: S2CLSJ104501+590403
gmw_00012648.overview: S2CLSJ104501+590403 is a bright submillimeter galaxy detected at 850 micrometers [arXiv:2103.09677]. It is undetected in optical, infrared, and radio wavelengths, suggesting it is either extremely reddened or located at a high redshift of z > 4.7 [arXiv:2103.09677].

sources.summary: The paper identifies S2CLSJ104501+590403 as a bright S2CLS-only source undetected in LOFAR, optical, IR, and other radio data, likely real, reddened, and at high redshift with z > 4.7.

quotes.quote: Two S2CLS detections -- S2CLSJ104554+584754 and S2CLSJ104501+590403, sources 8 and 16, respectively, in our sample -- are below the 5 detection threshold in the LOFAR map. Inspecting these sources in all other available wavelengths, we also find them to be below the 5 detection thresholds across the optical and IR as well as at all other radio frequencies (see Fig. fig:cutout8 ). While eight sources appear in the S2CLS catalogue that do not have counterparts in the LOFAR catalogue, these two sources are of particular interest due to their high S/Ns. With S/Ns of 8.14 and 7.14, respectively, they are very unlikely to be false detections, the false detection rate in S2CLS being less than 0.001 per cent for a 7 source. We focused on these two bright S2CLS sources as they are most likely to be real sources, and we did not attempt a further analysis of the fainter sources without significant multi-wavelength counterparts due to the probability of them being false detections.

However, if we assume that these are indeed single sources, then these objects are either extremely reddened or they are at very high redshift ( z > 4 ). Most likely, we are observing a combination of both redshift and reddening effects 1998Natur.394..241H, 2012Natur.486..233W , and thus these lone SCUBA-2 detections are candidate high redshift sources.
This results in limiting redshifts of z = 5.4 for source 8 and z = 4.7 for source 16, both of which are plausible for highly star-forming galaxies selected at 850 m.

gmw_00012649.overview: 0636+680 is a compact radio source located at a redshift of 3.17 [arXiv:1202.2193]. The source is unresolved on milliarcsecond scales [arXiv:1202.2193], showing weak core polarization at 5 GHz and no extended jet emission at 8.4 GHz [arXiv:1202.2193].

sources.summary: The paper treats 0636+680 as a z=3.17 compact radio source unresolved in earlier VLBA work; the authors find no 8.4 GHz extended jet alignment and detect only weak 5 GHz core polarization.

quotes.quote: 0636+680 This source has been observed previously on mas scales in the radio at 5 GHz by gurvits1994 and more recently by fey1997 with the VLBA at 2.3 and 8.6 GHz who found it to be unresolved. Their resolution at 8.6 GHz ( 1.3 1.2 mas) is similar to our resolution at 5 GHz ( 1.25 0.91 mas). It was first reported as a GPS source by . The redshift of 3.17 is quoted from the catalogue of veron-cettyveron1989 .

Due to the lack of extended jet emission at 8.4 GHz the images could not be aligned based on their optically thin jet emission. Polarized emission was detected in the core in our 5 GHz map at the level of m 1--2 ; no polarized emission was detected at 8.4 GHz above 0.2 mJy.

gmw_00012650.overview: The object 2048+312 is a high-redshift quasar with a redshift of 3.185 [arXiv:1202.2193]. Very Long Baseline Interferometry modeling reveals a structure consisting of a strongly polarized core and an inner radio jet [arXiv:1202.2193].

sources.summary: The paper gives 2048+312 a redshift of 3.185 and models a core plus inner jet aligned across frequencies, making it a strong follow-up target with polarized core and jet.

quotes.quote: 2048+312 veron-cettyveron1993 found a redshift of 3.185 for this quasar. The apparent shift in the position of the peak between 5 and 8.4 GHz is an artefact of the mapping process; the model fits indicate a core and inner jet whose positions agree well at the two frequencies, as well as another weaker jet component further from the core detected at 5 GHz. This is a promising candidate source for follow-up multi-frequency polarization observations because the jet is well resolved with VLBI and has a strongly polarized core and jet.

gmw_00012651.name: BCG in MACS 1149
gmw_00012651.overview: The BCG in MACS 1149 is a highly luminous giant elliptical galaxy with a stellar mass of approximately 6.3 × 10^{11} M_{☉} [arXiv:1805.05051]. It hosts a supermassive black hole of 8.4 × 10^9 M_{☉} that is offset from the galaxy's light center by 4.4 kpc [arXiv:1805.05051].

aliases_in_paper: brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) of MACS J1149.5+2223
sources.summary: The paper models the MACS 1149 BCG as a luminous giant elliptical hosting an offset 8.4e9 solar-mass SMBH whose lensing explains a nearby curved arc.

quotes.quote: Here, we directly estimate the mass of a SMBH in the brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) of MACS J1149.5+2223 at z=0.54 using one of the multiply-lensed images of a background spiral galaxy at z=1.49 projected close to the BCG. A lensed arc is curved towards the BCG centre, corresponding to an intrinsically compact region in one of the spiral arms. This arc has a radius of curvature of only 0 6 , betraying the presence of a local compact deflector. Its curvature is most simply reproduced by a point-like object with a mass of 8.4^ +4.3 _ 1.8 10^ 9 M_ , similar to SMBH masses in local elliptical galaxies having comparable luminosities. The SMBH is noticeably offset by 4.4 0.3 kpc from the BCG light centre, plausibly the result of a kick imparted 2.0 10^7 years ago during the merger of two SMBHs, placing it just beyond the stellar core.

The BCG is a very luminous galaxy with a V-band magnitude of M_V = -24.1 and an integrated luminosity of 3.9 10^ 11 L_ V within an aperture of radius 30 kpc. Assuming a standard initial mass function viewed at a look back time of 5 Gyrs Chabrier2003 , we obtain a stellar mass of 6.3 10^ 11 M_ .

gmw_00012652.name: J022912
gmw_00012652.overview: J022912 is a post-starburst galaxy hosting a central active galactic nucleus [arXiv:2401.09576]. Within its inner 5 kpc, the galaxy exhibits an active galactic nucleus-ionized, double-cone nuclear outflow, while its outer regions feature non-nuclear shocked flows likely driven by supernovae or galaxy interactions [arXiv:2401.09576].

sources.summary: The paper finds J022912 has galaxy-wide broad wings, a central AGN-ionized double-cone nuclear outflow, and outer non-nuclear shocked flows likely tied to interaction or edge star formation.

quotes.quote: J022912: figure f:J022912_summary_plot summarizes the broad line diagnostic diagrams used in this section. Broad wings are detected throughout the FOV, and show similar extent to the narrow emission lines. The spaxels can be roughly divided into two classes, with the spaxels within 5 kpc showing high velocity dispersion of 500--1000 km/sec, high maximal outflow velocities of > 800 km/sec, and Seyfert-like line ratios. Spaxels outside 5 kpc show lower velocities and velocity dispersions, and LINER-like line ratios. Within 5 kpc, the red and blue broad wings dominate different regions in the galaxy, forming a cone-like structure that is perpendicular to the rotating stellar disc.

Therefore, the properties of the broad wings within 5 kpc are consistent with a double-cone nuclear outflow (driven by the AGN, supernovae, or a combination of the two) that is ionized by the AGN. In the outer > 5 kpc regions, the line ratios [ NII ] /H and [ SII ] /H increase with velocity dispersion, and the line luminosities can be reproduced by shock models. Therefore, we suggest that the broad wings at > 5 kpc originate in a non-nuclear flow (supernova-driven winds at the edge of the galaxy or interaction-induced flows) that may be excited by shocks.

gmw_00012653.name: J080427
gmw_00012653.overview: The post-starburst galaxy J080427 features a 30 kpc tidal structure [arXiv:2401.09576]. It exhibits broad ionized gas wings throughout its field of view, with line properties that suggest non-nuclear flows driven by supernovae or merger-induced processes rather than a nuclear outflow [arXiv:2401.09576].

sources.summary: The paper finds J080427 has broad wings throughout the field, a 30 kpc tidal feature, and line properties favoring non-nuclear flows over a face-on nuclear outflow.

quotes.quote: J080427: figure f:J080427_summary_plot in a:ionized_outflow_props summarizes the broad line diagnostics. Broad wings are detected throughout the FOV with similar extent to that of the narrow lines. The red and blue wings show comparable extents, fluxes, and reddening values.

This rules out a nuclear outflow viewed face on, where we expect the red wing to suffer from larger extinction than the blue wing. Moreover, the maximal velocities of the red/blue wings show a trend along the main diagonal, suggesting that the redshifted velocities are maximal where the blueshifted are maximal. While all these observables may be consistent with a double-cone outflow viewed edge-on, we rule out this option since the flux in the red/blue wings shows spherical morphology. Therefore, in this case, we favor the non-nuclear flow interpretation (supernovae at the edge of the galaxy and/or merger-induced flows).

gmw_00012654.name: J112023
gmw_00012654.overview: J112023 is a late-stage galaxy merger that exhibits broad wings across much of its field of view [arXiv:2401.09576]. The inner 5 kiloparsecs of the primary galaxy host an active galactic nucleus-ionized nuclear outflow characterized by high velocity dispersions [arXiv:2401.09576].

sources.summary: The paper describes J112023 as a late-stage merger with broad wings across much of the field and inner high-velocity gas consistent with an AGN-ionized nuclear outflow.

quotes.quote: J112023: figure f:J112023_summary_plot in a:ionized_outflow_props summarizes the broad line diagnostics. Broad wings are detected throughout a large fraction of the FOV and show roughly similar extent to that of the narrow lines. Similarly to J022912, the spaxels can be divided into two classes. Spaxels in the inner 5 kpc of the primary galaxy show high velocity dispersions of > 400 km/sec and high maximal outflow velocities of > 500 km/sec.

The broad red and blue wings show asymmetry in the maximum outflow velocity, with the blue wing showing higher outflow velocities in all the spaxels. These observations are consistent with a spherical or double-cone outflow viewed face on, with the receding part of the flow suffering from large extinction. We therefore suggest that the broad wings in the inner 5 kpc originate from a nuclear outflow that is ionized by the central AGN.

gmw_00012655.name: J020022
gmw_00012655.overview: J020022 is classified as a Seyfert galaxy based on its integrated line ratios [arXiv:2401.09576]. The galaxy features broad emission-line wings with a cone-like structure aligned with its stellar disc rotation, suggesting these flows are induced by galaxy interactions rather than active galactic nuclei or supernova-driven outflows [arXiv:2401.09576].

sources.summary: The paper finds J020022 has broad wings with a cone-like structure aligned with stellar rotation, favoring interaction-induced flows rather than AGN or supernova-driven outflows.

quotes.quote: J020022: figure f:J020022_summary_plot in a:ionized_outflow_props summarizes the broad line diagnostics. The broad wings show similar extent to that of the narrow lines, and show a clear cone-like structure. However, the double-cone is parallel to the stellar disc rotation, with the blueshifted part of the cone corresponding to blueshifted stellar velocities and vice versa for the red.

This is inconsistent with the orientation of AGN or supernova-driven outflows in simulations (e.g., nelson19 ), and may suggest that the broad component originates from interaction-induced flows. For J020022, the [ OIII ] is masked-out by the reduction pipeline since it coincides with the WFM-AO laser's sodium lines. We therefore use the integrated [ OIII ] /H line ratio from the SDSS, which places the spaxels in the Seyfert region in the BPT diagram. The observed [ NII ] /H line ratio increases with velocity dispersion, and shock models can reproduce the observed line luminosity.

gmw_00012656.name: J111943
gmw_00012656.overview: The galaxy J111943 exhibits broad spectral line wings with extents, fluxes, and reddening values similar to its narrow lines [arXiv:2401.09576]. Its line ratios are consistent with either Seyfert or LINER radiation, ruling out both nuclear outflows and shock excitation as the primary ionizing source [arXiv:2401.09576].

sources.summary: The paper finds J111943 has broad wings matching narrow-line extents, similar red and blue wing properties, and line ratios that rule out both nuclear outflow and shocks.

quotes.quote: J111943: figure f:J111943_summary_plot in a:ionized_outflow_props summarizes the broad line diagnostics. The broad wings show similar extents to that of the narrow lines, with the red and blue wings having similar extents and comparable flux and reddening values. This rules out nuclear outflow origin for this source.

Similarly to J020022, the [ OIII ] is masked-out by the reduction pipeline and we use the integrated [ OIII ] /H line ratio from the SDSS. The line ratios are consistent with either LINER or Seyfert radiation, and they show no correlation with the velocity dispersion. We therefore rule out shock excitation as the main source of ionizing radiation.

gmw_00012657.name: NGC 799
gmw_00012657.overview: NGC 799 is a barred spiral galaxy that features a weak bar with a short ellipticity plateau [arXiv:0707.4599]. The galaxy is part of an interacting system with a companion of similar size and luminosity, though it shows no signs of a violent interaction [arXiv:0707.4599].

sources.summary: NGC 799 is the sample's interacting system; its photometry reaches only partway into the galaxy, and its short ellipticity plateau and gentle decline indicate a weak MD-type bar.

quotes.quote: We also note that, except for NGC 799, no galaxy in our sample is part of a pair, or of a multiple system, which means that their bars are most likely fully due to internal instabilities, with no significant help from tidal forces. This assessment was made using LEDA (the Lyon Extragalactic Data Archive) and publicly available images in the NASA Extragalactic Database (NED), but does not exclude the possibility that these galaxies are members of galaxy clusters. NGC 799 has an interacting companion similar in size and luminosity at a projected distance of the order of its own diameter. There are, however, no signs of a violent interaction, which suggests it might be in an early stage, or that the passage may be retrograde, or the deprojected separation much larger than the projected one.

NGC 799 has a plateau of a very short extent, followed by a gradual decrease in ellipticity, rather than by a steep drop as for the galaxies we discussed above. As we already noted, this latter property is characteristic of MD-type galaxies (AM02).

The weak bars in NGC 799 and NGC 7280 share a similar ellipticity profile ( with a gentle drop after the bar ) with their MD models, which indeed produce weak bars as a result of the limited bar-halo interaction.

gmw_00012658.name: NGC 1638
gmw_00012658.overview: NGC 1638 is a disk galaxy that is classified as unbarred [arXiv:0707.4599]. Its isophotal contours display a real asymmetry, which may be related to the presence of dusty spiral structures over the northeast corner of the galaxy [arXiv:0707.4599].

sources.summary: NGC 1638 shows real asymmetric isophotes, probably linked to dusty spirals, and its low-ellipticity plateau without a clear drop leads the authors to prefer an unbarred interpretation.

quotes.quote: The isophotal contours of NGC 1638 display a considerable asymmetry which seems to be real, and not an artifact from wrong sky subtraction, since it is also present in the images available in NED. The origin of this asymmetry might be related to the presence of dusty spirals over the northeast corner of the galaxy.

NGC 1638 is a less clear case. It shows just a big plateau at a relatively low ellipticity value, with no clear drop. Such a behaviour could be observed in a barred galaxy if the ellipse fits did not go beyond the end of the bar. Since our fits reach 50 arcsec, and 21.5 and 19.5 mag arcsec ^ 2 in the J and Ks images, respectively, this is rather unlikely. We are more inclined to believe that NGC 1638 is truly not barred.

gmw_00012659.name: NGC 7080
gmw_00012659.overview: NGC 7080 is a barred spiral galaxy that exhibits a steep bar-related ellipticity drop [arXiv:0707.4599]. It features a second ellipticity plateau at larger radii, which is likely influenced by its spiral arms [arXiv:0707.4599].

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 7080 has a very steep bar-related ellipticity drop and a second plateau likely influenced by spiral arms, followed by another drop after an oval-disc component.

quotes.quote: In some of our galaxies, like NGC 266 and NGC 7080, this drop is so steep that we have no isophotal fits in the corresponding very narrow radial extent.

Two of our galaxies, NGC 266 and NGC 7080 (and to a lesser extent also NGC 1211), have a second plateau at radii larger than the first one. It is interesting to note that these galaxies are those exhibiting the sharpest drops after the first plateau. A careful analysis of the images and the corresponding isophotal ellipse fits suggests that these plateaus are caused by a second component, also non-axisymmetric but not as much as the bar. We hesitate to call these components a lens, because their extent is longer than that of the bar, contrary to what was found by kor79 for lenses.

The second ellipticity plateau in NGC 7080 may be influenced by the spiral arms, since the position angle of this second structure varies somewhat with radius in a smooth way, as is generally expected for spiral arms. Note, however, that the position angle of the isophotes that describe its spiral arms and outer disc changes much faster. In these three galaxies there is a second steep drop in ellipticity after the oval disc (this drop is less sharp in NGC 7080).

gmw_00012660.name: J0812+5225
gmw_00012660.overview: J0812+5225 is a low-redshift, radio-quiet weak emission-line quasar [arXiv:1112.2228]. It exhibits weak C III] emission, strong Fe II emission, and is X-ray weak by a factor of 8 to 12 compared to typical quasars of similar luminosity [arXiv:1112.2228].

sources.summary: The paper describes J0812+5225 as a low-redshift radio-quiet WLQ with weak C III] and strong Fe II emission, included in an X-ray-weak stacked sample and weak by factors of 8-12.

quotes.quote: Some of our low-redshift , radio-quiet WLQs have similar UV emission-line properties to those of , as listed below: J0812+5225 ( =-0.42 ) has weak C iii ] and strong Fe ii emission.
A stacked spectral analysis was performed for the six low-redshift , radio-quiet WLQ candidates with < 0.3 (J0812+5225, J0945+1009, J1139 0201, J1252+2640, J2115+0001, and J2324+1443).
Three sources (J0812+5225, J0945+1009, and J1252+2640) are \ weak by factors of 8--12 compared to typical quasars with similar optical/UV luminosity.

gmw_00012661.name: J1109+3736
gmw_00012661.overview: J1109+3736 is a radio-quiet BL Lacertae candidate that exhibits strong X-ray emission [arXiv:1112.2228]. Its spectral analysis reveals a hard photon index of 1.77, which is consistent with high-energy peaked BL Lac objects [arXiv:1112.2228].

sources.summary: J1109+3736 is described as a radio-quiet BL Lac candidate, X-ray strong by a factor of 6.3, with spectral analysis showing a best-fit photon index of 1.77 and hard HBL-like behavior.

quotes.quote: J1109+3736: The Radio-quiet BL Lac Candidate J1109+3736 is a radio-quiet BL Lac candidate based on its multi-band properties. It is strong in X-rays by a factor of 6.3 ( = 1.10 , = 0.31 ). Its \ value is similar to that of the majority of the BL Lac population (see Fig. aroaox_fig ). \ spectral analysis was carried out for J1109+3736 following similar procedures as those in discuss:stack . The \ spectrum of J1109+3736 was grouped to have at least 20 counts per bin (see Fig. xspec_fig ).

The \ spectrum, the best-fit power-law model with Galactic absorption, and N_ H contours for J1109+3736 are also shown in Fig. xspec_fig . The best-fit photon index of J1109+3736 ( =1.77 0.14 ) is consistent with that from the band-ratio analysis. This photon index value indicates a harder \ spectrum than those for the majority of the high-energy peaked BL Lac objects (HBL), J1109+3736 would be classified as an HBL if it were indeed a BL Lac object, based on the criterion _ rx < 0.75 of Padovani Giommi (1995), where _ rx is the radio-to-X-ray spectral index, _ rx =-0.130 (f_ 1 keV /f_ 5 GHz ) .

gmw_00012662.name: J1252+2640
gmw_00012662.overview: J1252+2640 is a low-redshift, radio-quiet weak emission-line quasar [arXiv:1112.2228]. The active galactic nucleus exhibits weak C III] emission, strong Fe II emission, and is X-ray weak by a factor of 8 to 12 compared to typical quasars [arXiv:1112.2228].

sources.summary: The paper identifies J1252+2640 as a low-redshift radio-quiet WLQ with weak C III] and strong Fe II emission and as one of three sources X-ray weak by factors of 8-12.

quotes.quote: J1252+2640 ( =-0.39 ) has weak C iii ] and strong Fe ii emission.
A stacked spectral analysis was performed for the six low-redshift , radio-quiet WLQ candidates with < 0.3 (J0812+5225, J0945+1009, J1139 0201, J1252+2640, J2115+0001, and J2324+1443).
Three sources (J0812+5225, J0945+1009, and J1252+2640) are \ weak by factors of 8--12 compared to typical quasars with similar optical/UV luminosity.

gmw_00012663.name: J1530+2310
gmw_00012663.overview: J1530+2310 is an active galactic nucleus classified as a weak emission-line quasar [arXiv:1112.2228]. Its X-ray spectrum exhibits a photon index of approximately 2.11 and shows no evidence of strong intrinsic neutral absorption [arXiv:1112.2228].

sources.summary: J1530+2310 has enough counts for individual X-ray spectral analysis; its spectrum resembles the X-ray-normal WLQs, with a photon index near 2.11 and no strong intrinsic absorption.

quotes.quote: Two sources (J1109+3736 and J1530+2310) have sufficient \ counts for individual spectral analysis. We will discuss the \ spectral properties of J1530+2310 here and leave the radio-quiet BL Lac candidate J1109+3736 for the next subsection. The \ spectrum of J1530+2310 was extracted following the same procedure as for J1604+4326 and J1612+5118 (see above). The spectrum of J1530+2310 was first grouped to have at least 10 counts per bin (see Fig. xspec_fig ).

Fig. xspec_fig shows the \ spectrum, the best-fit power-law model with Galactic absorption, and the contour plot of the N_ H parameter space for the spectral model with intrinsic neutral absorption for J1530+2310. The \ spectral properties of J1530+2310 are similar to those of J1604+4326 and J1612+5118. The photon index of J1530+2310 from the spectral fitting ( =2.11^ +0.37 _ 0.34 ) is consistent with that from the band-ratio analysis (see Table cts_table ). J1530+2310 also shows no evidence of strong intrinsic neutral absorption ( N_ H 2.67 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 ).

gmw_00012664.name: J1612+5118
gmw_00012664.overview: J1612+5118 is a low-redshift weak emission-line quasar with an average X-ray spectrum similar to typical radio-quiet quasars [arXiv:1112.2228]. It exhibits very weak ultraviolet iron emission, no evidence of strong intrinsic neutral absorption, and a possibly highly blueshifted line [arXiv:1112.2228].

sources.summary: J1612+5118 is an X-ray-normal low-redshift WLQ with very weak UV Fe emission, a possibly highly blueshifted line, typical-quasar-like average X-ray spectrum, and no strong intrinsic absorption.

quotes.quote: Some of them (e.g., J1612+5118) have very weak UV Fe ii and/or Fe iii emission. J1612+5118 does seem to have a highly blueshifted \ line, for which the reason is unclear. However, the \ line of this source is close to the blue border of its SDSS spectral coverage. Further UV spectroscopy with better \ coverage is needed to confirm its \ blueshift.

The average \ spectral properties of these two sources are similar to those of typical radio-quiet quasars. They have an average photon index ( =2.07^ +0.31 _ 0.30 ), consistent with that of typical radio-quiet quasars ( 2 ). The average photon index is also consistent with those from their band-ratio analyses. We did not find evidence of strong intrinsic neutral absorption for J1604+4326 and J1612+5118 ( N_ H 1.58 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 ); the spectral fitting quality was not improved after adding the intrinsic neutral absorption component.

gmw_00012665.name: J1139 0201
gmw_00012665.overview: J1139 0201 is a radio-quiet, weak-featured quasar that was targeted as an optically selected BL Lacertae candidate [arXiv:1112.2228]. It is detected in the X-ray band and exhibits weak, highly blueshifted emission alongside strong Fe III emission [arXiv:1112.2228].

sources.summary: J1139 0201 is a radio-quiet weak-featured quasar targeted as a BL Lac candidate; it has weak highly blueshifted emission, weak C III], strong Fe III, and an X-ray detection.

quotes.quote: Three of them (J1013+4927, J1139 0201, and J1604+4326) appear in the radio-quiet, weak-featured quasar catalog (Table 6 in Plotkin et al. 2010a). J1139 0201 was targeted by \ as an optically selected BL Lac candidate in Cycle 5, while J1013+4927 and J1604+4326 were serendipitously covered by \ or \ observations.
J1139 0201 ( =-0.38 ) has weak and highly blueshifted ( 2950 km s ^ 1 ) \ emission, weak C iii ] emission, and strong Fe iii emission.
J0945+1009, J1139 0201 and J1302+0030 are similar to \ analogs in this diagram.

gmw_00012666.name: J1604+4326
gmw_00012666.overview: J1604+4326 is a radio-quiet, weak-featured quasar [arXiv:1112.2228]. Its X-ray spectral properties are similar to those of typical radio-quiet quasars, showing no evidence of strong intrinsic neutral absorption [arXiv:1112.2228].

sources.summary: J1604+4326 is a radio-quiet weak-featured quasar with archival X-ray coverage; joint fitting finds typical-quasar-like X-ray spectral properties and no evidence for strong intrinsic neutral absorption.

quotes.quote: For the two \ normal, low-redshift WLQs (J1604+4326 and J1612+5118), we performed joint fitting to study the average \ spectral properties of these sources. The \ spectra were extracted from apertures of 3'' radius centered on the \ positions of these sources via the standard CIAO routine psextract . The background spectra were extracted from annular regions with inner radii of 6'' and outer radii of 9'' , which are free of \ sources. Two spectra were extracted individually for J1604+4326 from its two \ observations.

The average \ spectral properties of these two sources are similar to those of typical radio-quiet quasars. They have an average photon index ( =2.07^ +0.31 _ 0.30 ), consistent with that of typical radio-quiet quasars ( 2 ). The average photon index is also consistent with those from their band-ratio analyses.
