We did not find evidence of strong intrinsic neutral absorption for J1604+4326 and J1612+5118 ( N_ H 1.58 10^ 22 cm ^ 2 ); the spectral fitting quality was not improved after adding the intrinsic neutral absorption component.

gmw_00012667.name: J2115+0001
gmw_00012667.overview: J2115+0001 is a radio-quiet, weak-featured quasar located at a redshift of approximately 2.4995 [arXiv:1112.2228]. This active galactic nucleus has been identified as a radio-quiet BL Lac candidate and is undetected in X-rays by Chandra [arXiv:1112.2228].

sources.summary: J2115+0001 is identified as a radio-quiet weak-featured quasar with redshift from Ly-alpha absorption, low SDSS signal for reliable emission-line measurements, and no Chandra detection.

quotes.quote: The other two objects (J2115+0001 and J2324+1443) were initially identified as weak-featured quasars by Collinge et al. (2005). They were also listed in the catalog of Plotkin et al. (2010a). These two sources did not have constraints on their radio fluxes in Collinge et al. (2005) or Plotkin et al. (2010a) but were later confirmed as radio-quiet sources by the VLA observations of Plotkin et al. (2010b). They were targeted by \ as radio-quiet BL Lac candidates in Cycle 10; their observations were briefly reported in Plotkin et al. (2010b).

The redshift of the other source (J2115+0001; z=2.4995 0.0052 ) is measured based on a Ly + \ absorption system. Plotkin et al. (2010b) did not report the redshift for this source. In this work we adopt the redshift of the Ly + narrow absorption system as the systemic redshift.
Two sources (J0945+1009 and J2115+0001) are undetected by .

gmw_00012668.name: J2324+1443
gmw_00012668.overview: J2324+1443 is a weak-featured quasar [arXiv:1112.2228]. It is classified as a radio-quiet source and has been detected in archival X-ray observations [arXiv:1112.2228].

sources.summary: J2324+1443 is a radio-quiet weak-featured quasar with confirmed radio quietness, included in the X-ray-weak stacked sample, and detected in archival X-rays.

quotes.quote: The other two objects (J2115+0001 and J2324+1443) were initially identified as weak-featured quasars by Collinge et al. (2005). They were also listed in the catalog of Plotkin et al. (2010a). These two sources did not have constraints on their radio fluxes in Collinge et al. (2005) or Plotkin et al. (2010a) but were later confirmed as radio-quiet sources by the VLA observations of Plotkin et al. (2010b). They were targeted by \ as radio-quiet BL Lac candidates in Cycle 10; their observations were briefly reported in Plotkin et al. (2010b).
A stacked spectral analysis was performed for the six low-redshift , radio-quiet WLQ candidates with < 0.3 (J0812+5225, J0945+1009, J1139 0201, J1252+2640, J2115+0001, and J2324+1443).

Three (J1139 0201, J1604+4326, and J2324+1443) of the five low-redshift , radio-quiet WLQ candidates with sensitive archival \ coverage are detected in X-rays , while two (J1013+4927 and J2115+0001) do not have \ detections.

gmw_00012669.name: UGC 2998
gmw_00012669.overview: UGC 2998 is the second brightest galaxy in the NGC 1550 group [arXiv:2005.12950]. It is located approximately 770 kiloparsecs from the group's core and is considered too distant to be a credible perturber of the group [arXiv:2005.12950].

sources.summary: UGC 2998 is considered as a possible perturber of the NGC 1550 group, but its large projected distance and travel time make it too distant to explain the observed sloshing front.

quotes.quote: Examining the volume of the group, we find that the second brightest galaxy, UGC 2998 is located 50 \ WNW of NGC 1550, with a velocity difference 400 \ between the two. At our adopted distance for the group of 53 Mpc, this is equivalent to 770 kpc. The velocity dispersion of the group has been estimated to be =263 34 Zhangetal17 or 300 Sunetal03 .

The velocity difference suggests that UGC 2998 is likely moving faster than this, but even assuming a velocity of 600 , it would take > 1 Gyr to travel from the group core to its current position. Our estimate of 40-80 Myr for the sloshing timescale only applies to the front we observe, and it is possible that the sloshing extends to larger radii, with older fronts still to be discovered. Nonetheless, UGC 2998 seems too distant to be a credible perturber.

gmw_00012670.name: UGC 3008
gmw_00012670.overview: UGC 3008 is a galaxy located approximately 260 kiloparsecs west-northwest of NGC 1550 [arXiv:2005.12950]. It has been evaluated as a potential perturber of the NGC 1550 group, although archival X-ray data do not show the additional sloshing front that its travel time would imply [arXiv:2005.12950].

sources.summary: UGC 3008 is evaluated as a possible NGC 1550 perturber; its travel time would imply an additional sloshing front, but archival X-ray data do not reveal one.

quotes.quote: The next nearest galaxy of comparable brightness to NGC 1550 (1.6 mag fainter in B band) is UGC 3008, 17 \ (260 kpc) WNW of NGC 1550 and separated from it by 500 . Again assuming a plane-of-sky velocity of 600 , it would take 420 Myr to travel from the group core to its current position. If UGC 3008 is the perturber, this would certainly suggest that at least one more sloshing front should be present. Examination of the archival \ data does not reveal such a structure, but the effect of the point-spread function and instrumental structures may impact our ability to detect it.

gmw_00012671.name: IC 366
gmw_00012671.overview: IC 366 is a nearby elliptical galaxy located to the south-southwest of NGC 1550 [arXiv:2005.12950]. It is considered a potential perturber candidate for NGC 1550, although its small size and required close passage make this scenario problematic [arXiv:2005.12950].

sources.summary: IC 366 is considered as a nearby elliptical perturber candidate with small velocity offset, but its small size, required close passage, and southern position make the scenario problematic.

quotes.quote: A third possibility is the elliptical IC 366, which lies 3 \ SSW of NGC 1550. The two galaxies are separated by < 25 \ in velocity, suggesting that the motion of IC 366 relative to NGC 1550 is primarily in the plane of the sky. If IC 366 is moving away from NGC 1550 at 600 , it would take 75 Myr to reach its current position, comparable to the longer sloshing timescale, and about a factor of 2 longer than the dynamical and radiative age estimates for the radio source. However, IC 366 is relatively small (2.3 mag fainter in K band), would probably have to have passed very close to NGC 1550 to cause the sloshing, and its position to the south of NGC 1550 is not what we would expect given the shape of the sloshing front.

gmw_00012672.name: NGC 3136
gmw_00012672.overview: NGC 3136 is an early-type galaxy with a complex stellar velocity dispersion map [arXiv:1610.01174]. Observations of the galaxy show no ordered stellar rotation, and its nucleus has been identified using [O I] emission [arXiv:1610.01174].

sources.summary: The paper reports no ordered stellar rotation in NGC 3136, uses [O I] emission to identify its nucleus, finds no dispersion peak, and classifies its dispersion map as complex.

quotes.quote: For NGC 1399, NGC 2663 and NGC 3136, where we did not detect any stellar structure in rotation, the heliocentric radial velocities shown in Table tab_mapa_vel_radial_stellar were extracted from the position of the nuclei of these galaxies. In NGC 2663, we set the position of the nucleus by the image of the red wing of the broad component of H (see paper II). This component is emitted by the broad line region (BLR), which is directly associated with AGNs in galaxies. In NGC 3136, we used the image of the [ O I ] 6300 line to find the nucleus.

We do not see any evidence of peaks in the velocity dispersion maps of NGC 1380, NGC 2663, and NGC 3136. In NGC 1404, we detected a sigma-drop in the central region. This feature has already been reported by 1998A&AS..133..325G . We will revisit the sigma-drop detected in NGC 1404 in Section ngc1404_case . The nuclear peaks detected in ESO 208 G-21, IC 1459, IC 5181, and NGC 4546 should correspond, in part, to unresolved stellar rotation. This interpretation is not new at all (see e.g., 1987IAUS..127...17K,1988ApJ...327L..55F ). First, the peaks of velocity dispersion agree, within the errors, with the positions of the kinematic centre and also with the positions of the AGNs of these four objects. Also, note that in IC 1459, IC 5181 and NGC 4546, the shape of the peaks are elongated perpendicularly to the direction of the rotation. The 1D profiles confirm this statement, since the velocity dispersion decreases slowly along this direction. A possible interpretation for this characteristic is that it is caused by a stellar structure in rotation. Along the plane of rotation, the sigma peak has a width limited by the PSF. On the perpendicular direction it may not be limited by the PSF as the size of the structure may go beyond it. In NGC 7097, the elongated structure detected in the velocity dispersion map is a possible consequence of the complexity of the stellar kinematics discussed in Section stellar_radial_velocities . Also the elongation detected in IC 1459, IC 5181, and NGC 4546 may be the result of a more complex setup of their stellar kinematics. For NGC 1380, one would expect a peaky structure, on the basis of the radial velocity map, somewhat similar to those of ESO 208-G21, IC 5181, IC 1459 or NGC 4546. Instead, for unknown reasons, it shows an irregular structure with a tendency to increase towards the centre. 2007ApJ...671.1321G , using data from the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) , analysed the stellar kinematics of NGC 1399, where we detected a nuclear peak, but no sign of stellar rotation. They showed that the stellar orbits are predominantly tangential near the SMBH. However, our data do not have enough spatial resolution to detect the decrease of the observed velocity dispersion that results from this anisotropy in the nuclear region. Circumnuclear velocity dispersion of the sample galaxies To obtain a representative value of the circumnuclear velocity dispersion of the sample galaxies, we calculated _ * within a radius R_ * as _ * _ i=1 ^ N_p I_i _i^2+V_i^2 _ i=1 ^ N_p I_i , where I_i is the intensity of the stellar continuum in the spaxel i and N_p is the total number of spaxels within R_ * . The values of _ * and R_ * are shown in Table tab_disp_vel_stellar . The statistical errors for _ * were estimated with a MC simulation and are 1 km s ^ 1 . Maps of the Gauss Hermite h_3 moment The maps of the h_3 moment are shown in Fig. cinematica_estelar_1 . The profiles extracted along and perpendicularly to the directions of the stellar rotations detected in the radial velocity maps are shown in Fig. perfil_stellar_kin_1 . The most remarkable feature of the h_3 maps is the anti-correlation between this parameter and the radial velocities in the galaxies where we detected stellar structures in rotation. LOSVDs with signatures of asymmetry are related to rotating stellar structures embedded in the spheroidal structure, which is common for some E galaxies 1994MNRAS.270..325C,2010gfe..book.....M . These asymmetric profiles have already been detected for IC 1459 by 1988ApJ...327L..55F . Indeed, these authors interpreted this result as a kinematically cold disclike component that is embedded in a kinematically hot structure, although they described the LOSVD profile as a sum of two Gaussian functions. In NGC 1404, the anti-correlation between h_3 and the radial velocity is probably a consequence of the KDC also being embedded in the spheroidal structure of this object. Comparison of the GMOS-IFU results with other data sets Comparison with long-slit data In order to check the consistency of our results, we compared the stellar kinematics extracted from the GMOS data cubes with other data sets presented in the literature. The kinematics of the galaxy IC 1459 were previously analysed by 2002ApJ...578..787C using long-slit data from the HST and from ground-based spectroscopy. In Fig. perfil_comparacao_ic1459 , we show 1D profiles of the radial velocity, velocity dispersion and h_3 moment (only ground-based results) along the major-axis of IC 1459 extracted from the GMOS data cube and from both long-slit spectrographs. One may see that the results from GMOS seem to be consistent with the data presented by 2002ApJ...578..787C . The differences are probably caused by the fact that these measurements have distinct spatial resolutions. For NGC 1399 and NGC 1404, we obtained ground-based long-slit data from 1998A&AS..133..325G along the major-axis of these objects. Fig. perfil_comp_fornax shows the 1D profiles of the kinematic parameters extracted from GMOS and from the long-slit data. Again, one may see a consistency between the GMOS and long-slit measurements. Moreover, the GMOS results of both galaxies match the stellar kinematics IFU data presented by 2014MNRAS.441..274S . A direct comparison between Fig. perfil_comp_fornax and fig. A2 from 2014MNRAS.441..274S confirms this statement. It is worth mentioning that both long-slit data and the IFU data from 2014MNRAS.441..274S do not have enough spatial resolution to resolve the KDC in NGC 1404. In fact, the differences between all results seem to be caused by distinct spatial resolutions among the data sets. Comparison with SAURON data cubes The galaxy NGC 4546 is also part of the SAURON sample and of the ATLAS 3D project. So, we decided to analyse their data cube of this object 2011MNRAS.413..813C The data cube was downloaded from the ATLAS 3D project website at http://www-astro.physics.ox.ac.uk/atlas3d/ mainly to compare the circumnuclear velocity dispersion measurements. We built kinematic maps for this data cube using exactly the same procedures described in Section ppxf_meth . We assured that the kinematic centres of both data cubes have the same radial velocity. In Fig. perfil_comparacao_atlas , we compare the 1D profile of the kinematic maps taken from the GMOS data cubes and from the SAURON data cubes along the direction of the stellar rotations. We used the same PA from Table tab_mapa_vel_radial_stellar for this extraction. Again, the differences that are seen in the 1D profiles are probably caused by the fact that the data sets do not have the same spatial resolution. Indeed, the 1D profile of the velocity dispersion shows that both measurements agree for > 1.0 arcsec, where the effects of the seeing of the GMOS data cube are less pronounced. Also the h_3 results seem to reasonably match each other. We may also compare if the values measured for _ * agree for both data cubes. For the SAURON data cube, we found _ * = 241 12 km s ^ 1 (assuming that the systematic error for the SAURON data cubes is 5 , as stated by 2006MNRAS.366.1126C ), while we found _ * = 254 1 km s ^ 1 using the GMOS data cube. Although both measurements agree within the errors, we explore some possibilities to explain this difference between them: since the spatial sampling of data cubes from the ATLAS 3D project is 0.8 arcsec, we resampled the GMOS data cubes to the same spaxel size. We built kinematic maps using such a configuration and we obtained _ * = 253 1 km s ^ 1 . Also the spectral resolutions of both data cubes are different ( _ inst = 30 km s ^ 1 in = 5577 \ for this GMOS data cube and, from 2004MNRAS.352..721E , _ inst = 108 km s ^ 1 for the SAURON data cube). So, we modified the spectral resolution of the GMOS data cubes to _ inst = 108 km s ^ 1 , but maintaining the spaxels with sizes of 0.2 arcsec. With this configuration, _ * = 243 1 km s ^ 1 . Using spaxels with sizes of 0.8 arcsec and a spectral resolution _ inst = 108 km s ^ 1 for the GMOS data cubes, we calculated _ * = 257 1 km s ^ 1 . Although the values of _ * for the GMOS data cube with spaxels of 0.2 arcsec and _ inst = 108 km s ^ 1 and for the SAURON data cube match each other, the spectral resolution does not seem to be, alone, the cause of the systematic differences for the GMOS data cubes. The point here is that the kinematic maps for the GMOS data cubes with such a configuration is noisier than the same results with any other configuration. Moreover, the GMOS data cube with exactly the same instrumental configuration as the SAURON data cube provides _ * = 257 1 km s ^ 1 , which is almost the same result found for the original GMOS configuration. Another parameter is the seeing of the observation, but it does not seem to affect the measurements made with GMOS. We found _ * = 251 1 km s ^ 1 using the GMOS data cube in its original configuration, but with a FWHM of the PSF degraded to 1.4 arcsec (the original PSF has a FWHM = 0.71 arcsec). One possible source of systematic errors in the _ * measurements using GMOS data cubes is the wavelength range where ppxf is applied. To test this, we fitted each spectrum of the GMOS data cube of NGC 4546 with the same wavelength range as the SAURON data cube, which is from 4800 to 5250 . We found _ * = 243 1 km s ^ 1 . We also applied ppxf from 5250 to 6100 . For this setup, we obtained _ * = 223 1 km s ^ 1 . These differences between the values may come from the fact that GMOS observations made with the R831-G5322 grating is noisier in the blue region of the spectra. Also, the spectral resolution is constant in wavelength but not in velocity. It seems that, depending on the wavelength range, the values for the _ * parameter may vary by 10 , at least for NGC 4546. For IC 1459, for example, which was observed with the B600-G5323 grating (that provides a better response on the blue region of the spectra), we obtained _ * = 319 1 km s ^ 1 for the 4800 \ to 5250 \ range and 322 1 km s ^ 1 for the 5250 \ to 6100 . Both measurements are very close to _ * = 325 1 km s ^ 1 , obtained when we fitted all the wavelength range of the data cube. It is worth mentioning that only these two gratings were used in all sample galaxies (see paper I). A systematic investigation about this issue will be revisited in a future paper, but, for now, we suggest that the errors caused by applying ppxf to different wavelength ranges may be as large as 10 . NGC 1404 was classified as a fast rotator by 2014MNRAS.441..274S . They used an IFU with a spaxel of 1 arcsec ^2 and a FOV of 25 28 arcsec ^2 . By comparing our results with those obtained by 2014MNRAS.441..274S , we conclude that NGC 1404 has a KDC with an extension of 200 pc that is counter-rotating with respect to the external structure of the galaxy. Indeed, the presence of a decoupled structure in NGC 1404 was suggested by 2014MNRAS.441..274S due to the detection of two peaks in their velocity dispersion map. 2008IAUS..245..303G have also reported this counter-rotating KDC. 2006MNRAS.373..906M found KDCs with diameters of about one-hundred pc in some ETGs. These features were detected in fast rotating galaxies and are counter-rotating with respect to their external stellar components. According to these researchers, the host galaxies of these compact KDCs are among the youngest ETGs of the SAURON sample. Moreover, these KDCs are usually (but not always) younger than the overall population of their host galaxies. Although this result might suggest a link between counter-rotation and enhanced star formation, 2006MNRAS.373..906M showed that it is more likely to detect compact KDCs in young galaxies due to the effect of luminosity-weighting. By means of a simple model, they demonstrated that when a KDC of a galaxy gets older, its mass-to-light ratio increases and it becomes harder to distinguish the KDC from the background rotation field. The results of their model revealed that KDCs older than 5 Gyr are barely seen in the radial velocity maps. In other words, it is easier to detect a younger KDC, but they may have a broad range of ages. Indeed, 2006MNRAS.373..906M showed in their fig. 16 that, among six galaxies with KDCs with intrinsic sizes of 100 pc, in five the central luminosity-weighted ages of these objects are between 0.5 and 4 Gyr. Only one galaxy has such a compact structure with a luminosity-weighted age of 18 Gyr. Probably, NGC 1404 would be similar to these objects studied by 2006MNRAS.373..906M , since the estimated age of the central region of NGC 1404 is about 5 8 Gyr 2008IAUS..245..303G . We detected a drop in the velocity dispersion map of NGC 1404. Moreover, the velocity dispersion profile rises more steeply along the direction of the stellar rotations. This may be a consequence of the superposition of the two stellar components in counter-rotation. Indeed, 2007MNRAS.379..418C , using three-integral Schwarzschild models, showed that such a configuration predicts two peaks in the velocity dispersion along the rotation of both stellar components. This result for NGC 1404 resembles that of the 2 galaxies (those objects that shows two off-centred peaks in the velocity dispersion maps along the major axis of the galaxy), as defined by 2011MNRAS.414.2923K , although it is worth mentioning that the definition of such a class of objects given by these authors claims that the separation of the peaks of the maximum values of the velocity dispersion should be, at least, half of the effective radius. However, the data from the ATLAS 3D project, analysed by 2011MNRAS.414.2923K , does not have enough spatial resolution to detect velocity dispersion peaks with a separation of a few arcsec. Thus, a systematic study with seeing-limited data would be of great value in order to better understand objects with the same characteristics. Another hypothesis is that the drop is not related to the KDC. In this case, we may speculate the existence a star-forming region that is kinematically cold and dominates the stellar light within a projected radius smaller than the PSF of the data cube of this object ( 1 arcsec, see paper I). According to 2006MNRAS.369..853W , kinematically cold gas is able to form stars in the centre of barred late-type galaxies. They showed that a star formation rate of 1 M _ yr^ 1 is enough to maintain kinematically cold stars, and thus a drop. The reason is that, although there are mechanisms that make stars kinematically hot (e.g., gravitational disturbances), the nuclear region is fueled again with kinematically cold stars. However, NGC 1404 is a non-barred ETG. One possible scenario suggested by 2006MNRAS.369..853W is that the star formation rate is quite low, possibly undetectable. In this case, the drop may fade away, but over a long time-scale ( 100 Myr). Therefore, an in-depth study of the stellar populations of NGC 1404 is necessary to verify this hypothesis ( 2008IAUS..245..303G do not give much detail of their analysis of the stellar populations of NGC 1404). In addition, a detailed modelling of the stellar kinematics would be interesting, since it may confirm if this is either related to a kinematically cold component or associated with the KDC. Stellar kinematics may provide information about the formation history of galaxies. Fast and slow rotators, for example, have different formation paths 2014MNRAS.444.3357N . However, to assert a global characteristic for the stellar kinematics, data cubes with a FOV that englobes, at least, the region within one effective radius of the galaxies are necessary. The problem is that such data cubes have a spatial resolution that is limited by the size of the fibre (diameters of 2 arcsec). Observations using seeing-limited IFU data are, thus, very important in order to access information about the circumnuclear (scales of 100 pc) properties of the stellar kinematics. There are features that are only seen in such scales, as in the case of NGC 1404 [ but see also 2006MNRAS.373..906M ] . The point here is that an analysis with seeing-limited data cubes may provide complementary information about the stellar kinematics of a galaxy as a whole. Another interesting issue is that, with such data cubes, one may look for off-centred AGNs in a reliable way. Comparing the position of the kinematic centre with the position of the AGNs and also with the position of the photometric centre of the stellar structure may reveal that the SMBH is not always at the centre of a galaxy (see e.g. 2014ApJ...796L..13M ). Thus, the main subject of the present paper is to provide stellar kinematics results from seeing-limited data cubes for the sample galaxies. A more complete analysis in order to search for general characteristics of the stellar kinematics from the circumnuclear region of massive ETGs demands more data and is not possible with the present sample. Below we present the main findings of the present paper. We detected stellar structures in rotation embedded in a kinematically hot stellar structure (spheroid) in the circumnuclear region of seven galaxies of the sample. The other three objects do not seem to possess any sign of stellar rotation within a radius of 100 pc from the nucleus. The galaxy IC 5181 seems to have a small variation of the PA of the stellar structure in rotation with radius. This may be a consequence of a non-axisymmetric potential within the central region of the galaxy. It is worth mentioning that an evidence of a non-axisymmetric potential for this galaxy is also seen in the gas kinematics (paper III). In six galaxies, we detected a nuclear peak in the velocity dispersion maps. A fraction of this peak in four galaxies is likely to be associated with the stellar rotation in a region that is not fully resolved. In NGC 1380, NGC 2663, NGC 3136, and NGC 7097, the velocity dispersion maps are more complex and require a careful analysis for a reliable interpretation.

gmw_00012673.name: UDS19627
gmw_00012673.overview: UDS19627 is a massive quiescent galaxy located at a high redshift [arXiv:1912.01619]. It is minimally gravitationally lensed, which modestly lowers its estimated stellar and dynamical masses [arXiv:1912.01619].

sources.summary: The paper includes UDS19627 as a massive quiescent high-redshift galaxy, presents new HST imaging for resolved morphology, and notes lensing modestly lowers stellar and dynamical masses.

quotes.quote: Finally, in the analysis presented here, the massive quiescent galaxy UDS19627, from , is included. This object are selected as part of early VLT/X-shooter GTO observations to be quiescent ( log(sSFR/yr) < -10 ), at a high redshift ( z_ phot =2.02^ +0.07 _ 0.08 ) and a bright source ( K=20.19 ) in the UKIRT Ultra Deep Survey Williams+09 . New HST /WFC3 H_ F160W imaging of this galaxy is presented, allowing us to measure resolved morphology.

UDS19627 is minimally gravitationally lensed, but showed that, after taking this effect into account, the systematic change in magnification factor of 10-20 \ correspond to a 0.07 and 0.03 dex resulting lower stellar and dynamical mass.

gmw_00012674.name: UV-105842
gmw_00012674.overview: UV-105842 is a peculiar galaxy at a redshift of z=2.0124 that exhibits minor-merger and strong tidal or post-merger features [arXiv:1912.01619]. A nearby source to the northeast shares this redshift but has a large velocity offset, suggesting the two systems are not gravitationally bound [arXiv:1912.01619].

sources.summary: The paper identifies UV-105842 as peculiar with minor-merger and tidal features; a nearby source has matching-redshift emission but a large velocity offset, suggesting it is not bound.

quotes.quote: The latter source north-east of UV-105842 shows significant [ OII ] 3726.2,3728.9 , [ OIII ] 4959,5007 , and H emission with a matching redshift of z=2.0124 . This corresponds to a velocity offset of 2130 120 km/s from UV-105842. If purely due to galaxy motion, such an offset suggests that the two sources are not gravitationally bound at the time of observation. Another explanation of the asymmetric morphology might be a high redshift analog of the locally observed offset AGN Comerford&Greene+14 , likely caused by recent merger event.

9/15 galaxies are categorized as Elliptical galaxies while the remaining are categorized as Peculiar galaxies with major mergers (UV-108899, UV-250513, CP-561356), minor mergers (UV-105842, CP-1291751) and/or strong tidal/post-merger features (UV-105842, UV-90676).

gmw_00012675.name: UV-108899
gmw_00012675.overview: UV-108899 is a double system of galaxies in close physical proximity [arXiv:1912.01619]. The system is undergoing an ongoing quiescent, dry major merger, as confirmed by matching absorption lines [arXiv:1912.01619].

sources.summary: The paper confirms UV-108899 as one of three double systems in close physical proximity, with matching absorption lines supporting an ongoing quiescent dry major merger and stellar-mass deblending.

quotes.quote: The RB color images, in Figure fig:spec_presentation , reveal that three galaxies (UV-108899, UV-250513, CP-561356) appear to be double systems. The spectra, shown in the same figure, are the total extraction of the combined light from the two galaxies. These objects are within close proximity and the light in the reduced 2-D frames are blended to an unknown extent (due to limited seeing).

At the expense of drastically decreasing the S/N, an attempt to separate the sources and determine if their individual redshift measurements can confirm their proximity are made. For each system, the resolved 1-D HST H_ F160W light profile (extracted parallel to the X-shooter slit) is overlaid on top of the wavelength collapsed 2-D spectrum trace. A double Gaussian profile fit allowed us to gauge the amount of blending and to make a conservative extraction of each individual galaxy, minimizing cross-source contamination. In Figure fig:mm_sep , the individual extractions and the best-fit to the composite spectrum from Section sec:stelpop are shown. Because of the low S/N of the individual conservative flux extractions, the estimation of the velocity offset are refrained, since it would be dominated by large uncertainties. However, the galaxies are within close physical proximity due to the matching absorption lines shown in the figure and can be considered ongoing quiescent (dry) major-mergers.

gmw_00012678.name: NSA-19694
gmw_00012678.overview: NSA-19694 is a dwarf galaxy identified as an ongoing ram-pressure-stripping candidate [arXiv:2311.02152]. It features a clear gas tail with no apparent stellar counterpart, consisting of a main tail extending to the northeast and a small nub on the northwest side [arXiv:2311.02152].

sources.summary: The paper identifies NSA-19694 as an ongoing ram-pressure-stripping candidate with a clear gas tail, no stellar counterpart, and a morphology consistent with gas forced northwest.

quotes.quote: In the cases of NSA-19694 and LS-359104-131 (Figure fig:rps_examples , left panels) we see clear \ tails with no apparent accompanying stellar component. For LS-359104-131, the tail extends only to the SW, with no convincing signs of extensions in any other direction (given the sensitivity and resolution of the data). For NSA-19694 there is a main tail that extends to the NE as well as a small nub on the NW side of the galaxy. Although we do not know the 3-dimensional velocity of the galaxy, and therefore cannot know the direction of any ram pressure exerted on it (we return to this point below), this configuration would make sense if ram pressure is forcing gas to the NW and the two tail components come from opposite sides of the galactic disk.

gmw_00012679.name: LS-359104-131
gmw_00012679.overview: LS-359104-131 is a dwarf galaxy candidate for ram pressure stripping [arXiv:2311.02152]. It features a one-sided gas tail extending to the southwest, which may connect to a distant, orphaned gas cloud to form a continuous structure spanning 170 kpc [arXiv:2311.02152].

sources.summary: The paper identifies LS-359104-131 as an RPS candidate with a one-sided gas tail and suggests a distant orphan gas cloud may extend that stripped tail by about 170 kpc.

quotes.quote: In the cases of NSA-19694 and LS-359104-131 (Figure fig:rps_examples , left panels) we see clear \ tails with no apparent accompanying stellar component. For LS-359104-131, the tail extends only to the SW, with no convincing signs of extensions in any other direction (given the sensitivity and resolution of the data).

Returning to LS-359104-131, we see in the wider field shown in Figure fig:rps_example_wide that there is an \ cloud with no apparent optical counterpart approximately 13 \ ( 140 kpc in projection) to the south, on the other side of the host (NGC 7541) and its largest satellite (NSA-637123). Although it first appears that this \ is likely from an ongoing interaction between NGC 7541 and NSA-637123, inspection of their \ morphologies and kinematics indicates that neither of these galaxies are significantly disturbed, and have likely not yet begun strongly interacting, despite their small angular and velocity separations. Thus, it is unlikely that the orphaned gas cloud originated from either of these galaxies and we instead speculate that it is an extension of the RPS tail of LS-359104-131. Indeed, we note that its velocity is only 60 \ offset from LS-359104-131 and in the \ cube there are low significance indications that LS-359104-131 and the southern \ cloud may connect via a spatially and kinematically continuous stream of gas. Deeper \ imaging might reveal this continuous structure or perhaps the stripped gas does not have a high enough density to self-shield and there may be gaps, especially near NGC 7541, where a portion could already have been photoionized. We note that with a full end-to-end length of 15 \ ( 170 kpc, in projection) and an assumed maximum velocity of 275 , if this is one continuous structure then the southern portion must be at least 600 Myr old.

gmw_00012680.name: DES-373393030
gmw_00012680.overview: DES-373393030 is a dwarf galaxy associated with the host galaxy NGC 1421 [arXiv:2311.02152]. It exhibits an offset neutral hydrogen distribution centered to the northwest, which is consistent with ram pressure stripping as the galaxy falls toward its host [arXiv:2311.02152].

sources.summary: The paper says DES-373393030 has an offset, northwest-centered H I distribution consistent with RPS, implying its gas may trail behind as it falls toward NGC 1421.

quotes.quote: NSA-343657 and DES-373393030 (Figure fig:rps_examples , right panels) show a slightly different morphology, but are still consistent with RPS. In these cases the VLA data do not have sufficient resolution to clearly resolve tails, but the \ distributions are offset in one direction from the optical centers of these galaxies. DES-373393030 is almost entirely unresolved, but its \ distribution is clearly centered to the NW. If we assume that it has not yet reached pericenter and is still falling towards its host, NGC 1421 We note here that this galaxy was assigned the incorrect host in SAGA-II. It is just within 300 kpc (projected) of PGC 13646, but is clearly associated with NGC 1421 immediately to the SE. , then this would mean that the bulk of its \ gas is trailing in its wake.

gmw_00012681.name: NSA-343657
gmw_00012681.overview: NSA-343657 is a dwarf galaxy and a strong candidate for ongoing ram pressure stripping [arXiv:2311.02152]. Its neutral hydrogen gas distribution is offset from its optical center, forming a V-like bow shape with most of the gas residing in its wake [arXiv:2311.02152].

sources.summary: The paper treats NSA-343657 as a strong RPS candidate: its H I is offset, forms a V-like bow shape, and most gas appears to reside in its wake.

quotes.quote: NSA-343657 and DES-373393030 (Figure fig:rps_examples , right panels) show a slightly different morphology, but are still consistent with RPS. In these cases the VLA data do not have sufficient resolution to clearly resolve tails, but the \ distributions are offset in one direction from the optical centers of these galaxies. DES-373393030 is almost entirely unresolved, but its \ distribution is clearly centered to the NW. If we assume that it has not yet reached pericenter and is still falling towards its host, NGC 1421 We note here that this galaxy was assigned the incorrect host in SAGA-II. It is just within 300 kpc (projected) of PGC 13646, but is clearly associated with NGC 1421 immediately to the SE. , then this would mean that the bulk of its \ gas is trailing in its wake. Finally, for NSA-343657 the \ distribution forms almost a ``V'' shape, which we suspect is another example of the bow shape typical of RPS, but smeared out by the large and elongated beam shape. However, it should also be noted that there is a slight suggestion of a leading tail, which could make the distribution more of a ``Z'' shape, which would instead suggest a tidal interaction as the cause. The resolution of the current data notwithstanding, the fact that most of the galaxy's gas appears to be residing in its wake (again assuming it is falling towards its host), still means this object is a strong candidate for RPS in progress.

gmw_00012682.name: dw0122+1735
gmw_00012682.overview: dw0122+1735 is a low-surface-brightness dwarf satellite of the galaxy UGC 903 [arXiv:2311.02152]. It is a gas-rich system with a gas fraction of 0.93, and its physical properties qualify it as an ultra-diffuse galaxy [arXiv:2311.02152].

sources.summary: The paper reports dw0122+1735 as a newly detected low-surface-brightness gas-rich satellite of UGC 903 with measured optical structure, H I mass, and ultra-diffuse-galaxy size.

quotes.quote: In the UGC 903 system we detected an additional satellite that was not in the SAGA-II sample, which we will refer to as dw0122+1735 (Figure fig:new_sat ). This is a LSB dwarf approximately 2.7 \ to the SE of UGC 903 that we identified via its \ line emission, which is lightly blended with that of UGC 903, but clearly concentrated on the LSB optical counterpart (Figure fig:new_sat , right).

We used GALFIT Peng+2002,Peng+2010 and DECaLS images to fit the optical properties of this dwarf galaxy. As it is faint and LSB we used a g+ co-add image to fit the structural properties ( R_ eff = 10.4 0.3 , n=0.36 0.03 , a/b=0.66 0.02 ).

For consistency with other objects in Table tab:sats we use the stellar mass estimator from Mao+2021 , which gives M_ / M_ = 7.56 . We separate the \ emission from UGC 903 using SlicerAstro to measure an \ mass of M_ HI / M_ = 7.53 0.09 . These correspond to a gas fraction of 0.93. We take the flux-weighted average velocity of the \ profile to estimate the radial velocity of dw0122+1735 as 2439 \ ( cz = -77 \ relative to UGC 903). Finally, we note that based on the photometry above and the distance estimate to the UGC 903 system (Table tab:hosts ), dw0122+1735 falls well within the standard criteria vanDokkum+2015 to be considered an ultra-diffuse galaxy (UDG), with an effective radius of 1.9 kpc and M_V = -13.9 .

gmw_00012683.name: IC 51
gmw_00012683.overview: IC 51 is a shell elliptical galaxy and a candidate polar-ring galaxy [arXiv:astro-ph/0407511]. It features a perpendicular disk of gas, dust, and stars, where radio-continuum filaments indicate active star formation [arXiv:astro-ph/0407511].

sources.summary: The paper describes IC 51 as a shell elliptical candidate polar-ring galaxy whose perpendicular gas disk and radio-continuum filaments point to current star formation in polar material.

quotes.quote: PRC B-01 (IC 51, Arp 230) This galaxy is a well-known shell elliptical galaxy, and was included in the sample of shell elliptical galaxies mapped in the radio continuum by W87. It is listed as a candidate polar-ring galaxy in the PRC because it is known to have a fast-rotating disk or annulus of gas, dust, and stars perpendicular to the apparent morphological major axis of the stellar body. Observations in the 21 cm line and \ by D. Schiminovich (1996, private communication) show gas components that rotate about the minor axis of the dust lane/polar ring, but also emission well above the plane of the dust lane.

The majority of the radio continuum emission in this galaxy, at both 20 cm and 6 cm, is aligned with the dust lane (the candidate polar ring). However, with our improved sensitivity, we see filaments that extend well above the optical plane of the candidate polar ring; maps made with both uniform and natural weighting, at both wavelengths, are shown in Figure 1. The morphology of this source indicates that current star formation is the most likely source of the radio continuum emission in this galaxy.

gmw_00012684.name: ESO 603-G21
gmw_00012684.overview: ESO 603-G21 is a candidate polar ring galaxy that may instead be a disk galaxy with abnormally heavy dust extinction [arXiv:astro-ph/0407511]. Its radio continuum emission is resolved along a single direction that aligns with the major axis of its central dust lanes [arXiv:astro-ph/0407511].

sources.summary: The paper finds ESO 603-G21's radio continuum aligned with central dust lanes, notes a smaller radio extent than the optical candidate ring, and cites evidence it may be a dusty disk galaxy.

quotes.quote: PRC B-21 (ESO 603-G21) The radio continuum emission in our 20 cm and 6 cm observations is only resolved along one direction, coincident with the major axis of the dust lanes observed in the central portions of the candidate polar ring (PRC; see Figure 2). However, the optical extent of the candidate polar ring is 50 (PRC), while the 20 cm and 6 cm continuum emission we detect has an extent of only 10 15 . Aetal95 find that most of the emission at 2 \ ( K band) comes from a plane close to that of the dust lane. They suggest that this is not a PRG, but a disk galaxy with abnormally heavy dust extinction and a very blue bulge.

gmw_00012685.name: UGC 4261
gmw_00012685.overview: UGC 4261 features 20 cm radio emission characterized by a bright central core surrounded by a smooth, nearly round halo [arXiv:astro-ph/0407511]. The radio emission lacks a typical active galactic nucleus core-jet morphology, though it shows a possible northeast extension along a candidate polar ring [arXiv:astro-ph/0407511].

sources.summary: The paper describes UGC 4261 as having 20 cm emission with a central radio core and round halo, possible extension toward the candidate polar ring, and no AGN-like core-jet morphology.

quotes.quote: PRC C-24 (UGC 4261) The 20 cm emission in this galaxy does not appear to be aligned with a particular plane; rather, there is a bright central core, surrounded by an apparently smooth and nearly round ``halo'' of emission. If the extension to the northeast of the majority of the emission is real, it would be interesting, as it lies approximately along the candidate polar ring in this galaxy (PRC; see Figure 3). Although there may be a bright core of emission in the galaxy center, the radio emission does not exhibit the typical ``core-jet'' morphology of an AGN.

gmw_00012686.name: A 2358-2604
gmw_00012686.overview: A 2358-2604 features two bright optical peaks that may represent distinct galaxies in the process of merging [arXiv:astro-ph/0407511]. Although previously identified as a polar ring candidate, its apparent ring structure is likely tidal debris oriented to resemble a polar ring in projection [arXiv:astro-ph/0407511].

sources.summary: The paper interprets A 2358-2604 as two optical peaks that may be merging galaxies, with an apparent polar ring better explained as projected tidal debris from the interaction.

quotes.quote: PRC C-73 (A 2358-2604) These two point sources correspond to the optical positions of the two brightest peaks at optical wavelengths (see Figure 6), which may be two distinct galaxies in the process of merging. This morphology is consistent with the interpretation in the PRC, that the apparent polar ring feature in this candidate PRG is actually tidal debris oriented so that it resembles a polar ring in projection. The difference between a tidal tail that looks like a polar ring, and an actual polar ring, may simply be a matter of the time at which one observes the interaction. Emission from both faint point sources was included in calculations of total radio flux and the radio spectral index for this object (see Table 2).

gmw_00012687.name: SDSS J1430-0011
gmw_00012687.overview: SDSS J1430-0011 is an active galactic nucleus with a complex X-ray spectrum [arXiv:0906.4974]. This spectrum can be explained by either an ionized or a partially covering absorber, placing the source on the borderline between narrow-line and broad-line Seyfert 1 classifications [arXiv:0906.4974].

sources.summary: The paper finds SDSS J1430-0011 has a complex X-ray spectrum explainable by ionized or partial-covering absorption; its intrinsic power law is normal, but its properties leave it between NLS1 and BLS1 classes.

quotes.quote: We present observations of the AGN SDSS 1430-0011. The low S/N spectrum of this source obtained in a snap shot observation showed an unusually flat continuum. With the follow up observations we find that the source spectrum is complex; it either has an ionized absorber or a partially covering absorber. The underlying power-law is in the normal range observed for AGNs.

Based on its optical and X-ray properties, it is hard to classify SDSS 1430-0011 as either a NLS1 or a BLS1; it is at the border line between the two. There are several AGNs in this ``in-between'' class; SDSS 1430-0011 is not unique.

gmw_00012688.name: AS2UDS0492.0
gmw_00012688.overview: AS2UDS0492.0 is a high-redshift galaxy that displays broad H-alpha emission [arXiv:2301.05720]. The system features a nearby companion galaxy to the northwest [arXiv:2301.05720] and exhibits active galactic nucleus indicators, including X-ray emission and AGN-like infrared colors [arXiv:2301.05720].

sources.summary: The paper describes AS2UDS0492.0 as unique in the sample, with a nearby companion component, broad H emission, AGN-like infrared colours, X-ray emission, and AGN-host indicators.

quotes.quote: The source AS2UDS0492.0 is unique in our sample, since we detect velocity structure from a separate component to the north west within the 2.8 '' field of the KMOS IFU -- this component is detected in the ground-based near-infrared imaging (see Fig. fig:kaoss_vel_fields_1 ) and corresponds to a companion galaxy. This particular source also displays a broad component in the H emission, AGN-like IRAC colours and has an X-ray component.
Like AS2UDS0492.0, this source displays broad H emission, but it also has a very low [ N ii ] /H flux ratio.
Tellingly, only two sources meet all four criteria, these are AS2UDS0492.0 and AS2UDS0287.1.

gmw_00012689.name: AS2UDS0707.0
gmw_00012689.overview: AS2UDS0707.0 is a high-redshift emission line galaxy that exhibits broad H-alpha emission [arXiv:2301.05720]. The galaxy displays a structured velocity dispersion map and a very low [N II]/H-alpha flux ratio [arXiv:2301.05720].

sources.summary: The paper flags AS2UDS0707.0 as difficult to fit despite high H signal, with broad emission, low line ratio, structured dispersion, and an unphysical photometric inclination.

quotes.quote: The fit to the H emission in AS2UDS0707.0 is poor despite the high S/N of the integrated emission (S/N _ H 56). Like AS2UDS0492.0, this source displays broad H emission, but it also has a very low [ N ii ] /H flux ratio.

The velocity dispersion maps in Fig. fig:kaoss_vel_fields_1 generally appear structured, even for the sources with the most significant detections, for example AS2UDS0707.0, AS2COS0025.1 and AS2UDS0338.0. Given the compact sizes of our sources (median R_ e 0.4 '' ) relative to the KMOS PSF (FWHM 0.6 '' ) our observations are susceptible to the effects of beam smearing, leading to increased observed velocity dispersions in the centres of galaxies and flattening of their observed rotation curves e.g. johnson18 .

Additionally, the axis ratio measured from galfit for AS2UDS0707.0 is less than (b/a)_0 (for which we adopted a value of 0.2, see sec:galfit ), resulting in an unphysical inclination angle; we also set a minimum inclination of i = 20 ^ for this source.

gmw_00012690.name: AS2UDS0125.0
gmw_00012690.overview: AS2UDS0125.0 is a high-redshift galaxy located at a redshift of z = 2.156 [arXiv:2301.05720]. It exhibits a disk-like morphology with clumpy, spiral-arm structures and kinematics that are consistent with rotation [arXiv:2301.05720].

sources.summary: The paper describes AS2UDS0125.0 as resolved in H emission and disc-like in JWST imaging, with clumpy spiral-arm structure and kinematics consistent with rotation.

quotes.quote: However, it can be seen in sources such as AS2COS0048.1 and AS2UDS0125.0 that we resolve the H emission on scales large enough to reach the point where the velocity dispersion profile flattens out.

One of the sources in the resolved sample, AS2UDS0125.0 ( z = 2.156), has been imaged with JWST NIRCam (Proposal ID 1837, PI J. Dunlop), which represents a significant improvement on the wavelength coverage and sensitivity of the existing HST imaging. Therefore, in Fig. fig:nircam we show cutouts of this source in NIRCam bands F090W, F115W, F150W, F200W, F277W, F356W, F410M and F444W, alongside a HST colour image (F606W/F814W/F125W). The NIRCam imaging clearly shows clumpy, spiral arm-like structures which are suggested in the HST imaging. AS2UDS0125.0 therefore appears to be disc like from the morphology of the stellar continuum, which is in agreement with the apparent velocity gradient and measured value of v_ rot / _0 = 3.6 0.4 (see sec:chp5_results ).

gmw_00012691.name: GDS0046.0
gmw_00012691.overview: GDS0046.0 is a high-redshift galaxy [arXiv:2301.05720] that exhibits a turbulent and complex morphology [arXiv:2301.05720]. It also displays a complex velocity structure and has a very low derived inclination [arXiv:2301.05720].

sources.summary: The paper treats GDS0046.0 as a turbulent, complex source whose continuum fit was unreliable and whose very low derived inclination required a minimum-inclination assumption.

quotes.quote: Others, such as GDS0001.0, GDS0046.0 and AS2UDS0428.0, display more turbulent, complex velocity structures and morphologies.

We visually inspect all fits and flag sources where we are unable to find a model that reproduces the source structure, or where the best-fit parameters are unphysical (for example, effective radii of 1 pixel), although this is only necessary for three sources (GDS0046.0, AS2UDS0707.0 and AS2UDS0287.1).

This only affects two sources, GDS0046.0 and AS2UDS0292.0, which have galfit derived inclinations of i = 12 ^ 17 ^ and i = 14 ^ 15 ^ respectively. Both are consistent with our chosen minimum inclination (20 ^ ) within their large uncertainties.

gmw_00012692.name: NE3
gmw_00012692.overview: NE3 is a foreground group galaxy that is morphologically classified as an early-type galaxy, likely an S0 lenticular [arXiv:astro-ph/0512163]. It has colors and magnitudes consistent with the red sequence, and its redshift has been derived from sodium and hydrogen absorption lines [arXiv:astro-ph/0512163].

sources.summary: NE3 is a foreground group galaxy observed spectroscopically; the paper gives its redshift measurement, identifies it as the closest observed galaxy to the absorber, and describes it as likely S0.

quotes.quote: We observed galaxy NE3 (see Table tab:spec_red ) in echellette mode with the 0.5 '' slit which provides 30 \ spectral resolution (FWHM). The fainter SE13 and SW3 galaxies were observed in low dispersion mode using a 1 '' slit which affords R 2000 at = 5000 . The exposures were flat fielded and wavelength calibrated with the ESIRedux package (Prochaska et al. 2003 http://www.ucolick.org/ xavier/ESI/index.html ). The NE3 redshift was derived from the centroids of the high-resolution Na 1 and H absorption lines, and the redshift uncertainty is 30 km s ^ 1 .
In the case of this strong H 1 system at , the closest observed galaxy to the sight line around this redshift has = 135 h_ 70 ^ 1 kpc (NE3 in table tab:spec_red ).

The NE3, SE13, and SE5 galaxies, which morphologically appear to be early-type galaxies, have colours and magnitudes consistent with the ``red sequence'' colour-magnitude relation observed in clusters (e.g., Bower, Lucey, Ellis 1992; McIntosh et al. 2005); these galaxies are likely S0s (the other 7 galaxies have blue colours characteristic of late types).

gmw_00012693.name: SE13
gmw_00012693.overview: SE13 is a faint foreground galaxy with a measured spectroscopic redshift [arXiv:astro-ph/0512163]. Morphologically, it is an early-type, red-sequence galaxy that is likely a lenticular S0 system [arXiv:astro-ph/0512163].

sources.summary: SE13 is a fainter foreground galaxy with a measured spectroscopic redshift; the paper groups it with early-type, red-sequence galaxies likely to be S0 systems.

quotes.quote: The fainter SE13 and SW3 galaxies were observed in low dispersion mode using a 1 '' slit which affords R 2000 at = 5000 .
For SE13 and SW3, redshifts were measured by fitting Na 1 , H , and Ca 2 H and K, and the uncertainties are 150 km s ^ 1 .

The NE3, SE13, and SE5 galaxies, which morphologically appear to be early-type galaxies, have colours and magnitudes consistent with the ``red sequence'' colour-magnitude relation observed in clusters (e.g., Bower, Lucey, Ellis 1992; McIntosh et al. 2005); these galaxies are likely S0s (the other 7 galaxies have blue colours characteristic of late types).

gmw_00012694.name: SW3
gmw_00012694.overview: SW3 is a faint foreground galaxy with a measured redshift [arXiv:astro-ph/0512163]. Visual inspection of the galaxy indicates that it possesses spiral structure [arXiv:astro-ph/0512163].

sources.summary: SW3 is a fainter foreground galaxy with a measured redshift; visual inspection places it among group galaxies showing spiral structure.

quotes.quote: The fainter SE13 and SW3 galaxies were observed in low dispersion mode using a 1 '' slit which affords R 2000 at = 5000 .
For SE13 and SW3, redshifts were measured by fitting Na 1 , H , and Ca 2 H and K, and the uncertainties are 150 km s ^ 1 .
We find from visual inspection that 4-5 of the 10 galaxies have indications of spiral structure (SW3, SE1, SE8, SE6, and possibly SE4).

gmw_00012695.name: SE1
gmw_00012695.overview: SE1 is a spiral group galaxy located near a quasar sight line [arXiv:astro-ph/0512163]. The galaxy exhibits a distorted morphology, making it a plausible source of tidally stripped matter [arXiv:astro-ph/0512163].

sources.summary: SE1 is identified as a spiral group galaxy with distorted morphology, making it a plausible source of tidally stripped enriched gas near the quasar sight line.

quotes.quote: We find from visual inspection that 4-5 of the 10 galaxies have indications of spiral structure (SW3, SE1, SE8, SE6, and possibly SE4).
One of the nearby galaxies, SE1, has a distorted spiral morphology. This galaxy is a plausible source of tidally stripped matter.

gmw_00012696.overview: 1418 064 is a flat-spectrum quasar located at a redshift of z = 3.69 [arXiv:1609.03425]. The object features a radio jet that was discovered in VLA 1.4 GHz observations and later detailed using 5 GHz A-array data [arXiv:1609.03425].

sources.summary: The paper includes 1418 064 as a z=3.69 flat-spectrum quasar whose radio jet was discovered in VLA imaging and clarified with new 5 GHz A-array observations.

quotes.quote: Four flat-spectrum radio jets from the z>3.4 radio sample were observed in a \ AO8 program (PI: Cheung) from 2007 Jan - June (ObsIDs: 7871-7874): 1239 + 376 ( z=3.82 ), 1754 + 676 ( z=3.60 ), 1418 064 ( z=3.69 ), 1428 + 422 ( z=4.72 ), in addition to one quasar at z=2.10 (0833 + 585 ; ObsID 7870) which had a known long radio jet mur93 . The jets have minimum projected lengths of 2.5 \ and up to 15 \ for the z=2.1 case (Table 1), so their X-ray counterparts are easily separable from the bright nucleus in the \ images. The exposures of 3.8 - 11 ks were tailored to the radio jet brightness and redshifts.
The radio jets in 1418 064, 1428 + 422, and 1754 + 676 were discovered in VLA 1.4 GHz A-configuration images from Oct - Dec 2004 (program AC755) and discussed in che05 .

The quasar 1418-064 has a hint of a radio extension in the VLA 1.4 GHz observation thus we obtained new A-array data at 5 GHz (July 27-28, 2007; program S8723) of this object which revealed more detail in the radio jet.

gmw_00012697.overview: 1754 + 676 is a flat-spectrum quasar located at a redshift of z=3.60 [arXiv:1609.03425]. It hosts a radio jet discovered with the VLA, and Chandra X-ray observations hint at possible emission between the core and the jet region [arXiv:1609.03425].

sources.summary: The paper lists 1754 + 676 as a z=3.60 flat-spectrum quasar with a VLA-discovered radio jet; its Chandra image hints at emission between core and jet despite no X-ray jet detection.

quotes.quote: Four flat-spectrum radio jets from the z>3.4 radio sample were observed in a \ AO8 program (PI: Cheung) from 2007 Jan - June (ObsIDs: 7871-7874): 1239 + 376 ( z=3.82 ), 1754 + 676 ( z=3.60 ), 1418 064 ( z=3.69 ), 1428 + 422 ( z=4.72 ), in addition to one quasar at z=2.10 (0833 + 585 ; ObsID 7870) which had a known long radio jet mur93 . The jets have minimum projected lengths of 2.5 \ and up to 15 \ for the z=2.1 case (Table 1), so their X-ray counterparts are easily separable from the bright nucleus in the \ images. The exposures of 3.8 - 11 ks were tailored to the radio jet brightness and redshifts.
The radio jets in 1418 064, 1428 + 422, and 1754 + 676 were discovered in VLA 1.4 GHz A-configuration images from Oct - Dec 2004 (program AC755) and discussed in che05 .

For instance, in 1754 + 676 (ObsID 7872), the X-ray jet is not detected, but the image showing deviations from the baseline (see panel () of Figure fig:7872 ) suggests the existence of an emission feature between the quasar and the radio jet region. A longer \ observation is necessary to confirm this emission.

gmw_00012698.name: S4 0444+63
gmw_00012698.overview: S4 0444+63 is a gamma-ray blazar that has been observed in an elevated state of radio emission [arXiv:2001.02875]. The source exhibits a synchrotron peak frequency of 10^12.97 Hz and a Doppler factor exceeding 5.0 [arXiv:2001.02875].

sources.summary: The paper identifies S4 0444+63 as a gamma-ray blazar in an elevated radio state, with evolving spectrum, synchrotron peak near 10^12.97 Hz, and Doppler factor above 5.

quotes.quote: Following the detection of a new gamma-ray source by Fermi LAT in July 2017, we observed S4 0444+63 in order to secure its identification as a gamma-ray blazar. We conducted observations with the Medicina and Noto radio telescopes at 5, 8, and 24 GHz for a total of 12 epochs between 2017 August 1 and 2018 September 22. We carried out the observations with on-the-fly cross scans and reduced the data with our newly developed Cross-scan Analysis Pipeline, which we present here in detail for the first time. We found the source to be in an elevated state of emission at radio wavelength, compared to historical values, which lasted for several months. The maximum luminosity was reached on 2018 May 16 at 24 GHz, with L_ 24 =(1.7 0.3) 10^ 27 \ W Hz ^ 1 ; the spectral index was found to evolve from slightly rising to slightly steep.

Besides the new observations, which have proved to be an effective and efficient tool to secure the identification of the source, additional single dish and very-long-baseline interferometry data provide further insight on the physics of the source. We estimate a synchrotron peak frequency _ peak =10^ 12.97 Hz and a Doppler factor in excess of 5.0 , with both quantities playing a role in the gamma-ray emission from the source.

gmw_00012699.name: WISEA J080816.59+210857.0
gmw_00012699.overview: WISEA J080816.59+210857.0 is an active galactic nucleus located at a redshift of z = 0.142090 [arXiv:2209.08147]. It is classified as a low-luminosity Type 1 active galactic nucleus in which the central engine is viewed directly without obscuration [arXiv:2209.08147].

sources.summary: The paper identifies WISEA J080816.59+210857.0 as the nearest AGN in the surveyed fields, a low-luminosity Type 1 AGN, and includes it in transmitter-constraint parameter space.

quotes.quote: The nearest AGN identified by NED is WISEA J080816.59+210857.0 (also identified as SDSS J080816.59+210857.1) located at a redshift of z = 0.142090 in field BL J080812.8+210612.2. The source is included in the quasars and active nuclei catalogue of Veron2010 . Kyuseok2015 classify the source as a low-luminosity Type 1 AGN in which the central engine is viewed directly without significant obscuration.

Inclusion of our results for the interacting pair NGC 7714/NGC 7715, the 3C402 galaxy group, and the nearby AGN WISEA J080816.59+210857.0 extend the parameter space into a new region of smaller transmission rates and larger EIRP_ min .

gmw_00012700.name: MM184222+593828
gmw_00012700.overview: MM184222+593828 is a high-redshift submillimeter galaxy located at a redshift of z = 3.93 [arXiv:2209.08147]. Gravitational lensing by a foreground lens magnifies the galaxy by a factor of approximately 12 and reveals a complete Einstein ring [arXiv:2209.08147].

sources.summary: The paper discusses MM184222+593828 as a high-redshift lensed submillimeter galaxy with a CO redshift, Einstein ring, and lens magnification considered in SETI sensitivity estimates.

quotes.quote: MM184222+593828 was first identified as a high redshift Sub-mm Galaxy using the MAMBO bolometer array at IRAM Lestrade2010 . Initially, extremely large lensed magnifications ( ) were implied ( 300 ) in order to account for estimated IR luminosity of the system but radio observations by McKean2011 using the Westerbork Synthesis Radio Telescope (WSRT) at 1.4 GHz suggested much more modest values were required. VLA observations of CO Lestrade2011 determined the redshift of the system to be z = 3.93 and revealed a complete Einstein ring with a diameter of 1.4 arcseconds. A revised estimate of the IR luminosity of the system by Lestrade2011 , was consistent with the analysis of McKean2011 , implying a lens magnification of 12 Lestrade2011 .
It's interesting to include the gravitational lens MM184222+593828 in this analysis because of the large source magnification factor ( 12 ) but the EIRP _ min for this system remains extremely large.

gmw_00012701.name: 3FHL J1040.5+0618
gmw_00012701.overview: 3FHL J1040.5+0618 is a bright gamma-ray source associated with a low-synchrotron-peaked BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1901.10806]. The source awakened in gamma-rays in mid-2015, exhibiting variable emission after seven years of no detected emission [arXiv:1901.10806].

sources.summary: The paper investigates 3FHL J1040.5+0618 as a bright gamma-ray source with an SDSS BL Lac counterpart, recent gamma-ray awakening, and variable LAT emission near a neutrino event.

quotes.quote: In the following, we study if the multi-wavelength features of this source indicate a connection to the high-energy neutrino. The source is included in 3FGL as well as 3FHL as 3FHL J1040.5+0618. We note that it is not included in 2FHL, and so it is not a >50 GeV emitter. It is among the brightest 26.1 (47.0 ) 3FGL (3FHL) sources in terms of gamma-ray energy flux for the 4-year (8-year) integration time. The most likely optical counterpart of this object is SDSS J104031.62+061721.7, located 1 arcmin from the 3FHL position, and associated with the low-synchrotron-peaked (LSP) BL Lac object .

As discussed in the next sections, our detailed investigation indicates that this source awakened in gamma-rays in mid-2015. We find no significant emission observed during the first 7 years of LAT monitoring, including the specific times around the detection. During the 9.6 years considered in this work, the brightest persistent gamma-ray emission observed is consistent with the blazar .

gmw_00012702.name: NGC 4451
gmw_00012702.overview: NGC 4451 is a spiral galaxy that exhibits minor residual velocities along its spiral arms [arXiv:2203.00694]. In its outer regions, there is an offset between the carbon monoxide and ionized gas rotation velocities [arXiv:2203.00694].

sources.summary: NGC 4451 has a low-quality prior mass model, offset outer CO and ionized-gas velocities, matching inner rotation-curve slopes, and minor spiral-arm residual velocities.

quotes.quote: NGC 4451 --- This spiral galaxy was also observed in , though its mass model was graded as low-quality relatores19b . There is a significant offset between the CO and \ rotation velocities at large radii and the \ rotation velocity is slightly lower than that of the CO overall, which may be due to irregular ionized gas kinematics in the outer regions of this galaxy. In the inner regions, the slopes of the rotation curves agree well. We use a slightly larger inclination compared to relatores19a (49 \ compared to their 45.1 ), but this does not completely explain the small offset between the CO and \ rotation curves at radii less than 1.5 kpc.

In NGC 4451, the residual velocities are positive along the spiral arms. The magnitudes of these residual velocities are small, so they likely have only a minor effect on the robustness of the derived rotation curve.

gmw_00012703.name: NGC 5692
gmw_00012703.overview: NGC 5692 is a spiral galaxy that shows no evidence of a central bar [arXiv:2203.00694]. Its stellar components have been measured using Pan-STARRS1 band data, and its carbon monoxide and optical rotation curves show excellent agreement [arXiv:2203.00694].

sources.summary: NGC 5692 is a non-barred spiral whose CO and comparison rotation curves agree well; the analysis uses nearly the same inclination as prior work and PS1-band stellar data.

quotes.quote: NGC 5692 --- This spiral galaxy has no evidence for a bar. The agreement between the rotation curves derived from the CO and \ is better than 3 \ on average. We use nearly the same inclination as relatores19a (53 \ compared to their 52.4 ). There is no \ IRAC data available for this galaxy, so band data are used to measure the stellar components instead.

gmw_00012704.name: NGC 6106
gmw_00012704.overview: NGC 6106 is a spiral galaxy characterized by strong spiral arms and no reported bar [arXiv:2203.00694]. Its carbon monoxide detections are concentrated within these spiral arms, and it exhibits excellent agreement between its carbon monoxide and hydrogen rotation curves [arXiv:2203.00694].

sources.summary: NGC 6106 is the sample's largest CO galaxy, with strong spiral-arm CO detections, no reported bar, excellent CO-comparison rotation agreement, and inner behavior affecting dark-matter fits.

quotes.quote: NGC 6106 --- This spiral galaxy has strong spiral arms, and the CO detections are concentrated in the spiral arms. There is no reported bar, though the spiral arms in the centre may contribute to the non-zero radial velocity component seen in the CO rotation curve fits. This is the largest galaxy in the sample, in terms of angular and physical CO extent. We use a slightly higher inclination of 59 \ compared to relatores19a who found 53.4 . The agreement between the CO and \ rotation curves is excellent, though there appears to be a slight increase in the CO velocity at very small radii that is not seen in the , though this may be explained by the finer radial sampling of the CO data.

This is most evident for NGC 6106, which has the largest R_ max in the sample. The power-law fit continues to rise, producing a steeper dark matter slope in the centre, resulting in the slightly larger values of \ compared to the gNFW, all else being equal.

gmw_00012705.name: SDSS J0037-0942
gmw_00012705.overview: SDSS J0037-0942 is a foreground galaxy situated at a redshift of 0.195 [arXiv:1512.00462]. It features a doubly lensed image configuration and lies in front of the cluster ACO 85 [arXiv:1512.00462].

sources.summary: SDSS J0037-0942 is a doubly imaged lens foreground to ACO 85, with several line-of-sight galaxies considered when assessing environmental effects on lens modelling.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0037-0942 is a galaxy with a doubly lensed image configuration. With a redshift of 0.195 it is situated in the foreground of the cluster ACO 85 at z=0.55 with 10 Galaxies along the line of sight within a 1 Mpc projected distance SDG98 . auger08 finds five galaxies along the line of sight, within a 30 ^ distance to the lens. The brightest of these, a late-type galaxy, can be seen in a WFPC2 I-band image. However, the SIMBAD database does not list this galaxy as a member of the cluster.

gmw_00012706.name: SDSS J0044+0113
gmw_00012706.overview: SDSS J0044+0113 is a strong gravitational lensing system that features a distinct lensed arc and a central image [arXiv:1512.00462]. The nearby cluster-environment galaxies do not significantly affect the model fit of this system [arXiv:1512.00462].

sources.summary: SDSS J0044+0113 has one arc and a central image; GALFIT modelling leaves low residuals, and nearby cluster-environment galaxies do not affect the model fit.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0044+0113 features one distinct arc and one image close to the centre. The arc has been modelled using the new features of Galfit, resulting in residuals below 4 in both bands within the region of interest. The small uncertainty stays below the RMS of the background, measured in the outer regions of the images. The image close to the centre of the lens can be seen in the residual of the V-band photometry as a 10 spike, but it does not show up in the H-band, as it is either too faint to be seen in infrared or the surface brightness fit incorporates it. However, masking out this region does not change the fit significantly.

gmw_00012707.name: SDSS J0955+0101
gmw_00012707.overview: SDSS J0955+0101 is a structured, late-type spiral galaxy with a disk and a bulge [arXiv:1512.00462]. Seen almost edge-on, the galaxy acts as a strong gravitational lens with an extended arc located to its southwest [arXiv:1512.00462].

sources.summary: SDSS J0955+0101 is an almost edge-on late-type spiral lens with a disk and bulge; the arc is modelled or masked, and residuals remain low.

quotes.quote: SDSS J0955+0101 is a structured late-type spiral with a disk and bulge seen almost edge-on. One extended arc with three brighter knobs is located 1.3 ^ South-West of the light-centroid of the lens. This arc is masked out in the modelling of the I-band image, but fitted with GALFIT in the H-band frame. In both bands the lens galaxy is modelled as a composite of S ersic along witg an edge-on disk. The residual map shows that the S ersic-plus-disk model describes the galaxy well in the the region within 1 ^ , as the median residual profile stays below 10 in both bands.

gmw_00012708.name: SDSSJ0959+0410
gmw_00012708.overview: SDSSJ0959+0410 is a gravitational lens system that doubly lenses a background galaxy [arXiv:1512.00462]. Its optical surroundings feature several clumps and plumes, while near-infrared observations reveal ringlike structures that suggest a recent tidal interaction or merger [arXiv:1512.00462].

sources.summary: SDSSJ0959+0410 is a doubly lensed system with structured optical surroundings, masked clumps and plumes, and near-infrared ringlike features suggestive of recent interaction or merger.

quotes.quote: SDSSJ0959+0410 is a lens with a doubly lensed background galaxy. In the visual band its surrounding is highly structured with several clumps and plumes, apparent in the residual maps. As we are not able to model them one by one photometrically, we mask them out. In the H-band this was not necessary, although there are ringlike features suggesting a recent tidal interaction or merger. However, there does not seem to be any nearby galaxy at the same redshift.

gmw_00012709.name: SDSS J1204+0358
gmw_00012709.overview: SDSS J1204+0358 is a gravitational lens that produces a system of two arcs [arXiv:1512.00462]. The lens is located near a compact galaxy group and may be tidally interacting with a nearby, warped edge-on late-type galaxy [arXiv:1512.00462].

sources.summary: SDSS J1204+0358 produces two arcs and lies near a compact group or cluster environment; a warped late-type neighbor may be tidally interacting with the lens.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1204+0358 produces a system of two arcs, whose brightest pixels are used as input to the free-form lens model. There are many objects along the line of sight, probably part of the nearby (45 arcsec) distant compact galaxy group, which in turn may be associated with the galaxy cluster ACO 1463, whose centre lies only 109 arcsec South of the lens. There is one late-type galaxy (5 ^ ) appearing almost edge-on. Judging by its warped disked, it might be in tidal interaction with the lens. There is a fair amount of small sources in the vicinity of the lens, but the photometric models are hardly influenced by them.

gmw_00012710.name: SDSS J1213+6708
gmw_00012710.overview: SDSS J1213+6708 is a doubly imaged background galaxy that is strongly gravitationally lensed [arXiv:1512.00462]. The system forms a single arc alongside a central image, and its modeling is complicated by an isophotal twist in the V-band [arXiv:1512.00462].

sources.summary: SDSS J1213+6708 is a doubly imaged lens with an arc and central image; the authors mask faint arcs and report modest residuals in both bands.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1213+6708 is another doubly imaged background galaxy, which forms one arc and another image close to the centre. The V-band modelling is complicated by an isophotal twist, leading to larger residuals at the centre. As this does not occur in the H-band image for a S ersic profile with comparable parameters, we tend to discard other model fits that give a better agreement in the centre, but worse results in the outskirts of the lens. The stellar mass estimates from the H- and V-band are thus very similar. As the arcs are faint but clearly visible in the residual map of the H-band model, we mask them out.

gmw_00012711.name: SDSS J1402+6321
gmw_00012711.overview: SDSS J1402+6321 is a lens galaxy surrounded by two extended arcs that nearly form an Einstein ring [arXiv:1512.00462]. These arcs are visible in the I-band but are invisible in the H-band [arXiv:1512.00462].

sources.summary: SDSS J1402+6321 has two extended arcs that nearly form an Einstein ring; simple Sersic fits work well, and arc knobs drive the lens model.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1402+6321 exhibits two extended arcs which form almost an Einstein ring around the lens galaxy. In H-band they are invisible. In I-band we mask them out. S ersic fits yield good results, below 5 in both bands. auger08 finds six objects within 30 arcsec along the line of sight.

gmw_00012712.name: SDSS J1525+3327
gmw_00012712.overview: SDSS J1525+3327 is a gravitational lens galaxy with at least five objects located within a projected distance of 35 arcseconds [arXiv:1512.00462]. Its closest projected neighbor lies 2.8 arcseconds to the northwest and is possibly interacting with the lens [arXiv:1512.00462].

sources.summary: SDSS J1525+3327 has well-modelled H- and V-band photometry, nearby projected objects including a possible interacting companion, and a lower-redshift cluster projection.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1525+3327 The H-band photometry is modelled with residuals below 10 and the V-band residuals stay below 5 , except for one spike around the radius of the arc, which was largely masked out. There are at least five objects within a projected 35 arcsec distance to the lens, and additional light extending towards two of them. The closest object in projection, quite possibly interacting with the lens galaxy, is just 2.8 ^ North-West of the lens. We mask it out and model the arc and one counter image close to the centre as it was done in newton11 . Furthermore, this lens appears, in projection, close to a cluster, with its BCG about 45 arcsec South-West of the lens.

gmw_00012713.name: SDSS J1531-0105
gmw_00012713.overview: SDSS J1531-0105 is a gravitational lens system that forms nearly an Einstein ring with three extended features [arXiv:1512.00462]. The lens galaxy is located 320 kpc from a same-redshift compact galaxy group, placing it close enough to be affected by the group's gravitational pull [arXiv:1512.00462].

sources.summary: SDSS J1531-0105 is nearly an Einstein ring with multiple nearby sources masked; it also lies close enough to a same-redshift compact group to feel its gravity.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1531-0105 is almost an Einstein ring with three extended features, two bright ones and one faint but more extended arc indicating a fold caustic configuration. Judging by the present photometry there are at least three stars and more than six galaxies within a radius of 30 ^ along with additional faint sources, one of which could even be another arc in the lens configuration. All these were masked to get a good photometric model. However, we model the lens as a three image configuration as there are no distinct maxima in the light distribution of the faint arc.

It should be noted that there is a compact galaxy group 2 ^ South of the lens, confirmed at the same redshift as the lens galaxy MPES09 . The physical distance between the lens galaxy and the group is 320 kpc, i.e. close enough to be affected by its gravitational pull.

gmw_00012714.name: SDSSJ1630+4520
gmw_00012714.overview: SDSSJ1630+4520 is a gravitational lens system that features two extended arcs [arXiv:1512.00462]. The outer arc almost completes an Einstein ring, while the inner arc also contains two brighter regions used for modeling the lens [arXiv:1512.00462].

sources.summary: SDSSJ1630+4520 has two extended arcs, including an outer near-Einstein ring; despite noisy H-band data, the I-band fit and stellar masses are reliable.

quotes.quote: SDSSJ1630+4520 is a lens with two extended arcs, an outer one that almost completes an Einstein ring and an inner one. Both have two brighter regions which we use to model the lens. auger08 finds four objects within 30 arcsec along the line of sight. In the H-band the data are noisy. The I-band, however, allows for a very good fit, with a flat residual profile below 4 within the Einstein radius.

gmw_00012715.name: SDSS J1719+2939
gmw_00012715.overview: SDSS J1719+2939 is a galaxy that exhibits a quadruply lensed image configuration [arXiv:1512.00462]. It has very few faint objects within a 30-arcsecond radius, though the closest neighbor shows distorted isophotes suggesting a tidal interaction [arXiv:1512.00462].

sources.summary: SDSS J1719+2939 is a quad lens with few nearby objects; the closest one has distorted isophotes suggesting tidal interaction and is masked in modelling.

quotes.quote: SDSS J1719+2939 exhibits a quadruply lensed image configuration. The brightest pixels serve as input to PixeLens . There are very few faint objects within a 30 ^ radius from the lens. The closest one ( 3.8 ^ to the South-East) has distorted isophotes in the V-band, suggesting a tidal interaction. It is consequently masked out.

gmw_00012716.name: SDSS J2303+1422
gmw_00012716.overview: SDSS J2303+1422 is a gravitational lens system that displays two arcs, each containing two distinct bright peaks [arXiv:1512.00462]. A faint, elongated object located 4.4 arcseconds north of the lens may be in tidal interaction with it [arXiv:1512.00462].

sources.summary: SDSS J2303+1422 shows two arcs with bright peaks, a possible tidally interacting elongated neighbor, and photometric fits with residuals below background RMS.

quotes.quote: SDSS J2303+1422 shows two arcs with two distinct bright peaks each. auger08 finds six objects within 30 ^ of the lens. There is a faint elongated object 4.4 arcsec North of the lens which could be in tidal interaction with the lens. The arcs have been modelled in the V-band but masked out in the H-band.
